sentence: The company Our purpose: To explore and discover the infinite possibilities of energy so that everyone can move forward.

Our values help us to make our purpose a reality – “Courage: we question with meaning and ambition”, “Curiosity: we are open to learning”, “Achievement: we make things happen” and “Support: we unlock people’s potential”.
Further information can be found at www.repsol.com.

The Management Report Repsol1, as a further show of its commitment to transparency, has drawn up this consolidated Management Report, which integrates both financial and sustainability information, serving as the cornerstone of the Group's annual public reporting.

This Management Report faithfully represents the Repsol Group's business, results, and financial position, and provides a description of the main risks and uncertainties it faces, along with its strategic lines of action and their foreseeable trend moving forward.
It also provides information on sustainability, including Environmental, Social and Governance (ESG) criteria.

The report not only complies with the applicable2 legal requirements but is also aligned with best practices and, in particular, with the recommendations of the Conceptual Framework for the Preparation of Integrated Reports of the International Integrated Reporting Council (IIRC) and the Guide for the Preparation of the Management Report of Listed Entities of the CNMV, Spain’s securities market regulator.

This report should be read in conjunction with the 2025 consolidated Financial Statements, which together with this Report have been filed with the CNMV (www.cnmv.es) and are also available at www.repsol.com.

Report information The financial information included in this document, unless expressly stated otherwise, has been prepared in accordance with Repsol’s segment information reporting model (revised in 2025), which is described in Appendix II and in the Note 4 “Segment information” of the 2025 consolidated Financial Statements.
This reporting model uses Alternative Performance Measures (AMS) in accordance with the Guidelines of the European Securities Markets Authority (ESMA),3 i.e.
"adjusted" magnitudes with respect to those presented in accordance with EU-IFRS.
They are updated quarterly on the Repsol website(www.repsol.com).

The information on sustainability is presented in accordance with the requirements of the Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) and the European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS), as adopted through Commission Delegated Act of July 31, 2023.

It also includes the information required by Spanish Law 11/2018, as well as the disclosures on environmentally sustainable activities according to the Sustainable Finance Taxonomy, and addresses the requirements of Royal Decree 214/2025 of March 18, which establishes the obligation to calculate the carbon footprint and to prepare and publish greenhouse gas emission reduction plans.
This report also includes voluntary indicators in accordance with the Guidelines of the Global Reporting Initiative (GRI).
Together, this information makes up the Statement Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information included in Appendix IV.

In the realm of corporate governance, Repsol publishes an Annual Corporate Governance Report (Appendix V) and an Annual Report on Directors’ Remuneration (Appendix VI), drawn up in accordance with Articles 540 and 541 of the Spanish Companies Act, following the instructions of the CNMV4.

The forward-looking information contained in the different sections of this document reflects the plans, objectives, commitments, aspirations, forecasts or estimates of the Group’s directors at the date of their authorization for issue.

Such forward-looking information is based on assumptions that are considered reasonable as of the date of their preparation, and cannot be interpreted as a guarantee of the entity’s future performance, in the sense that such plans, forecasts or estimates are subject to risks and uncertainties, meaning that the Group’s future performance may not necessarily coincide with what was initially envisaged or planned.

1 Hereinafter, the names "Repsol", "Repsol Group" or "the Company" will be used, interchangeably, to refer to the group of companies formed by Repsol, S.A. and its subsidiaries, associates and joint agreements.

2 Among others, the Spanish Commercial Code, the Consolidated Text of the Spanish Companies Act and Law 11/2018 of 28 December, which amends the Commercial Code, the Consolidated Text of the Companies Act and the Auditing Act as regards non-financial information and diversity, and transposes into Spanish law Directive 2014/95/EU as regards disclosure of non-financial and diversity information by certain large undertakings and groups.

3 Annex II "Alternative Performance Measures" includes the reconciliation between the adjusted figures and those corresponding to the IFRS-EU financial reporting 4 As set out in CNMV Circular 3/2021 of 28 September, which amends the templates for the annual corporate governance and directors' remuneration reports of listed companies.
The Company also follows the recommendations of the Good Governance Code for Listed Companies, as last revised by the CNMV on 26 June 2020.

Message from the Chairman The EU has a real opportunity to regain relevance if it embraces reindustrialization and turns the energy transition into a driver of growth Dear Shareholders, The world is going through a period of profound transformation, marked by major geopolitical shifts, trade tensions, and growing global competition for leadership in energy, industry, and technology.

The current scenario is uncertain, and the European Union faces a historic challenge: redefining its role and moving toward a more resilient model that will allow it to successfully address these global challenges.
For decades, growth across the globe was underpinned by trade openness and multilateral cooperation.
Today, those foundations are being challenged by an unrelenting drive to secure a more strategic position in the value chains of the future.

In this new context, access to reliable and competitively priced energy, critical raw materials, and homegrown industrial and technological capabilities is essential to ensure that Europe continues to have a voice in the world and is heard.
Europe facing the challenge of rebuilding its industrial base The European Union approaches this moment from a challenging position.
Its industrial fabric has steadily weakened over recent decades, and a significant technological gap persists compared with the United States and China.
This is compounded by structurally higher energy costs, which undermine the competitiveness of European companies and accelerate offshoring.
This context marks a decisive turning point.
The EU has a real opportunity to regain relevance if it embraces reindustrialization and turns the energy transition into a driver of growth — rather than a drag — on its economy.
Achieving this goal will also require the EU institutions to focus on promoting investment rather than generating additional regulation.
Recognizing the strategic nature of the refining sector in terms of fuel supply security, industrial development, and strategic autonomy will be critical for the continent’s future.

Refineries supply 97% of the energy used in the European transport sector and provide approximately 50% of the essential raw materials for the chemical industry, supporting critical value chains ranging from automotive manufacturing to aviation and defense.

However, since 2009, one in four European refineries has closed or been converted, reducing total refining capacity by nearly 20%, creating growing vulnerabilities in Europe’s security of supply, and undermining the region’s industrial competitiveness.
This erosion has turned the European Union into a net importer of diesel and jet fuel, increasing its dependence on third countries in an increasingly unstable geopolitical context.

This situation contrasts with the more pragmatic energy policies of the United States, which has reduced greenhouse gas emissions as much as, if not more than, the European Union, while pursuing all technologies that can incrementally improve sustainability and meet the world’s growing energy demand.

As a result of these policies, the United States has achieved significantly lower energy costs than Europe and has become a major exporter of natural gas and crude oil, helping Europe reduce its dependence on Russia.

The role of hydrocarbons in ensuring supply Consolidating a strong industrial refining base in transition is essential if we are to safeguard Europe’s strategic autonomy and ensure highly skilled industrial jobs across the continent.

In this transformation process, it is crucial to acknowledge the role that hydrocarbons will continue to play over the coming decades in guaranteeing supply and ensuring the functioning of society, industry, and transport.
At Repsol, we clearly understand the strategic role of oil and natural gas in the global economy.
That is why we have been investing for years in efficient exploration and production of these energy resources.

Our activity focuses on developing new projects in key geographic areas where we have competitive advantages, including Spain, Portugal, the United States, Brazil, the United Kingdom, and Peru, among others.

Business leadership to secure Europe’s future A robust industrial base is the foundation for fostering sustainable economic growth capable of withstanding different economic cycles and contributing to societal progress.
The European Union must create space for genuinely robust, innovative, and forward-looking companies to continue investing within its borders.
The roadmap we defined at Repsol years ago is moving in this direction.

We are busy transforming our industrial complexes into multi-energy hubs capable of processing a wide range of feedstocks and producing low-carbon solutions that are essential for a more strategically autonomous Europe.
At our Cartagena industrial complex, we are already operating a plant dedicated to the large-scale production of 100% renewable fuels.
A second facility will follow in Puertollano in 2026.
Moreover, in 2025 we took the decision to invest in the Ecoplanta in Tarragona, a ground-breaking project in Europe that will convert municipal solid waste into renewable methanol for transport.
These initiatives not only strengthen our industrial position; they also place Spain and Europe in a strategic position to lead the energy transition and become more competitive.
Industry needs far more decisive support from the European Union to make meaningful progress in projects of this kind under truly competitive conditions.
This requires regulatory certainty and stable, predictable frameworks that allow companies to plan long-term investments and facilitate access to the necessary financing.
Clear and coherent investment signals are also needed to align climate objectives with industrial realities and to reduce the current administrative complexity that delays project execution.

The European Union must move toward a common energy policy that enables it to compete with the United States, along the lines highlighted by former European Central Bank President Mario Draghi in his report on the competitiveness of the European economy.
This means strengthening interconnections, investing in strategic infrastructure, coordinating energy and industrial policies, and not losing sight of the importance of defense.
Only a joint strategy involving both institutions and companies will allow Europe to maintain its stability and global influence in the decades ahead.

All of us in Europe need to be more ambitious and aspire to build a more cohesive and pragmatic European Union — one that is capable of responding more quickly and decisively to the challenges posed by the new world order.
The challenge is immense, but so is the opportunity.
Europe can shake off the risk of decline to achieve a genuine industrial revitalization if it chooses to act resolutely, ambitiously, and with a long-term vision.
Repsol will continue working to help make this happen.

Message from the CEO We are convinced that our strategy and path to becoming a truly multi-energy company will enable us to successfully navigate all the changes going on around us Dear Shareholders, The global environment in which companies such as Repsol operate has become increasingly uncertain lately.

Over the past year, the geopolitical world order has shifted abruptly, and the priority for most countries is now to secure reliable access to the energy and raw materials needed to fuel their economies.
While the rapid transformation of our industry creates uncertainty around market trends, it also presents business opportunities for the companies that adapt most quickly to the new rules.
In this new phase, the ability to anticipate change will be essential to ensuring long-term business viability.
To grasp these emerging opportunities, energy companies must build sufficient flexibility into their operations to respond swiftly to increasingly frequent changes.

The sector must also focus on further diversifying supply chains and reducing critical dependencies, with the aim of ensuring clients have a reliable supply of all forms of energy.. At Repsol, we are ready to meet the challenges of the new global economic order.

In recent years, our strategy has focused on concentrating our operations in countries where we hold competitive advantages, such as Spain, Portugal, the United States, Brazil, and the United Kingdom.
This approach reduces our exposure to geopolitical volatility and guarantees access to the resources we need.
We also happen to operate one of the most efficient and competitive refining systems in the European Union, comprising five industrial complexes in Spain.
These facilities ensure the production of fuels used by most transport—particularly road transport—and are fully capable of adapting output to market developments and even to potential energy crises.
Another of our strengths is our business model built on diversification.

For instance, in a matter of years we have become a significant player in low-carbon power generation, primarily in Spain, with a portfolio that includes wind and solar assets, as well as hydroelectric and combined cycle plants.

These facilities are strategically important in providing added stability to the Spanish energy system The diversification of our activities has enabled us to evolve into a multi-energy company capable of producing and providing customers with the full range of energy they need, whether for mobility, homes, or businesses.
We are convinced that this new corporate profile positions us to capitalize on the opportunities emerging from this new phase of the global economy.

Significant progress across all businesses In such a demanding environment, our company achieved significant progress in all business areas during the past year, reaching milestones that have strengthened our competitiveness and consolidated our position as a leading multi-energy company.
At Repsol, we remain committed to responsible exploration and production of oil and gas, two energy sources that will continue to be necessary for society over the coming decades.
Our strategy is underpinned by active portfolio management, prioritizing operations in key markets where we have top-tier assets and future growth projects.
In 2025, average roduction stood at 548,000 barrels of oil equivalent per day, in line with the strategic plan.
The United States is a key country for our Upstream business, where we have invested $21 billion over the past 15 years.
In 2025, we began oil production at Leon-Castile, offshore Louisiana, and in the first half of 2026 we will start production at Pikka in Alaska.
These two projects will allow us to increase energy output in a country that is critical to the company’s growth moving forward.

In 2025, we strengthened Repsol’s position in the United Kingdom by integrating our North Sea assets with NEO Energy Group and subsequently reaching an agreement to merge them with those of TotalEnergies.
The resulting joint company will be called NEO NEXT+ and will become the largest oil and gas producer in the region, with expected production of 250,000 barrels of oil equivalent per day this year.

The international context has not slowed the transformation of our industrial complexes, which we are converting into multi-energy hubs capable of processing different types of feedstock to manufacture low-carbon products, such as 100% renewable fuels.
These fuels can be used without modifying internal combustion engines and will be key to reducing CO2 emissions from transport.

Repsol currently leads the production of 100% renewable diesel in Spain, thanks to the plant inaugurated in Cartagena in 2024, which will be joined this year by a second plant in Puertollano with a capacity to produce 200,000 tons annually.

Moreover, our Tarragona complex achieved a technological milestone by producing 100% renewable gasoline at industrial scale for the first time, a clear example of the company’s innovation capabilities.

We have also approved ground-breaking projects that confirm our commitment to industrial development, such as the construction of the Tarragona Ecoplant, the first facility of its kind in Europe to convert urban waste into renewable methanol.

We have further announced the construction of our first two large scale electrolyzers in Cartagena and at Petronor, which will have a capacity of 100 MW each and be able to produce 15,000 tons of renewable hydrogen every year for use as feedstock at both industrial centers.
In the Customer business, we continue to expand our multi-energy offering and strengthen key business attributes, such as improving customer experience and product quality.

A key highlight in the period was the rollout of Nexa Diesel of 100% renewable origin—our premium-grade diesel—which is now available at more than 1,500 service stations across Spain and Portugal, making ours one of the largest 100% renewable fuel distribution networks in Europe.
In 2025, our electricity and gas retail business continued to grow, reaching three million customers.
This allowed us to increase the volumes sold to 8,568 GWh of electricity and 2,239 GWh of gas.
We now also have 10.8 million digital customers, most of them using Waylet, our loyalty and payment app, which is helping to drive the company’s digital strategy.
In renewable energy, our business model is based on developing projects from their earliest stages and subsequently bringing in strategic partners to secure higher returns.

In 2025, two examples of this asset rotation strategy were the entry of Schroders Greencoat into a 400 MW portfolio of wind and solar assets in Spain, and Stonepeak into several solar parks in the United States.

Our renewable generation capacity increased by more than 2,000 MW, reaching a total of 5,813 MW, mainly due to the commissioning of two large-scale solar facilities in the United States: Outpost and, partially, Pinnington, which is set to become our largest photovoltaic plant.
The start of commercial operations at these projects, along with others in Spain, was key to increasing our wind and solar output by 34%.
Technology and digitalization play a central role in the management of our businesses and have become cross-cutting pillars of Repsol’s strategy.
In recent years, we have deployed artificial intelligence across all areas, from optimizing well drilling times to personalizing commercial offers and managing legal matters.

We are transforming our businesses on the back of technology and digitalization, but above all through the capability, dedication, and talent of the more than 24,000 professionals who work at this company— a diverse and multicultural team whose commitment is enabling us to deliver on our projects and make progress toward the objectives set out in our 2024–2027 strategic plan.
Improved shareholder remuneration In 2025, Repsol once again increased its shareholder remuneration, one of the most attractive in the market.
During the year, dividends totaling €0.975 per share were paid out, representing an 8.3% increase over the previous year.
Moreover, share capital was reduced through the cancellation of 52 million treasury shares, improving shareholder returns by increasing earnings per share.
Last year we achieved results that allow us to continue pursuing our strategy and maintaining our remuneration commitments.
Net income amounted to €1.899 billion, up 8% on the previous year, despite operating in a context marked by geopolitical uncertainty, market volatility, and declining oil prices.
All of these figures confirm the soundness of the strategy we have designed for the 2024–2027 period.
In March, we will present an update of our projections, enabling us to adapt to developments in the macroeconomic, regulatory, and business environment of the energy sector.

This update will include minor adjustments to business strategy guidelines, without altering the core principles of our plan: competitive shareholder remuneration, financial strength, and disciplined investment.

At Repsol, we look to the future with resolve, convinced that our strategy and our path to becoming a truly multi-energy company will enable us to successfully navigate all the changes going on around us.
In 2026, we will continue to strive to become more competitive and, above all, to capitalize on the opportunities in the energy sector.

2025 overview The economic environment during 2025 was marked by persistent market volatility, geopolitical uncertainty and concerns about the oversupply of oil.

The energy markets witnessed a decline in crude oil prices (Brent averaged $69/bbl, down 14% versus the 2024 price) and higher gas prices (the average Henry Hub price was up 51% compared with 2024 to reach $3.4/MBtu) and electricity prices in Spain (up 4% to €66/ MWh).
For more information, see section 3.

Results and financial position5 Despite the prevailing volatility and uncertainty within the energy markets, Repsol’s 2025 results improved on those of the previous year by 8%, which included negative impacts arising from value adjustments at Upstream assets and the Temporary Energy Levy in Spain.

Results for the period € millions Upstream Industrial Customer LCG Corporate and other Adjusted income Inventory effect Special items Net income 2025 957 963 754 53 (159) 2,568 (633) (36) 2024 1,028 1,447 644 (24) (70) 3,025 (417) (852) 1,899 1,756 Δ (7%) (33%) 17% — (128%) (15%) (52%) 96% 8% Adjusted income, which measures the ordinary management of the businesses, amounted to €2,568 million, down 15% on 2024.
At Upstream, earnings (€957 million) were down 7% as a result of falling crude oil prices, divestments and earnings at investees (Brazil, etc.
), mitigated by the rise in gas realization prices and lower depreciation.
At Industrial, earnings totaled €963 million, 33% down on the previous year, impacted by the poorer performance of Refining in Spain, Chemicals and the crude oil and gas trading businesses.

At Customer (€754 million), earnings were up 17%, driven by Mobility (higher sales of gasoline and diesel, impacted in 2024 by fraud in the Spanish market), Aviation (higher margins and volumes) and LPG (higher margins from bottled gas).
Improved net income and shareholder return amid high uncertainty and volatility in the energy markets • Results impacted by the worse performance of industrial businesses.
• Earnings at Customer continue to improve, thanks to its multi-energy offer.
• Financial strength and high liquidity.
• Increasing shareholder return.

Last but not least, Low Carbon Generation (LCG) saw an improvement in net income (€53 million) on the back of higher captured prices and increased production (due to the contribution of both new renewable projects and combined cycles).
The inventory effect was very unfavorable (-633 million), as was the case in 2024, due to the decline in the prices of crude oil and products in the period.

Special items (-36 million) primarily include provisions for credit risk in Venezuela and expenses recognized in connection with the agreement to end the dispute with Hecate Holdings LLC, along with reversal of impairment at Upstream and provisions for other risks and expenses.
In short, net income, which reflects the profit to which the shareholders of Repsol, S.A. are entitled, stood at €1,899 million, up 8% on 2024.
This figure includes a tax expense of €1,983 million (effective tax rate of 50%), with a total of €13,265 million in taxes paid in 2025, of which €9,640 in Spain.

Operating cash flow amounted to €5,365 million in 2025, up €400 million on the 2024 figure, at year in which €986 million was paid under the agreement to settle the arbitration proceedings with Sinopec in the UK (Upstream).

Significant investments (albeit 33% lower than in the previous year) and divestments, mainly in non-strategic Upstream assets, contributed to free cash flow of €2,208 million in 2025 (-391 million in 2024).
After taking into account net collections from non-controlling interests and other items, cash generated by the businesses amounted to €2,939 million (€-188 million in 2024).
Net debt stood at €4,487 million, up €472 million on 2024, resulting in a leverage ratio of 14.0% (€1,616 million excluding leases; leverage ratio of 5.5%).
The company’s high liquidity (€10,271 million) and robust financial position were recognized by the rating agencies, which maintained Repsol's investment grade status.
The shareholder return in 2025 totaled €0.975 gross per share, materialized through the payment of two cash dividends, up 8.3% on 2024.
Moreover, share capital was reduced through the retirement of 52 million treasury shares.
5 In the fourth quarter of 2025, the Group revised the financial and performance measures of its business segments that are reviewed internally for decision-making purposes.

Specifically, the results and financial metrics of joint ventures are now integrated using the equity method in the segment financial performance presentation model, and the segment profit measure has been revised to reflect profit attributable to the parent company (adjusted net income).
In addition, a new metric has been defined to monitor segment cash performance (cash generated by the businesses).
The comparative segment information reported has been restated to allow for appropriate comparability between periods.
For further information, see Note 4, “Business segments”, to the consolidated financial statements.

Pursuing transformation and a profitable energy transition • The industrial transformation continues with the decision to invest in the Ecoplanta in Tarragona and in the first large-scale electrolyzers in Cartagena and Bilbao, together with the progress made in constructing the 100% renewable fuel production plant in Puertollano.
• New partners at Upstream (new joint venture in the United Kingdom) and renewables (asset rotations in Spain and the United States).

• Development of the multi-energy offer (3.0 million electricity and gas customers and more than 1,500 service stations supplying fuels of 100% renewable origin across the Iberian Peninsula) supported by digitalization (more than 10 million digital customers, mostly through the Waylet app).
• Commissioning of new LCG wind and solar projects in Spain, the United States, and Chile, reaching 5,120 MW of wind and solar capacity in operation.
Performance and transformation Repsol has continued to drive the transformation of its businesses, focusing on a profitable energy transition.

At Upstream, the company continued to deploy the strategic lines through active portfolio management, prioritizing those areas with the greatest strategic fit in terms of value and competitive advantages, such as the integration of assets in the North Sea in the United Kingdom alongside NEO Energy Group and the agreement to merge them shortly with those of TotalEnergies UK, or divestments in certain countries (Colombia and Indonesia) and in non-strategic assets.
Production (548 Kboe/d) was 4% lower than in 2024, largely due to divestments, partially mitigated by the alliance with NEO Energy (see section 5.1) and the absence of force majeure outages in Libya.

At Industrial, comprising the company’s industrial complexes in Spain, the use of facilities was affected by various stoppages and incidents, including those caused by the electricity blackout that occurred on April 28.
Meanwhile, the Chemical businesses had to adapt their production, logistical, and commercial schemes to respond to an environment of weak demand and low product prices.

Repsol pressed ahead with the transformation of large industrial complexes during the period, focusing on to the future sustainability of the industry by approving the investment at the Ecoplanta in Tarragona, which will become the first plant in Europe to manufacture renewable and circular methanol from waste, and two large-scale electrolyzers (100 MW each), one in Cartagena and the other at the Petronor refinery.

The company is also moving forward with the construction of its second renewable fuels plant in Puertollano, as well as the synthetic fuels plant (demo e-fuels) and the 10 MW electrolyzer at the Port of Bilbao.
Moreover, a major technological milestone was achieved with the first industrial-scale production of 100% renewable gasoline (Nexa 95 gasoline) in Tarragona.

Further highlights included the acquisition of 40% of three industrial complexes through our stake in Bunge Ibérica, thus allowing Repsol to access a broad portfolio of low-carbon intensity raw materials to produce renewable fuels.
At Customer, notable efforts were made in the period to develop Repsol’s multi-energy offer, focusing on the customer and supported by digitalization.

Repsol managed to increase the number of electricity and gas customers (3.0 million of customers, up 21% on the previous year) and digital customers (more than 10 million), most of whom now use the Waylet app.
At the end of the year, Repsol already had upward of 1,500 service stations supplying 100% renewable fuel across Spain and Portugal, under the Diesel Nexa brand and 100% renewable Nexa gasoline.
Moreover, the number of public charging points up and running increased to more than 3,600.

At Low Carbon Generation, a total installed capacity in operation of 8,030 MW (5,120 MW in renewable power) was reached, with the start-up of new projects in Spain (wind: Delta 2, Pi and Cerrato; and solar: Trillo), the United States (solar: Outpost and Pinnington), and Chile (wind: Antofagasta), all of which helped to increase the generation of renewable wind and solar electricity by 43%.
Further progress was also made in developing operations in the United States, with the start of construction of Pecan Prairie (a 595 MW solar plant in Texas).

Other highlights included the fifth asset rotation in Spain (sale of 49% of a 400 MW wind and solar portfolio) and the first rotation in the United States (with the incorporation of a partner in a 777 MW solar and storage portfolio and the agreement to incorporate it in another 629 MW solar plant), which also included financing obtained through the monetization of tax credits (“tax equity”).
For more information, see section 5. Business performance.
Sustainability Repsol remains aligned with the global objectives of limiting warming to 1.5°C by the end of the century and achieving net-zero greenhouse gas emissions by 2050.

As part of its strategy to manage climate-related risks and opportunities, the Company has established targets to reduce absolute greenhouse gas emissions across Scopes 1, 2, and 3: a 20% reduction by 2030 compared with the 2018 base year (224 Mt CO₂e) and the objective of net-zero greenhouse gas emissions by 2050.
In 2025, Repsol’s emissions (Scopes 1, 2, and 3) amounted to 184.7 Mt CO₂e, representing a reduction of 17.4% compared with the base year.
Furthermore, Repsol has defined a Carbon Intensity Indicator (CII), measured as emissions per unit of primary energy, with a target of greenhouse gas emissions neutrality by 2050.
In 2025, this indicator reached its interim target, with a reduction of 15.1% compared with 2016.
Also in 2025, Repsol defined new Company-wide targets related to air pollution and biodiversity.
For more information, see section 6.

Brand refresh: “With all the energy” En 20In 2025, Repsol launched its revamped brand to adapt it to the Company’s strategy; a strategy which, through its businesses, responds to the energy needs of society, with a focus on all energies to be able to offer solutions that best suit each person.

Our company 2.1 Business model Exploration After acquiring new acreage, we carry out geological and geophysical work, conduct environmental impact studies, and perform exploratory drilling to evaluate its potential, using cutting-edge digital technologies to analyze the information.
Retail and wholesale supply of gas We market and trade natural gas in North America, where we also have an LNG regasification plant in Canada.
We also market and trade LNG globally and natural gas to wholesale customers in Spain and Portugal.
Refining We transform crude oil and various alternative raw materials into value-added products such as fuels, advanced biofuels and carbon- neutral materials.
Our priorities also include the production of renewable hydrogen, which will be key to decarbonizing various sectors.

Development We drill wells, build collection systems and processing plants and evacuation and transport systems, always under sustainability, safety and transparency policies that ensure the proper development of the project.
Production We extract hydrocarbons from the reservoir to market and sell them.
In addition, we carry out maintenance, control and transport work leveraged on artificial intelligence technologies and following the same sustainability and safety policies as in previous phases.
Trading We transport the hydrocarbons produced to supply raw material to our refineries or we sell them on international markets.
We also export the surplus production of our industrial complexes that are not consumed in the domestic market.
Lubricants, Asphalts, Aviation and Specialized products We develop, produce and market lubricants, aviation fuels, asphalt bitumen and petroleum-based specialties in more than 90 countries worldwide.
• Customer: mobility (service stations) and marketing of fuels (gasoline, diesel, aviation kerosene, liquefied petroleum gases, biofuels, etc.
), electricity and gas and lubricants, asphalts and other specialties.
• emissions electricity (combined cycle natural gas and pumped storage) and renewable sources.

Industrial: oil refining, petrochemicals and trading, transportation and marketing of crude oil, natural gas and fuels, including the development of new growth platforms such as hydrogen, sustainable biofuels and synthetic fuels.
For more information on business segments, see section 5 Performance of our businesses.

Chemicals We produce and market a wide variety of petrochemical products, which are used to manufacture everyday objects that improve people’s quality of life, well-being and safety and are found in almost all industrial segments.
LPG We distribute and market liquefied petroleum gas in Spain, Portugal and France.
We do this in various formats: Bottled, bulk and AutoGas.
Low-emission electricity generation Repsol has low-emission electricity generation projects (hydroelectric plants, combined cycles, cogeneration plants, wind and photovoltaic farms).
It builds new renewable assets to increase their capacity.
Mobility We have the largest network of service stations in Spain, where we offer customers multi-energy solutions linked to the Waylet app.
We are pioneers when it comes to introducing renewable fuels and we will soon be offering renewable hydrogen for mobility.
We have one of the most important networks of electric charging points in Spain and we are a leader in AutoGas.

Retail supply of electricity and gas We supply low-emission gas and electricity in Spain and Portugal by offering our customers cutting-edge digital solutions and energy management initiatives such as self-consumption and distributed generation.

Environment Environment 2025 Brent Henry Hub Electricity pool – Spain 69.1 USD/bbl 3.4 USD/MBtu 65.5 €/MWh -14 % Change vs. 2024 +51% Change vs. 2024 +4% Change vs. 2024 Rising geopolitical and trade tensions High volatility in energy markets Depreciation of the dollar 3.1 Macroeconomic environment Recent economic trends Global activity remained relatively stable in 2025, following a prolonged and challenging period marked by several significant shocks.

According to International Monetary Fund (WEO, October 2025), global economic growth was around 3.2% in 2025 (3.3% in 2024), with global output close to the potential level, that is, sustainably achievable without generating serious imbalances.
More importantly, the disinflation process continued, albeit more gradually, with global inflation projected to be around 4.2% in 2025.
Since early 2025, the global environment has become substantially more uncertain as a result of the shift in trade and geopolitical policy promoted by the U.S. administration.

On April 2, the U.S. government imposed new tariffs on a broad group of countries, including the European Union and China, setting a universal minimum tariff of 10% and additional tariffs for partners with larger bilateral surpluses.

Although the initial market reaction was adverse, it was subsequently tempered by the exemptions announced and the efforts made by most countries to negotiate bilateral agreements to avoid an escalation of trade protectionism.
The only exception was China, which has taken a more aggressive stance by imposing restrictions on the export of rare earths.

In recent months, both countries have reached a partial truce that reduces part of the tariffs and puts back certain restrictions for a year, although without resolving the underlying structural tensions.
The level of protectionism remains the highest since the mid-20th century.
In this context, the impact on inflation is manifested asymmetrically.

In the United States, tariffs are already exerting upward pressure on goods prices, partially offset by the fall in energy and services, while in Europe and other regions the appreciation of the euro and lower external demand are generating disinflationary effects.
Trend in interest rates Source: Bloomberg and Repsol's Research Department.
In this context, a divergence in monetary trajectories emerged between the European Central Bank (ECB) and the U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed), although this has become less noticeable in recent months.
The ECB began a cycle of cuts in June 2024, bringing its deposit rate close to 2.0% by mid-2025.

The Fed, on the other hand, remained more cautious (given the relative buoyancy still shown by the US economy and the persistence of inflationary risks linked to tariffs) and put back its rate cuts until September 2025, from which time, and until the end of the year, it implemented three cuts, leaving the federal funds rate in the 3.50–3.75% range.
This change in tone is explained by a slowdown in the labor market that is beginning to outweigh the risk of inflation.

As for the exchange rate, and following a 2024 dominated by the strength of the dollar, aided by the rate differential and the greater relative buoyancy of the U.S. economy, we witnessed a gradual depreciation of the U.S. currency in the early stages of 2025.
This trend reflected the change in tone of the Fed's monetary policy, the lower attractiveness of dollar assets and a certain perception of legal and macroeconomic uncertainty.
The depreciation was especially visible against the euro, which has acted as a strong reserve currency.
However, after this initial phase of appreciation, the movement has stabilized, and in recent months the euro/dollar exchange rate has remained around 1.16– 1.17.
More precisely, at the end of December 2025, the euro/dollar exchange rate stood at 1.18.

≡ Summary of the year 2025 Our Company Environment Financial performance and shareholder return Performance of our businesses Sustainability Outlook Appendices EUR/USD exchange rate performance (monthly averages) In this context, towards the end of 2025, global geopolitical tensions eased, which further contributed to the moderation of crude oil prices.

However, uncertainty remains, especially in relation to developments in major producing countries such as Russia, Iran, and Venezuela, where further developments could reintroduce volatility into the market.
Brent price performance ($/bbl) Source: Bloomberg and Repsol Research Unit.
For more information, see section 7.1 Outlook for the sector.
3.2 Energy landscape Crude oil – Brent In 2025, the average price of Brent stood at $69.1/bl, after a year marked by high volatility that led crude oil to move in a range of more than $20.
The year started with strong upticks, peaking at $82.03/bl on January 15 due to low temperatures in both the United States and Europe, which boosted demand for diesel.
Subsequently, the market experienced a drop to near $60/bl on May 1, reflecting a broader context of lower economic strength and trade tensions.

The trend in the price was largely determined by geopolitical uncertainty, with alternating episodes of increases and corrections, closing the year at around $61/bbl, as geopolitical uncertainty eased.
On the demand side, the initial impact of trade tensions between various economies moderated as the year progressed.
The IMF's improved growth projections for 2025 and 2026 offered a more favorable environment for global oil consumption.

However, significant doubts persisted over Chinese demand, driven by structural factors such as prolonged weakness in the real estate sector and the transition to cleaner energy, especially LNG and electric vehicles.
As a result, despite a somewhat better macroeconomic climate than that observed after the April tariff announcement, the pace of demand growth showed signs of slowing compared to 2024.
The factor that exerted the greatest downward pressure on prices was supply, especially due to OPEC+'s production strategy.

Between April and September, the group reintroduced the 2.2 million barrels a day it had cut in November 2023 to the market, accelerating a process that had originally been planned for an 18- month horizon.
Moreover, the gradual reversal of the 1.65 million barrels a day withdrawn in April 2023 got underway in October 2025.
These movements unbalanced the market and weakened the fundamentals supporting prices, causing its potential for recovery to be limited.
OPEC+’s announcement to suspend barrel returns during the first quarter of 2026 reflects its intention to halt a further decline in a period characterized by lower seasonal demand.
Source: Bloomberg and Repsol Research Unit.
Natural Gas - Henry Hub (HH) In 2025, the price of U.S. HH natural gas averaged $3.4 MBtu, above the 2024 average ($2.3/MBtu).

During January and February of 2025, the intense cold caused production freeze-offs and high heating demand, which increased the price during the first quarter and eliminated the surplus of gas in U.S. storage compared with the average for the last five years, which had kept prices moderate.
In the second and third quarters, the existing deficit in storage was reduced, bringing it above the average for the last five years.

Production remained at historically high values of 109.1 bcf/d in August and is expected to remain close to these levels for the rest of the year, driven mainly by demand from exports, due to the increase in LNG feed gas (new LNG export facilities and additional trains at existing terminals that have come online) and increased pipeline exports to Mexico.
Additionally, HH prices increased in the fourth quarter due to higher winter demand for heating in the United States.
Henry Hub price performance ($/MBtu) Source: Bloomberg and Repsol Research Unit.

≡ Summary of the year 2025 Our Company Environment Financial performance and shareholder return Performance of our businesses Sustainability Outlook Appendices Electricity prices In 2025, the average wholesale price of electricity in Spain stood at €65.5/MWh, slightly higher than in 2024 (62.9 €/MWh).

Despite the significant change brought about by the increase in self-consumption and new photovoltaic generation facilities, the system remains in a context of growth in demand and an increase in gas prices compared with 2024.
This, together with lower hydroelectric generation, despite the maximum levels in hydroelectric dams due to the rains in the first half of the year, led to an increase in prices compared with 2024.
Although the annual average has hardly changed, there is significant volatility in hourly prices.

While last year (the first in which negative prices were recorded), these only reached -2 €/MWh, this year the minimum has reached -15 €/MWh (largely as a result of the start of operations in “enhanced mode” following the blackout that affected the entire Iberian Peninsula and the south of France).
Maximum prices have also increased, reaching 240 €/MWh, a figure that had only been reached in 2021 and 2022, in the midst of the gas crisis.

The trend of steadily declining prices in the central hours of the day continues, causing the price captured by solar installations to be approximately half of the average daily price (just five years ago it was higher than the daily price).
In addition, in 2025 there were almost as many hours with prices close to zero as there had been in 2024, although they were spread out more evenly during the year.
In total, they amounted to more than 1,500 hours (the years with the most hours at low prices —2014 and 2023—they barely reached 500 hours).

All of this has been causing difficulties among the promoters of photovoltaic installations, especially those who sell their production to the market and those who are considering the construction of new plants.
Conversely, it is conducive to energy storage projects, either at pumping stations or with batteries, given their greater need and profitability.
On the demand side, demand increased by almost 3% compared with 2024 (this figure is substantially lower after adjusting for the effects of temperature and working-day effects).
This growth, the second consecutive increase, appears to be related to the demographic and economic growth of the country.

Notably, the preliminary (provisional) data indicate that, despite the fact that the blackout in April may have sparked greater interest in the installation of residential self-consumption systems (with and without batteries), this was not able to offset the lower level of installations in the industrial sector, causing a decline in new self-consumption installations for the third year running.
The generation mix was once again characterized by new highs in photovoltaic generation, driven by increased installed capacity, although the greatest growth was seen in gas-fired generation.

Since the blackout, the operation of the system has prioritized stability of supply, which led to greater use of combined cycles (increasing almost 30% compared with 2024), reaching levels similar to those recorded in 2020, when the price of gas was less than half of what it reached in 2025.
OMIE electricity pool – price performance (€/ MWh) Source: Bloomberg and Repsol Research Unit.
CO2 emission allowances The average price of CO2 emission allowances in 2025 increased to almost 74.9 €/t, up 10 €/t on the average for 2024.
During the first part of the year, the market continued to trade in parallel to European gas.
However, in the second quarter, while the price of gas remained stable, CO2 prices rose from 65 €/t to over 80 €/t, even with lower demand for emission allowances.

This loss of correlation is due to the expectation that in 2026 there will be a deficit of allowances for various reasons, including the entry into force of the Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM); the possible tightening of the Market Stability Reserve (MSR) and delays in the cancellation of surplus transport rights.
In addition, this tension in the market is happening in a context of the gradual phase-out of free allocations, which is constraining supply in the medium term.
Trend in CO2 prices (€/t) Source: Bloomberg and Repsol Research Unit.

Adjusted income in 2025 amounted to €754 million, compared with €644 million in 2024.
The change is mainly due to: • At Mobility, results in Spain improved following an increase in volumes sold of automotive diesel and gasoline (lower in 2024 as a result of market fraud).
Meanwhile, supply procurement costs fell in Spain and Portugal.
• Results were up at Lubricants, Aviation, Asphalts and Specialized Products, driven by improved margins and also by higher volumes at Aviation and Lubricants.
• Improved results at LPG, mainly due to better margins (predominantly in relation to bottled gas).
• Earnings at Electricity & Gas sales were up, due to higher sales arising in turn from an increase in customers, who reached 3.0 million (including Spain and Portugal).
For more information on the activities of the segment, see section 5.3 Customer.
The main reasons for this change are as follows: • The Refining business in Spain underperformed compared with 2024.

Although margin indicators were, on average, higher than those in 2024 (7.9 $/bbl compared with 6.6 $/bbl in 2024), the results were lower as a result of lower distillation and conversion utilization ratios (impacted by an increase in maintenance shutdowns and incidents, such as the presence of water in the Maya crude processed or the power outages that occurred in the second quarter— including the one on April 28—), higher costs (maintenance, personnel, depreciation, etc.
), and the effect of the depreciation of the dollar.
• At Repsol Peru, results increased compared with the previous year, due to better refining margins, and higher earnings at service stations.
• At Chemicals, weak demand and strong international competition persisted, leading the European business to incur losses, despite efforts to optimize operations and costs.
The activity of the plants was also impacted by the electricity blackout that took place on April 28.

• At the Trading businesses, although a good level of results was maintained, they fared worse than in 2024 due to a lower contribution from the gas businesses in the United States (where margins captured in the winter season of 2024 were extraordinarily high), and also the interruption since March of operations with Venezuela.
For further information on the segment's activities, see section 5.2 Industrial.

• At Renewables, earnings were higher than in 2024, mainly due to higher captured prices and increased production (new projects commissioned in 2025 that increased operating capacity by 73% vs. December 31, 2024), as well as the application of tax incentives in the United States.
These impacts were partially mitigated by higher fixed costs.

• At Combined Cycle Plants, results were up due to higher volumes (greater contribution of this technology following the blackout experienced in the Iberian Peninsula on April 28) and prices, partially offset by higher costs (gas prices and depreciation).
For more information on the segment's activities, see section 5.4 Low-Carbon Generation.

Shareholder return in 2025 During 2025 dividends totaling €0.975 gross per share were paid out, as follows: • • In January, a total of €0.475 gross per share was paid, corresponding to: (i) a gross dividend of €0.45 per share charged to voluntary reserves; and (ii) an interim dividend of €0.025 gross per share charged against 2024 earnings.
The amount paid out amounted to €5509 million.
In July, a total of €0.50 gross per share was paid out against voluntary reserves, for a total amount of €56810 million.

In addition, in July and December, two capital reductions were carried out, through the cancellation of 52 million treasury shares (29 and 23 million shares in the first and second reductions, respectively), which contributes to the shareholder return by increasing earnings per share.
As of December 31, 2025, Repsol held a total of 274,966 treasury shares11, representing 0.02% of share capital at that date.
Shareholder return in 2026 In January 2026, shareholders were remunerated with the payment of €0.5 gross per share charged to voluntary reserves.
The total amount paid out amounted to €55212 million.

Additionally, the Company’s Board of Directors has agreed to propose to the next Annual General Meeting the payment in 2026 of a supplementary dividend (in addition to that paid in January 2026), charged to 2025 profit, of €0.551 gross per share, which is expected to take place on July 8, 2026.

It has also agreed to propose to the next Annual General Shareholders’ Meeting a capital reduction through the redemption of the shares acquired under a share buyback program, with a maximum net investment of €350 million.
For more information, see Note 11 “Equity” to the 2025 consolidated Financial Statements.

Performance of our businesses 5.1 Upstream Our activities • New areas: identification and entry into new projects (organic or inorganic growth).
• Exploration: geology, geophysics and exploratory drilling activities in the search for hydrocarbon resources.
• Evaluation: drilling of appraisal wells, definition of the volumes discovered and determination of their commercial viability.
• Development: drilling of production wells, construction of collection systems, processing plants, and evacuation and transport systems to produce reserves.
• Production: commercial operation of hydrocarbons.
• Decommissioning: abandonment and refurbishment of all facilities to leave the area in the same environmental condition as prior to the commencement of operations.

Information on oil and gas exploration and production activities Repsol simultaneously publishes, together with this Management Report, the “Information on oil and gas exploration and production activities” available at www.repsol.com, which contains detailed information on mining acreage, exploration and development activity, proven net reserves, future cash flows, and production.

Main events in the period Efficiency measures and a focus on the value of the asset portfolio have been instrumental in capturing a strong adjusted net profit in the current context of price volatility.
For more information, see section 4.1 Results.

Portfolio management An agreement was reached in 2025 to consolidate the Upstream business in the United Kingdom with NEO UK and TotalEnergies UK and Repsol divested its operations in certain countries (Colombia and Indonesia) and in non-strategic assets.
These decisions are part of the broader portfolio optimization strategy, prioritizing assets with greater alignment with its profitability and sustainability objectives.
These divestments allow Repsol to focus resources on key regions and projects with greater potential for long-term value generation.

≡ Summary of the year 2025 Our Company Environment Financial performance and shareholder return Performance of our businesses Sustainability Outlook Appendices Reserves The change in net proven reserves in 2025 was -38 Mboe, mainly reflecting the net effect of purchases and sales of reserves, extensions and discoveries, and revisions of previous estimates.
The total reserves replacement ratio stood at -19% in 2025 (55% in 2024).
Financing In September, Repsol successfully completed its largest bond issuance in dollars, totaling $2.5 billion, through its subsidiary Repsol E&P Capital Markets US LLC.
The transaction, structured in three tranches with maturities between 2028 and 2035, was oversubscribed by 6.9 times the amount offered, reflecting strong interest from the U.S. market.
The proceeds will be used to refinance intragroup debt.
This issuance strengthens Repsol E&P’s financial position and expands its investor base in the United States, consolidating its access to international capital markets.
The bonds are guaranteed by Repsol E&P and are rated BBB+ by S&P and Fitch.

Average production Average production amounted to 548 Kboe/d in 2025, down 4% on 2024, mainly due to divestments in Colombia and Indonesia in 2025, in Eagle Ford Southwest in June 2024, and in mature assets in Trinidad and Tobago in December 2024, as well as maintenance activities in the United Kingdom and Norway, and the natural decline of the fields.

This was partially offset by the production start-up of Cypre and Mento in Trinidad and Tobago and of León and Castilla in the Gulf of Mexico, the integration of the assets in the United Kingdom with NEO, the acquisition of Tomoporo-La Ceiba in Venezuela in the second quarter of 2024, as well as the absence of periods of force majeure in Libya, and the connection of new wells in Libya and at Marcellus.

Exploratory activity The drilling of five exploratory wells was completed in 2025: two under evaluation (one in Trinidad and Tobago and one in Libya), two positives in Bolivia and one with a negative result in Libya.
The drilling of two delineation wells was also completed: one positive in Trinidad and Tobago and one under evaluation in Libya.
As of December 31, there was an exploratory drilling operation underway in Bolivia (YPC-X43).

(2) P: Production / D: Development / E: Exploration / L: Liquids / G: Gas Main events in the period United States: development of Alaska (Pikka) and start-up of production in GoM (Leon and Castile) On December 31, 2024, Santos Ltd received approval from the Alaska Department of Natural Resources (ADNR) for the 2025 Development Plan (POD 2025) for the Pikka field in the North Slope Basin, where Repsol holds a 49% interest and Santos is the operator with 51%.
The Plan began on February 1, 2025 and is progressing as planned with the drilling of 23 wells (10 production, 11 injection and two service) of the 45 planned and the commissioning of the facilities.
The Nanushuk Processing Plant is 95% complete.
All other work areas required for the start of production are already operational.
Production is estimated to begin at the end of the first quarter of 2026, reaching a sustained gross production level of 80,000 barrels per day for Phase I in the third quarter.
Although not required for the start of production, the seawater treatment plant will start operating in May 2026.

≡ Summary of the year 2025 Our Company Environment Financial performance and shareholder return Performance of our businesses Sustainability Outlook Appendices In December, Repsol was awarded 12 exploration blocks in the Gulf of Mexico at the Big Beautiful Gulf 1 (BBG1) auction, the first deep water oil and gas auction conducted by the United States since 2023.
Repsol submitted bids for 13 ultra-deep water blocks and was awarded 12 (3 alone and 9 with a partner), with a total of 303 km².
In December, the license for the AC 380 and AC 424 blocks in the Alaminos Canyon area of the Gulf of Mexico expired.
In April, the Salamanca floating production unit was mobilized to the Gulf of Mexico to start operations in the Leon and Castile fields, where Repsol holds 50% and 35.62%, respectively.
The production capacity is 60,000 barrels per day of crude oil and 40 million cubic feet of gas.
The Salamanca platform is the result of a refurbishment of an old facility, which has led to an 87% reduction in emissions compared to the construction of a new unit.
In September, the first well began production in the León field and, in October, two other wells began production, one in León and the other in Castile.
This strategic project adds significant long-term production capacity in the United States and reflects Repsol's focus on this key basin for the Upstream business.

Main events in the period Bolivia: variable gas demand Operations in Bolivia were marked by the variability of gas demand, mainly due to the adjustment that Petrobras Brazil makes on a recurring basis on its volumes between the guaranteed contractual minimum (Take or Pay) and the committed daily amount (QDC).
There are contractual obligations that ensure a minimum level of payment and supply even if actual consumption fluctuates, providing stability to the seller's income.

the Group to carry out the ordinary, incidental and necessary transactions for the winding-down of the previously authorized operations until May 27, 2025.

Brazil: progress in the Raia project In June, the Brazilian regulator (Agencia Nacional do Petróleo) technically approved the development plan for the Raia project (Block BM-C-33) in the Campos basin, where Repsol is a partner along with Equinor, Sinopec and Petrobras.
In July, Brazil’s environmental regulator (IBAMA) granted the installation license for the Raia export gas pipeline project, allowing work to begin on the 15-kilometer section of shallow water.
The complete gas pipeline will span approximately 200 kilometers, connecting Raia's production, storage and offloading vessel (FPSO) with the Cabiúnas terminal in Macaé.

Raia is one of the country's most important natural gas developments, with the capacity to transport 16 million cubic meters of natural gas per day and containing recoverable oil/condensate reserves of more than one billion barrels of oil equivalent (boe).
The project is scheduled to start operating in 2028.
Colombia: withdrawal from the country In February, the sale of 45% of the CPO-9 asset and 25% of SierraCol Energy Arauca LLC was completed.
For more information, see the section “Assets and liabilities held for sale” in Note 19 to the consolidated Financial Statements.

Peru: sustained production In tandem with this, and within the framework of the Recapex II project to maintain production at Block 57, the seventh and last compressor was successfully installed and commissioned, improving the reliability of the plant and guaranteeing the production of the block.

Trinidad and Tobago: development of Ginger and commissioning of Cypre and Mento The company BpTT, a company owned by BP (70%) and Repsol (30%), approved in March the development of the Ginger offshore project, a gas field discovered in 2019.
This field will be connected to the Mahogany B platform, with a production target of approximately 62,000 barrels of oil equivalent per day (boe/d).
Gas production is expected to start in 2027.
In March, gas production began at the Cypre project, located in Block 3 off the coast.
This first phase connects four wells to the Juniper platform, with expected production of up to 45,000 boe/d.
A second phase with three additional wells is planned for the second half of the year and will come into production in 2026.
In June, gas production began at the Mento-1ST well, located in the SMR (Ska Mento Reggae) block.
This well, which was discovered in 2020, marks the beginning of the development of the Mento field, which includes a facility with a capacity for 12 wells.
At the same time, a drilling campaign will be launched to complete the project's remaining seven wells.
In August, production began on the second well at Mento.

Venezuela: Revocation of the U.S. OFAC license and change in the political landscape In March 2025, the OFAC revoked the Special License that allowed the Company to continue its operations in Venezuela and issued a Wind-Down License authorizing Throughout 2025, the political and economic crisis persisted, influenced by the international sanctions imposed on PDVSA and the Government of Venezuela.
This situation worsened following the military activities deployed by the United States in the Caribbean under the counter-narcotics program.

In January 2026, the National Assembly approved a partial reform of the Organic Hydrocarbons Law, which includes three main areas of amendment: (i) the incorporation of “successful business models” from the Anti-Blockade Law; (ii) the proposal of mechanisms to ensure the viability of projects which, due to their technical or economic characteristics, require special considerations (e.g., undeveloped fields); and (iii) the proposal to strengthen legal safeguards for investment by introducing the possibility of resorting to independent mediation and arbitration mechanisms.

In addition, in January and February 2026, the OFAC issued several General Licenses enabling operations in the oil and gas sector in Venezuela, including those involving the Government of Venezuela, Petróleos de Venezuela, S.A. (PDVSA), or any entity in which PDVSA owns, directly or indirectly, an interest equal to or greater than 50%.

In this regard, several events have taken place from year-end to the date of authorization for issue of this consolidated Management Report that could have an impact in the future on Repsol’s operations and assets in the country, in particular the new general license (GL 50) that authorizes Repsol to carry out transactions with the Government of Venezuela or entities of the PDVSA group related to the oil and gas sector in Venezuela.

Spain: progress in decommissioning assets In October, the company Dolphin Drilling signed a contract with Repsol for the abandonment of underwater wells in Spain.
To this end, the Borgland Dolphin semi-submersible platform will be mobilized to Tarragona at the end of 2026 to begin work.
The contract envisages a scope of seven firm wells and has a minimum duration of 220 days, with an option to extend it to include three additional wells.
In the meantime, the decommissioning of the Casablanca platform and its connections to the wells continues, and is expected to be completed by the end of March 2026.

United Kingdom: integration into Neo Next and Neo Next+ and consolidation of acreage In April, Repsol started the drilling campaign for three development wells at Piper, in which Neo Next holds a 100% stake.
In July, the alliance between Repsol Resources UK and NEO Energy was completed, forming the joint venture Neo Next Energy Limited (“Neo Next”).

The joint venture, owned by Repsol E&P (45%) and NEO UK (55%), is now one of the largest oil and gas producers on the United Kingdom's continental shelf (UKCS), with a projected production for 2025 of around 130,000 boe/d.
NEO UK's production as of December 31, 2024 was approximately 68,000 boe/d, while RRUK's was 34,000 boe/d.
The new entity operates a diversified portfolio that, as of the date of this Report, includes 11 production sites, as well as significant undeveloped reserves.

In addition, this alliance will significantly enhance the joint venture's operational scale, efficiency and growth prospects, while reinforcing Repsol’s long-term goal of maximizing the value of its exploration and production assets in the United Kingdom.
In September, Neo Next completed the acquisition of Gran Tierra North Sea Limited (GTNSL) for $7.5 million.
GTNSL holds 100% of the UKCS P2358 licence, which encompasses the Serenity discovery off the coast of the Moray Firth.
This asset has development potential through connection with the Bleo Holm FPSO floating production unit, already operated by Neo Next.
In October, the redeveloped Murlach crude oil field, with a subsea connection operated by BP (Neo Next 20% WI), was put into production with a gross capacity of 15,000 boe/d.

In November, Neo Next exercised its pre-emptive right over two BP assets in the North Sea to acquire the licenses operated by TotalEnergies P111 (Culzean, the country's largest gas-producing field) and P2544 (exploratory permit), after Serica Energy announced its intention to acquire 32% for $232 million.
Following the operation, Neo Next increased its stake to 50.01%.
The transaction was completed on December 31, 2025.
In December, the Clyde field (Neo Next 100% WI) ended its production to begin the abandonment and decommissioning of the facilities.
In December 2025, NeoNext agreed with TotalEnergies to merge its Upstream assets in the British North Sea.

This transaction will result in the creation of the NEO NEXT+ joint venture, which will position itself as the largest independent oil and gas producer on the UK Continental Shelf (UKCS), with production expected to be around 250,000 barrels of oil equivalent per day in 2026.
The shareholding structure of NEO NEXT+ will be divided between TotalEnergies (47.5%), HitecVision (28.8%), and Repsol (23.6%).
The addition of TotalEnergies’ high-quality assets will strengthen the new company's balance sheet and operating capabilities.
In addition, TotalEnergies will retain up to $2.3 billion in decommissioning obligations, improving the cash flows of the combined business.
The transaction, pending regulatory approval, is scheduled to close in the first half of 2026.
Norway: Seismic campaign in Visund and improvements for Mikkel In July, Repsol, through its operating partner Equinor, carried out a 232 km² 4D seismic campaign in the Visund field in the North Sea.
In September, Equinor commenced phase 2 of the underwater compression project in the Asgard field in the Norwegian Sea.
The second compressor module was installed at a depth of 270 meters, which will allow production to be maintained by increasing the pressure between the wells and the Asgard B platform.
With both phases, it is expected to increase the recovery rate to 90% of the Midgard and Mikkel fields (in which Repsol has a 7.65% stake as a non-operating partner).

Algeria: extension of the Block 405A license and further progress at Reggane In January 2025, the contract signed for the 25-year extension of operations, with an option for an additional 10 years, at Block 405a (MLN, EMK and Ourhoud licenses), where Repsol is a partner with a 24.5% stake in MLN, 6.37% in EMK, and 1.34% in Ourhoud, was published in the Algerian Official Gazette (at the time of its entry into force).
In February, Repsol received authorization from the Algerian government to carry out its investment in Block 405a.

In November, the Algerian Council of Ministers approved and published in the country's Official Gazette, the amendment of Factor A (part of the formula that regulates the distribution of production between international companies and the state-owned company) of the new block 405a contract to start receiving hydrocarbon rights.
The Indonesian company Pertamina is the temporary operator until the new Groupement is formed in the first quarter of 2026.
In September, the drilling and development of the Reggane, Kahlouche and Kahlouche Sud fields was completed at Reggane.
The final well at the Azrafil Sudest field (AZSE-20) continues to operate and is expected to be completed by the end of the first quarter of 2026.
Meanwhile, the Tio 4 well remains under evaluation.

Libya: First exploratory tender In December 2024, exploratory activity resumed, after having been suspended for a decade, in the Murzuq basin with the drilling of the Nesser-1 well, which began on December 31 and was declared negative in 2025.
On March 3, 2025, the NOC started a bidding round consisting of 22 blocks (11 onshore and 11 offshore), spanning a total area of 235,000 km2.
In 2025, the development drilling campaign continued with five active rigs; 30 wells were completed and five were pending completion as of December 31, 2025.

≡ Summary of the year 2025 Our Company Environment Financial performance and shareholder return Performance of our businesses Sustainability Outlook Appendices 5.2 Industrial Our activities • Refining: the transformation of oil into fuels and other derivative products.
• Chemicals: production and marketing of a wide variety of products (including basic petrochemicals and derivatives), with a focus on the circular economy.

• Trading: supply of crude oil, intermediate products, chemical raw materials, and lipid residues to Repsol's industrial complexes, and third parties; marketing of petroleum products, renewable fuels, and petrochemical products in international markets; and maritime transport for the Group's activity and third parties.
• Wholesale, marketing, and gas trading: supply and trading of LNG/natural gas, including LNG regasification, and marketing to customers in North America and the Iberian Peninsula.

• Hydrogen, Renewable Fuels and Circular Economy: development, production and marketing of renewable, low-carbon footprint and circular products, promoting the use of alternative resources obtained in a sustainable way, such as renewable and low carbon electrolytic hydrogen, biofuels, synthetic fuels, and other products that contribute to decarbonization and the manufacture of circular materials, promoting innovation and the circular economy.

Main events in the period The activity of industrial businesses has been marked by the international context determined by the high volatility in the markets for raw materials and products, which has led to the adaptation of production, logistics and commercial schemes to the changing situation.

All this has not detracted from the drive towards decarbonization, as evidenced by the progress made toward the various projects at the industrial complexes: final investment decision for the Tarragona Ecoplant, progress in the construction of the second renewable fuels plant in the Puertollano Industrial Complex, acquisition of a 40% stake in Bunge Ibérica, which owns various industrial facilities in the Iberian Peninsula, and key milestones for the development of renewable hydrogen projects in Spain, such as the construction of two electrolyzers at the Cartagena and Petronor plants.
For more information, see section 4.1 Results.

≡ Summary of the year 2025 Our Company Environment Financial performance and shareholder return Performance of our businesses Sustainability Outlook Appendices The Group owns and operates six refineries, five in Spain (Cartagena, A Coruña, Bilbao, Puertollano and Tarragona) with a total distillation capacity of 896 kboe/ d (including in Tarragona the stake in Asfaltos Españoles S.A.), and one in Peru, where it owns a 99.2% stake, with an installed capacity of 117 kboe/d.
Performance: volatility in margins The volatility of the environment has been evident in the evolution of the refining margin indicator.

In Spain, in the first quarter, the margin indicator was slightly higher than in the fourth quarter of 2024 ($5.3/ bbl vs $4.8/bbl), although it was below the first quarter of 2024 ($11.4/bbl) due to lower margins for diesel.

After a slight improvement in the second quarter ($5.9/ bbl), mainly due to the effect of lower Brent prices, in the third quarter there was a significant increase ($8.8/ bbl) driven by the improvement in diesel due to growing concerns about supply disruptions (capacity closures, geopolitical conflicts), which was further accentuated in the fourth quarter (US sanctions, Ukrainian drone attacks on Russian refineries, refinery maintenance), leading the indicator to a value of $11.1/ bbl.
The average indicator for 2025 (7.9 $/bbl) is higher than for 2024, mainly due to the increase in middle distillate differentials and the positive effect of a lower Brent price.

Average distillation utilization was 82.8% in Spain, compared with 88.1% the previous year, impacted by maintenance stoppages, operational incidents associated with the presence of water in the Maya crude being processed, as well as incidents associated with various electrical blackouts on the Iberian Peninsula, including the mass blackout that took place on April 28.
In July, Petronor completed the shutdown of Plant I after two months of work and an investment of more than €89 million.
Maintenance and technological updating tasks were carried out with the aim of improving safety, increasing efficiency, and reducing emissions.
Among the improvements, the installation of the new Preflash tower stands out, which will reduce CO2 emissions by more than 26,000 tons per year.

In August, the multi-year shutdown began in the Hydroskiming area in Cartagena, with the aim of carrying out maintenance tasks and incorporating new technologies, with a planned investment of €67 million.
In September, Repsol and the Port Authority of A Coruña approved the definitive transfer of their operations to the outer port in Punta Langosteira, currently in the inner port until the end of 2027.
It is planned to invest €140 million in the second phase of the transfer, raising the total investment to more than €250 million.
The agreement includes a 15-year extension in the concession and an occupation of 127,000 m².
The new facilities will allow the operation of organic raw materials and low-carbon products, promoting the A Coruña Green Port.
In 2024, Repsol will manage 10 million tons, equivalent to 60% of total port traffic.
In November, Petronor began the installation of the new polymer-grade propylene splitter to replace the current one.

The project, which has an investment of €58 million, will be launched during the first quarter of 2026 and will allow greater purification of propylene, while aiming to improve energy efficiency and contributing to the decarbonization of the refinery.
In Peru, the refining margin index stood at 10.4 $/bbl, compared with 5.9 $/bbl in 2024, mainly due to better product spreads.
Average distillation utilization was 89.4% in Peru, compared with 83.9% the previous year, as a result of the multi-year shutdown of the refinery that began in May 2024.

Chemicals Main assets Production at Repsol’s Chemicals business is concentrated in three petrochemical complexes, located in Spain (Puertollano, Tarragona) and Portugal (Sines), in which there is a high level of integration between basic chemicals and derived chemicals, as well as with the Group’s refining activities in the case of the Spanish complexes.

Repsol owns subsidiaries and affiliates through which it operates plants that manufacture polyolefin compounds, synthetic rubber and specialty chemicals, the latter through Dynasol, a 50% alliance with the Mexican group KUO, with plants in Spain, Mexico and China, alongside local partners.
