≡ Summary of the year 2025 Our Company Environment Financial performance and shareholder return Performance of our businesses Sustainability Outlook Appendices Differentiation of our products In July, Dynasol commissioned a new styrene-butadiene rubber (sSBR) production line in Gajano (Santander), increasing capacity by 20 kt/a.
The investment for this new line amounts to 72 million euros, making it the largest in the history of the complex.
Electrification of the complexes In May, the compressor electrification project at the Sines olefins unit was completed.
With the implementation, this project will reduce the direct CO2 emissions of the center by 95 kt/a.

Along the same lines, in Tarragona, progress has also been made in the electrification of large machines, including the olefins unit, with its commissioning scheduled for mid-2026 and a reduction in direct emissions of 165 kt/a.
A new compressor with an electric motor was also installed at the OP/SM plant.
Both actions meet the requirements to be generators of CAEs (Energy Savings Certificates).

Trading The main function of Trading is the management of a diversified portfolio of positions based on experience in the markets, hedging activities, as well as technical inspection activities for ship safety.

The Trading portfolio comprises: (i) the supply of crude oil, intermediate materials, chemical raw materials and lipid residues to industrial complexes and third parties; (ii) marketing of petroleum products, renewable fuels and petrochemicals in international markets; (iii) chartering and maritime transport for the activity of the Group and third parties; and (iv) the management of hedging of crude oil and other products in the financial derivatives markets.
Trading has a presence in Madrid, Houston, Singapore, Lima, and Shanghai.
In July, Repsol added to its fleet the first diesel-electric oil tanker, known as Bahía Candela, designed specifically for the supply of new fuels.
It is equipped with 12 tanks, which allow it to transport up to 8,000 cubic meters of different fuels, and a diesel-electric propulsion system, complemented by a battery energy storage system.
This allows fuel supply operations to be carried out in an all- electric manner.
Performance: environmental uncertainty In 2025, a total of 1,529 vessels were chartered (1,558 in 2024) and 660 Time Charter fleet voyages were made (593 in 2024).

Wholesale and Gas Trading Main assets and operations As of December 31, 2025, the Group had regasification and transmission assets at its marketing businesses in North America, including the Saint John LNG regasification plant and gas pipelines in Canada and the United States.
In the northeastern U.S., where natural gas supply is often constrained by a lack of storage capacity, cold weather scenarios can cause significant spikes in the area’s benchmark prices.
The Company’s activity in this area focuses on optimizing the margin from the marketing and sale of regasified LNG from Saint John LNG and natural gas acquired on the market.
Repsol also markets and trades natural gas in North America from its own production in the United States (Marcellus), as well as from acquisitions from third parties.

It also allows for the efficient supply of gas to meet the Repsol Group’s demand and is responsible for marketing and the trading in the Spanish and Portuguese gas system and in the international LNG market.
Performance: higher gas prices In 2025, commercial activity took place amid rising gas prices but worse margins.
In the United States, volumes sold were up on 2024 (616 TBtu in 2025, +4%), although supply and marketing increased in Spain and internationally (331 TBtu in 2025, +33%).

New agreements and decarbonization In February, Venture Global announced that its Calcasieu Pass LNG facility would begin commercial operations on April 15 of that year, which has allowed Repsol to begin receiving the corresponding cargoes under the long-term contract, albeit with a significant delay compared with what was initially estimated.
The first cargo was received on April 30 and 11 cargoes had been received by December 31, which improves Repsol's LNG business activities and strengthens security of supply.
In September, Repsol became the first supplier in Spain to offer bioLNG from biomethane injected into the grid.

This operation has been carried out from the LNG bunkering terminal (in the port of Bilbao) to the vessel “Salamanca” of Brittany Ferries,which covers the route between Bilbao and Rosslare, in Ireland.

Hydrogen, Renewable Fuels, and Circular Economy Renewable and low-carbon hydrogen In May, Repsol reached a strategic milestone with the publication of the final approval of the Hy2Use Important Project of Common European Interest (IPCEI), which includes its renewable hydrogen projects in Petronor and Cartagena.
These projects, which received a joint allocation of €315 million in public aid (€160 million for Petronor and €155 million for Cartagena), each have an electrolysis capacity of 100 MW.
Together, they will enable the production of up to 30,000 tons of renewable hydrogen per year, while cutting CO2 emissions by up to 334,000 tons annually.

The construction of both projects, which are integrated into strategic industrial clusters and will contribute significantly to the decarbonization of industry and transport, has already been approved, and they are expected to enter into operation in 2029.

Specifically, the Cartagena electrolyzer, construction of which has been approved jointly with Enagás, will involve an investment of over €300 million, while the construction of the electrolyzer at the Petronor Refinery in Muskiz will entail an investment of €292 million.
Overall, both projects are estimated to generate around 1,800 jobs, including direct, indirect, and induced employment, across their various phases.

In June, the final resolution was published whereby the Spanish Government has allocated a total of €1,223 million in NextGenEU funds for the H2 Valles program to seven strategic projects located in Aragon, Andalusia, Castilla y León, Catalonia and Galicia, of which Repsol has been awarded €104.2 million for the T-HYNET project for a 150 MW electrolyzer at Tarragona and €40.8 million for another electrolyzer in the A Coruña valley.

In July, Repsol announced an investment of €16 million in the Puertollano industrial complex, with the aim of producing 5,300 tons per year of renewable hydrogen from bio-based gas (produced from waste), replacing natural gas.
This hydrogen will be used at the new 100% renewable fuels plant, reducing its emissions intensity.
This plant is already in an advanced construction phase and is estimated to be operational by the end of 2026.

In August, a major milestone was reached in moving forward with the 10 MW electrolyzer project in the Port of Bilbao, led by Repsol and in which EVE and Enagás Renovable are participating, with the arrival and start of the assembly of the electrolyzer itself and the main equipment.

This new facility, which is scheduled to be commissioned in 2026, will produce up to 1,330 tons of renewable hydrogen per year, which will be used primarily to supply Repsol and Saudi Aramco’s synthetic fuels (e-fuels) demonstration plant, currently under construction and expected to be fully operational in early 2027.

Moreover, the renewable hydrogen produced there will be used to power Repsol and Saudi Aramco's synthetic fuels (e-fuels) demo plant, under construction, and will be sent to two hydrogen refueling stations that will supply fleets of heavy-duty vehicles and the bus line to the Bilbao airport.

Renewable fuels In March, the acquisition of 40% of Bunge Ibérica, S.A. was completed (a company that operates three industrial assets dedicated to the production of renewable fuels and oils in Bilbao, Barcelona and Cartagena) in exchange for $285 million.
In April, an major milestone was reached in the construction of the second renewable fuels plant at the Puertollano Industrial Complex, with the installation of the new reactor.

The new plant, which is progressing as planned and is expected to be operational in 2026, has the capacity to produce up to 200,000 metric tons of renewable fuels per year, which will cut CO2 emissions by some 750,000 tons per year.
In September, the first industrial-scale production of 100% renewable gasoline was achieved at the Tarragona industrial complex.
Nexa 95 gasoline, of 100% renewable origin, is a high-quality product, designed to make the most of the engine’s performance and keep it in optimal condition.
It is already on sale at 30 service stations distributed in the Community of Madrid, Catalonia, Valencia, the Basque Country, and Aragon.
This new fuel can be used in any gasoline-powered vehicle without the need to modify the car or the refueling infrastructure.
Its use reduces net CO2 emissions by more than 70% compared with conventional gasoline.
In October, Repsol signed an agreement with Norwegian Cruise Line Holdings to supply renewable fuels, including biofuels and renewable methanol for the next eight years.
This partnership has the goal to move both companies to the forefront of the energy transition within the cruise and maritime sectors.

In 2025, the Group's renewable fuel production capacity stood at 1.25 million metric tons per year (Mt/y), of which 0.25 Mt/y corresponds to the Cartagena plant, which is entirely devoted to the production of renewable fuels, and 1 Mt/y to other industrial assets (including ETBE, HVO, SAF and other renewable fuels).

Circular economy In January, Repsol announced that it will invest more than €800 million in the Tarragona Ecoplant, the first plant capable of transforming municipal solid waste into renewable methanol and circular products through a gasification process.

The new plant will have a total production capacity of 240,000 tons per year of renewable and circular methanol and is scheduled to be commissioned by the end of 2029, creating 340 new total jobs (direct, indirect and induced) in the operation phase.
The plant is expected to CO2 emissions by 3.4 Mt in its first 10 years of operation.
The project is being financed under the EU’s Innovation Fund program.

In November, the Tarragona Ecoplant was recognized at COP30 in Brazil as one of the six international success stories in decarbonization, included in the report of the Sustainable Business Group COP (SBCOP).

The Diesel e+5 was also launched in the period, a fuel of intermediate quality lying between e+ and e+10, and already available at 200 service stations.

Following the production, in September, of the first batch of 100% renewable gasoline in Tarragona (a global milestone), its deployment got underway, reaching 30 service stations in Spain by the end of the year.

On the islands of Gran Canaria, Tenerife, and Fuerteventura, the availability of 100% renewable Nexa diesel was expanded during the period, with 65% of service stations in the Canary Islands now offering this fuel.

In December, Repsol was received an award in the Energy Excellence category at the Platts Global Energy Awards, for overcoming the dual challenge of supplying affordable and reliable energy, while promoting decarbonization across its operations and products in the Iberian Peninsula.
At Repsol, we are focused on the circular economy by collecting used cooking oil at authorized service stations.

By recycling this waste, we guarantee its proper treatment, enabling its transformation into renewable fuels with the aim of doubling the percentage of residential waste oil that is currently recycled per year in Spain, which currently barely exceeds 4%.
Since the implementation of the project, more than 195 thousand liters have been collected at the 793 service stations adhered to the initiative across six autonomous regions of Spain.
Repsol handles the entire process, up to the production of the 100% renewable fuel at the Cartagena plant, thus supporting its traceability.
Throughout the year, several agreements were reached to cover electricity and fuel needs, notably including those concluded with: 1.
Aena, for the supply of diesel, biofuel and propane for the 2025–2026 period (extendable by one year) up to a maximum of 700,000 liters of diesel and HVO and up to a 30% increase on each of the lots.

The Repsol Toyota Rally and Toyota Gazoo Racing teams, which competed in the Dakar Rally using 70% renewable fuels; and the agreement reached with Toyota Gazoo Racing was renewed for the entire World Rally Raid Championship.

Alsa, to transform the energy model of road passenger transport in Spain, which includes the goal of reduction of net CO₂ emissions through the use of renewable fuels and the study and development of initiatives for the supply of renewable energies.
It also includes the promotion of energy storage solutions and the exploration of alternatives based on hydrogen and biomethane.
Synergies will be sought in lubricants and digital loyalty and payment platforms, such as Waylet, will be integrated.
Parque Warner Madrid, to cover its entire energy supply, providing electricity, natural gas and 100% renewable fuels, reducing its CO2 emissions by more than 400 kt per year.

Furthermore, Repsol demonstrated its focus on society during the period by collaborating with Omoda&Jaecoo and Record Go, for the rental of 300 vehicles to those affected by the devastating flash floods in Valencia, which includes €100 per month in fuel to be used at Repsol service stations.

Lubricants, Aviation, Asphalts, and Specialized Products Assets and operations The production of Lubricants, Asphalts, and Specialized Products is mainly concentrated in Spain, although in the case of Lubricants there are two additional manufacturing hubs through joint ventures: the Americas, with Bardahl in Mexico, which covers the region, and Southeast Asia, with a presence in Indonesia and Singapore through United Oil, and in the Philippines with Unioil Lubricants.
Both cover areas where the lubricants market is expected to grow the most on average over the coming years.
Repsol also distributes lubricants in Eastern Europe through the Valtec Premium Lubricants joint venture located in Romania.
The commercial division has a strong international component, with deliveries in more than 90 countries around the world.
Repsol markets and sells aviation fuel in various locations, most notably in Spain, France, Portugal and Peru.

It is available for 1,130 connectors and 1,030 charging points.
In June, a new electricity tariff for electric vehicles was launched, with Movistar+ free for one year, and the Zunder network, with more than 1,200 charging points, was also integrated into Waylet.
In December, strategic agreements were signed with Mazda, Toyota and KIA to jointly promote sustainable mobility and energy efficiency in Spain.

In the case of the agreements with Mazda and KIA, the collaboration focuses on proposing comprehensive charging solutions for their customers, and, in the case of KIA, also on the possibility of developing publicly accessible charging infrastructure in its dealer network.

In addition, Repsol has taken part in events to champion sustainable mobility, including the VEM fair in Madrid and the sponsorship of the Eco-rally in A Coruña, where charging points were installed for more than 50 electric vehicles.

New businesses Distributed generation Repsol’s strategy in the Distributed Generation business encompasses various models, including Solar360, collective self-consumption, Energy Communities, and individual self-consumption.

During 2025, the following milestones were achieved: • Through Solar360, photovoltaic panels are being installed on the rooftops of buildings at the Tarragona complex, with an installed capacity of 830 kW.
They aim to generate 1.2 GWh annually, equivalent to the average consumption of 350 households.
This project will be rolled out to all industrial sites across the Iberian Peninsula.

• Significant progress has been made in the promotion and construction of collective self-consumption communities, both in the form of Local Energy Communities and Solar Communities, reaching 65 MWp of installed capacity.
This enables the supply of locally generated solar energy (km 0) to more than 135,000 households across Spain.
• Moreover, the Zaragoza City Council has awarded Repsol the contract to install solar power systems at four public car parks, with a total capacity of 2.8 MW and 40 charging points.
These new solar communities will supply municipal facilities and more than 260,000 households with locally generated energy.

Electric mobility In 2025, Repsol installed 3,648 publicly accessible electric charging points (795 more than in 2024), of which 3,013 are up and running, and Waylet users already have 6,500 charging points available to them (adding the charging points of other interoperated networks).

Throughout 2025, Repsol made progress in electric mobility with innovative solutions and strategic alliances: • Since January, Repsol has remained Redeia's sole partner in electric mobility in Spain, with a 3-year contract for the management of 167 charging points.

It also completed construction and first power was delivered at the Antofagasta project, one of the largest in Chile, with a total installed operating capacity of 364 MW.

In December, Repsol commissioned its first solar hybridization project in Spain, which will add 82 MW of complementary solar capacity to the wind assets of the Delta I project in Aragon (41 MW already in operation).
This expansion improves the management of the energy generated, optimizes the use of existing infrastructure, and increases the operational flexibility of the asset.

Meanwhile, a 50-year extension was granted for the water concession on the Torina River (Cantabria) for the use of hydro energy storage at the Aguayo I and II pumped-storage power plants.

Last but not least, the Escatrón combined-cycle plant obtained authorization for 400 MW under a self- consumption regime to hybridize its facility with renewable energy, enabling it to supply power to a data storage project promoted by a third party.
Asset rotation in Spain and the United States In April, Repsol completed the sale of a stake in its 400 MW wind and solar portfolio in exchange for €114 million.

The portfolio includes eight wind farms, with a total capacity of 300 MW, in the provinces of Huesca, Zaragoza, and Teruel, in northern Spain, as well as two solar plants, with a total capacity of 100 MW, in the province of Palencia.
Repsol retains control of the assets.
As part of the transaction, in December 2024 Repsol signed a long-term syndicated financing agreement for €348 million.

In July, Repsol completed the sale to Stonepeak of a percentage stake in a company that owns a portfolio of renewable assets (777 MW solar and storage, in New Mexico and Texas) in the United States, in exchange for $340 million.
Both companies will jointly control the company.
The operation includes the Frye solar farm and the Jicarilla solar and storage complex.

In December, Repsol reached a second agreement with Stonepeak for the sale of a percentage stake in a company that owns a renewable asset project (629 MW, in Texas) in the United States, in exchange for $252.5 million.
Both companies will jointly control the company.
The transaction will go ahead once regulatory clearance has been obtained (See note 19 of the Consolidated Financial Statements).

Commissioning of projects in Spain and Chile During the period, Repsol began the electricity production phase at the Antofagasta wind farm (with generation activity levels below its capacity, and expected to reach its anticipated generation level in 2026), its largest wind farm to date and one of the largest in Chile.
Located in the commune of Taltal, in the Antofagasta region, it has a total installed capacity of 364 MW.
With an investment of around €400 million, Antofagasta is the first renewable project fully developed by Repsol in Chile.
In 2025, operational solar capacity increased by 304 MW, while wind capacity increased by 393 MW in Spain.
In July, Repsol acquired 805 MW of wind farms from Forestalia with the aim of promoting the largest energy hybridization project in Spain.
Under this project, Repsol’s combined-cycle power plant in Escatrón (804 MW) would be hybridized with the 15 wind farms acquired from Forestalia.
This project aims to make it possible to harness the synergies of both energy sources, stabilize supply, maximize use of the grid connection point, and optimize infrastructure and maintenance.

Technology Repsol Technology Lab, Repsol's R+D center, worked closely with the different businesses on more than 250 projects in 2025, 61% with the aim of lowering emissions.

Key highlights: • 100% renewable gasoline: a technological milestone was achieved by producing, for the first time on an industrial scale, 100% renewable gasoline from organic waste at the Tarragona industrial complex.

• E-Fuels: technological development continues for the production of synthetic fuels at the Bilbao Decarbonization Hub Demonstration Plant, which is scheduled to start in 2026.

Conceptual engineering completed.

National Innovation Award 2024 Repsol has been recognized with the 2024 National Innovation Award by the Spanish Ministry of Science, Innovation and Universities “as the leading exponent of the ground-breaking transformation of innovation in Spain and the world and the undisputed architect of the scale of global change that the energy transition, decarbonization and the development of clean energies have reached” Digitalization Key figures for the digital program More than 1,300 digital initiatives More than 75 generative AI initiatives.
In 2025, Repsol reached new milestones in its digital transformation, having launched more than 1,300 initiatives since the start of the Digital Program (end-2017).
This progress helps to cement the Company’s position as a benchmark in innovation within the energy sector.
The interest in to Generative and Agentic AI is reflected in the Competence Center, which, after two years, has promoted more than 75 initiatives.
Key milestones include the launch of the Multi-Agent Platform, a pioneering initiative worldwide.

Developed in collaboration with Accenture, it already has 34 Agents organized across three Workflows, allowing teams to create work environments where AI agents self-organize, coordinate, and solve tasks autonomously, providing efficiency and new capabilities in key areas of the business.

The impact of these technologies is reflected in the businesses, with Generative AI being applied at Upstream to give just one example, and with initiatives such as Automated Well Data Ingestion, which automates daily drilling and geology reports, integrating data into corporate systems and accelerating decision-making in exploration.

At industrial, notable solutions include Transcribex, allowing for the automatic transcription of audio and video meetings or Email 2.0, which organizes and classifies emails with advanced algorithms, improving documentary efficiency, or Regulatory Management, to automate regulatory analysis with generative AI, improving efficiency, traceability, and regulatory compliance through intelligent assistants and legal bases.
At the Customer area, Energy Buddy centralizes information and tools for the sales force, allowing Repsol to generate personalized simulations and offer tailor-made recommendations for our customers.

Meanwhile, the Transversal Virtual Assistant aims to integrate customer service across all channels (WhatsApp, website, apps, and telephone), resolving queries efficiently and swiftly, centralizing management, and offering a coherent experience for the customer.

In the corporate sphere, we are moving forward with Alex 2.0, which, following the incorporation of Generative AI, is now more interactive and responsive, more effectively satisfying the needs of employees.
Further highlights include RDD2, aimed at third-party due diligence, with the aim of automating and optimizing compliance processes and control in risk management through Generative AI.

Sustainability This chapter provides a summary of the Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information set out in Appendix IV to this document.

This appendix complies with the relevant requirements under the European Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD), and under Spanish Law 11/2018, on non-financial information and diversity (still in force).

6.1 General disclosures Governance Repsol’s commitment to sustainability is reflected in its governance structure, in which the leadership of the Board of Directors and the Executive Committee is accompanied by the oversight function of the Board’s Sustainability and Audit and Control Committees, as well as the coordination and expert advice of the Sustainability Department (SD).

The Sustainability Coordination Committee—set up in 2025 and featuring representatives from the various businesses and corporate areas— supports the operational integration of sustainability throughout the Organization.

Its function is to streamline the identification and management of material impacts, risks and opportunities, facilitating decision-making and improving the alignment and connection between expert areas and businesses.
This governance model allows social, environmental and governance criteria to be integrated into decision-making processes in line with its good governance responsibilities.
To help with the Organization’s alignment with this commitment, the remuneration of Repsol employees is linked to the achievement of sustainability and decarbonization objectives.

In 2025, these objectives were incorporated into those of each business unit, reaching a weighting between 20% and 45%, which, together with other objectives, determine the employee’s variable remuneration.
In the case of the CEO, these targets accounted for 25% of his annual variable remuneration.
In addition, in the Long-Term Incentive (LTI) for those employees entitled to receive it for the 2025–2028 period, 40% of the targets are linked to the energy transition.
This approach allows for sustainability to be part of Repsol’s performance and compensation evaluation criteria.

Repsol has a Code of Ethics and Business Conduct, approved by the Board of Directors, following a favorable report from the Audit and Control Committee, the Sustainability Committee, and the Ethics and Compliance Committee.
This code sets forth the minimum standards of conduct that must guide the behavior of all employees and actors along the Company’s value chain.

Within the framework of this standard, Repsol has fourteen policies, which make up the Company’s regulatory corpus that define public commitment and management fundamentals, establishing principles and guidelines for action for all its employees with the purpose of fostering relationships, processes, and decision-making in line with the Company’s values.

Among the policies related to sustainability, the most notable are those on Sustainability, Environment, People Management, Occupational Health and Safety, Human Rights and Community Relations, Tax, and Privacy and Personal Data Protection.

Identification of impacts, risks and opportunities The double materiality assessment, carried out as required under the CSRD, has made it possible to identify the impacts, risks and opportunities that are material in environmental, social and governance aspects.

The results show a number of positive impacts, most of which are concentrated in the social sphere (own personnel, workers in the value chain and affected groups) and in governance (business conduct).
Most of the negative material impacts are in the field of environment, especially in relation to climate change, water, soil, and air pollution, water management, and biodiversity.
For all of them, the actions the Company is taking to mitigate adverse impacts and enhance positive ones are described throughout this section.

Key risks include those related to climate change mitigation, potential regulatory changes, competition and reduced sales, adoption of new technologies, shortages of raw materials, and decoupling of investment in decarbonization.

Likewise, material risks linked to industrial safety, cybersecurity, and operational continuity are identified, including accidents in facilities, physical or digital attacks, vulnerabilities to cyberattacks, and possible operational interruptions caused by blockages or incidents in the facilities, as well as regulatory non-compliance in areas such as accounting, tax, financial, and sustainability, including safety and environment, artificial intelligence, intellectual property, and other regulations specific to the markets in which Repsol operates.

However, the energy transition also offers opportunities for the Group, such as optimization through energy efficiency, which can increase profitability and competitiveness in the market, or investment in low carbon-intensity energies.

Moreover, access to aid for sustainable financing helps to improve alignment with sustainability objectives and allows entities to raise capital with an ESG risk focus such as Next Generation EU funds, Innovation Fund Large Scale, ICO, etc.
Repsol has defined objectives and taken steps to mitigate risks and seize opportunities, as described in the following sections of this chapter.

Climate change Repsol’s energy transition strategy is aligned with the goal set out in the Paris Agreement to limit global temperature to below 2 °C compared with pre-industrial levels, making every effort not to exceed 1.5 °C by the end of this century.

To succeed in this task, it relies on various enabling levers, with the aim of steadily decarbonizing its own activities (reducing its Scope 1 and 2 emissions), while also helping to decarbonize the value chain, especially for consumers, through the development of a portfolio of low- or zero-carbon fuels that progressively displaces those of mineral origin (thus reducing its Scope 3, category 11 emissions).
Technological evolution and regulation in the markets in which Repsol operates are key and required so that the necessary investments deliver an adequate economic return.
In this context, Repsol has the goal of reaching net-zero greenhouse gas emissions by 2050, in Scopes 1, 2, and 3.

The Company has also set two interim absolute emissions reduction targets for 2030: direct emissions down by 55% compared with 2016, and total emissions down by 20% compared with 2018, considering in Scope 3 those emissions associated with category 11, based on sales of gas and petroleum products.
These emissions currently account for more than 90% of the Company’s total emissions along its value chain (Scopes 1, 2 and 3).

Since the energy transition involves reducing emissions, while also meeting society’s energy demand, the Company also uses an internal Carbon Intensity Indicator (CII) as a complementary metric to assess progress towards decarbonization per unit of energy produced and which allows the effect of the key decarbonization pillars of its strategy, such as the production of electricity and renewable fuels, to be incorporated.
Repsol has set targets for reducing the CII compared with 2016 by 15% in 2025, 25(±1)% by 2030, 55% by 2040, and 100% by 2050.

The alignment of Repsol’s energy transition strategy with the objective of limiting global warming to 1.5 °C above pre-industrial levels can be assessed by reference to the C1 scenarios developed by the IPCC (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change).

These scenarios show a probability greater than 50% of limiting global warming to 1.5 °C in 2100 without exceeding this limit, or with only limited overshoot during the 21st century, and are consistent with global net-zero emissions pathways by 2050.
The median reduction in oil and gas emissions under these scenarios in 2030 is 14% compared with 2019.

Repsol’s target of reducing absolute Scope 1, 2 and 3 emissions by 20% by 2030 compared with 2018 is therefore consistent with the IPCC C1 climate scenarios that limit global warming to 1.5 °C and achieve net-zero emissions by 2050.
(5) The CII reduction target for 2030 has been updated from 28% to 25% ±1 due to the update of the 2026–2028 projections.

Over recent months, and after meeting all its decarbonization targets for 2025, Repsol has reviewed certain metrics and targets from its latest strategic update (now included in this report) in order to adapt them to the new global energy context, prevailing trends in technology, regulations and demand, and the pace of the energy transition.

This has been done without relinquishing its goal to remain on a decarbonization pathway toward net-zero greenhouse gas emissions by 2050 and limiting warming to no more than 1.5 °C by the end of this century, supported by investments that are profitable under these new operating conditions.
Furthermore, the allocation of capital to the Upstream business is consistent with stable production levels in the range of 550–600 kboe/d throughout this decade.

Priority is given, as indicated earlier, to investment in assets and projects commanding a higher economic margin and with lower greenhouse gas emissions, which may allow the Company to reduce its carbon intensity over the coming years, reaching a carbon intensity of 10 kgCO2e/ boe in 2027.
At its industrial businesses (Refining and Chemicals), the Company expects to continue implementing energy efficiency measures, process electrification and increased use of renewable energy.
Pillar 2: Renewable electricity generation Renewable generation is key to Repsol’s energy transition to reduce emissions per unit of energy produced.

Since the start of this activity in 2018, Repsol has built a portfolio of renewable electricity projects (wind, solar and hydroelectric), with 5.8 GW of installed capacity in 2025 and with the goal of exceeding 10 GW17 by 2030, especially in Spain and the United States.

Growth in the coming years has been adjusted to the evolution of the environment, marked by higher development and financing costs, as well as tax incentives in the United States, prioritizing investments based on the profitability thresholds set by Repsol.
The strategy envisages the rotation of the asset portfolio, bringing in partners and securing project financing, to maximize value generation and support better rates of return on net investments.

Pillar 3: Liquid and gaseous renewable fuels The competitiveness of Repsol’s refineries, in the first European quartile by economic margin, facilitates their transformation, aligned with the demand trends promoted by the European regulatory framework on climate matters applies, in line with the European Green Deal and its implementation through the Fit for 55 legislative package and with a focus on the circular economy, with the use of organic waste to produce renewable fuels and the recycling of plastics.

Until 2030, refineries will maintain their activity high due to the resilience shown by demand in Spain and the selective expansion to adjacent markets, while increasing the production of renewable fuels.

Repsol expects to reach production capacity16 by 2030 of 1.6–1.8 Mt of renewable fuels, 0.7–0.8 TWh of biomethane and 0.6–0.8 GWe of renewable hydrogen, marking certain adjustments to the metrics forecast in 2024 in response to prevailing demand and regulatory trends, especially in relation to renewable hydrogen.

Pillar 4: Multi-energy for customers Repsol is moving towards a multi-energy model, focused on the supply of different energy sources to adapt to the needs of its customers and to decarbonization objectives in its markets of Spain and Portugal.
On the Iberian Peninsula, Repsol has developed one of the most extensive 100% renewable fuels networks in Europe, with availability at more than 1,550 service stations in Spain and Portugal.
In Spain, 56% of the network offers multi-energy solutions, also including more than 3,600 public electric vehicle charging points.

The Company also develops the sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) market and already has a 100% renewable fuel production plant at its Cartagena refinery, with a capacity of 250,000 metric tons per year of co- production of renewable road diesel and SAF produced from oily waste.
In the field of residential energy marketing, Repsol has 3 million electricity and gas customers in Spain and Portugal, with the expectation of reaching 5 million by 2030.

Development of carbon sinks Carbon sinks represent a pillar that will not be material for the Company at least until after 2030.
Until then, Repsol’s activity is limited to reducing technical, regulatory, and business risks through its participation in certain projects at an early stage of development.

Repsol’s decarbonization pathway considers the long- term contribution of carbon sinks (carbon capture and storage projects and, eventually, nature-based solutions if required to complete net-zero GHG emissions by 2050).

To carry out all these initiatives, Repsol plans to invest in low‑carbon activities, with the level of investment adjusted to the prevailing macroeconomic environment, technological developments, regulatory changes, project maturity, and progress in asset rotation and planned divestments.
Beyond 2030, Repsol conducts resilience analyses of its long-term decarbonization strategy and economic performance in response to risks arising from the energy transition.

To this end, it develops scenarios, with projections of the main business metrics, under conditions of global demand for various energy sources corresponding to scenarios of organizations specialized in such matters, and demand for energy products in their assets in the Iberian Peninsula consistent with the latest regulations stemming from the European Green Deal.

This resilience analysis carried out on the evolution of the businesses according to the assumptions of the scenarios used, shows that the proposed strategy is resilient to the varying paces of the energy transition.
See Appendix IV, section 2.1 Climate change.

Pollution prevention Repsol has established commitments to prevent and control polluting emissions into the air, water, and soil, minimizing the environmental impact of its operations and products, as set out in the Environmental Policy.
In 2025, a new air emissions target was adopted: to reduce nitrogen oxide (NOx) emissions by 30% by 2035 compared with 2018 in the Refining and Chemicals complexes in the Iberian Peninsula.

With regard to the objective set for 2024 to define action plans within a period of less than 12 months for the management and remediation of soil or groundwater in the event of contamination, it should be noted its compliance in its first year of implementation.

New target to reduce NOx emissions by 30% in 2035 in Refining and Chemicals complexes in the Iberian Peninsula compared with 2018 Repsol continues to implement other actions for environmental protection and operational improvement.

In 2025, progress has been made in the implementation of statistical control of fuel inventories at service stations owned by and operated by third parties, with progressive expansion to new stations.

Redundant equipment has been installed and improvements have been made to the facilities for the control of air emissions, including the adaptation of abatement systems (regenerative thermal oxidation) in chemical plants.
Likewise, improvements were made for the protection of the soil, such as the replacement of chemical product tanks, measures to prevent spills and waterproofing works.
In terms of prevention of spills and discharges into the water, the integrity of the Multibuoy No.

1 Terminal was reinforced at La Pampilla, through the installation of automatic safety valves, subsea manual valves and pile-supported frames on the seabed, with similar actions projected for Terminal No.
See Appendix IV, section 2.2 Pollution prevention.
Water and marine resources Repsol assesses, through its own tool known as the Repsol Water Tool (RWT), the material impacts, risks, and opportunities related to water in the Company’s own operations.
It also relies on the World Resource Institute’s (WRI) Aqueduct Water Risk Atlas to identify physical risks related to water stress, in the short and long term.

The main actions carried out in the assets include initiatives to increase water efficiency and operational improvement; water recycling and reuse; use of alternative sources and water offset measures.

Its implementation contributes to the fulfillment of the commitments established in the Environmental Policy, specifically to the reduction of freshwater withdrawal from the environment, especially in areas at water risk, in the vicinity of the operations.

Net zero freshwater withdrawal from the environment for the Refining and Chemicals facilities in the Iberian Peninsula by 2050 and for the Marcellus asset of the Upstream business by 2035, both compared with 2022 Notable progress was made in 2025 in line with the plan to achieve the established interim milestones compatible with the ambition of reaching net zero freshwater withdrawal of conventional sources from the environment by 2050 for the Refining and Chemicals facilities located on the Iberian Peninsula, and by 2035 for the Marcellus Upstream asset in the United States.

In 2025, the indicator for freshwater withdrawal of conventional sources from the environment amounted to 39.7 Hm³ for Refining and Chemicals on the Iberian Peninsula and 0.27 Hm³ for Marcellus, representing reductions of 10.4% and 84.1%18, respectively, compared with the 2022 baseline value.

Biodiversity and ecosystems Together with the double materiality assessment, Repsol has identified the Company’s sites with a material impact on biodiversity and ecosystems following TNFD’s LEAP approach.

Those that affect sensitive areas, in terms of biodiversity, have also been identified, as they are located in or near them, For more information, see Appendix IV, section 2.4 Biodiversity and ecosystems.
At these sites, actions have been implemented for ecosystem restoration and reforestation, to promote knowledge of biodiversity and ecosystems, and to provide compensation measures.

Through these actions, the Company contributes to the achievement of the commitments set out in the Environmental Policy, in particular the protection and conservation of biodiversity, ecosystems, and associated ecosystem services, as well as the management of impacts on and dependencies on them.
To improve the conservation status of endangered species 2.

To develop biodiversity action plans in biodiversity-sensitive areas or protected areas In 2025, Repsol voluntarily established two measurable objectives related to biodiversity and ecosystems to address their impacts, risks, and material opportunities, aligned with the commitments established in the Environmental Policy: • Improve the conservation status of at least five threatened species by 2030 through the financing, implementation and monitoring of projects.
• Have biodiversity action plans or equivalent surveillance, monitoring and control plans for tangible operated assets that are located in biodiversity- sensitive areas or protected areas.

Use of resources and circular economy Repsol has adopted a series of commitments through its Environmental Policy to integrate the principles of the circular economy into its processes, products, and services, which include optimizing the use of resources and raw materials, as well as promoting the use of alternative and renewable resources obtained in a sustainable way.

Own employees Repsol has 24,679 employees with the capabilities needed to address the energy transition processes and the transformation of its business models.
People management is a pillar that contributes to this process, acting as a lever to drive innovation, competitiveness, and the aforementioned business transformation.

In this context, the Organization is focused on the development and well-being of its employees by aligning people management policies with the corporate strategy and promoting a positive, safe, and inclusive work environment based on respect for human and labor rights, as well as on social dialogue and cooperation with employee representatives.
To this end, Repsol has policies such as the People Management Policy and the Occupational Health and Safety Policy, among others, which enshrine these commitments.

Repsol’s Global Diversity and Inclusion Framework establishes the principles and commitments related to equal treatment and opportunities, diversity, equity, and non-discrimination in line with the People Policy.

Based on this framework, objectives and initiatives have been defined to promote work environments that comply with applicable regulations and encourage the participation of people with diverse profiles, ensuring responsible diversity management.
Some examples of these initiatives are the internal Winer Network and campaigns promoting respectful and inclusive environments for the LGTBI+ community.

In line with the policy, Repsol is committed to attracting, developing, and hiring young talent, promoting professional opportunities from the early stages of training and professional development through structured Dual Vocational Training programs, academic internships, and Graduate programs.
This commitment is part of a comprehensive talent management approach aimed at offering fair working conditions and strengthening employability throughout a professional career.

In this regard, Repsol promotes the attraction, retention, and development of talent through a value proposition that considers not only compensation, but also benefits, well-being, safety, flexibility, and work–life balance.
At the same time, it strengthens employability through specific training and continuing education plans that supports professional development and adaptation to future challenges.

Since 2024, Repsol has rolled out the Company’s talent program as a strategic lever to anticipate future needs, attract and retain critical profiles, foster continuous learning, evolve leadership, and strengthen organizational culture.
In 2025, Repsol expanded the training offering of Repsol University with new courses to support the professional development of its employees, expanding and consolidating 54 areas of knowledge.

Repsol has set two new targets: consolidate an attractive value proposition for employees and improve the employability of the workforce through the training offer See Appendix IV, section 3.1 Own workforce.

Workers in the value chain Repsol’s value chain includes suppliers, partners, distributors, and customers, marketing a wide range of products in more than 90 countries, covering activities such as Upstream oil and gas, electricity production and sales, refining, chemicals, and product marketing.
Repsol manages its value chain in response to both stakeholder expectations and compliance with international ethical, labor, environmental, safety, and human rights standards.
Protecting the rights of workers along the value chain is a key part of these efforts.

Among the corporate policies, the Code of Ethics and Business Conduct establishes the minimum principles that the Company expects from its suppliers, promoting their knowledge and acceptance before starting a contractual relationship and their compliance throughout the business relationship.
Repsol has a supply chain risk management model to identify, assess, and minimize environmental, social, and governance risks at different stages of the procurement and contracting process.

This model consists of a due diligence phase to understand and assess the third parties it engages with, and establishes environmental, social, and governance requirements in the purchasing and contracting process (including specific clauses tailored to the nature of the purchase or service) and a supplier performance evaluation process, as well as audit and control mechanisms for the effective implementation of the entire process.

≡ Summary of the year 2025 Our Company Environment Financial performance and shareholder return Performance of our businesses Sustainability Outlook Appendices As far as partners are concerned, Repsol conducts a complete due diligence process in each mergers and acquisitions (M&A) transaction.
During this process, environmental, social and governance aspects are evaluated, among others.
If any such risks are identified, Repsol is able to introduce corrective or commitment clauses in the contract to achieve certain standards.
In some specific cases, a contractual follow-up is included to verify this commitment after the contract is signed.
The Company endeavors to include local companies among its network of suppliers and contractors, thus promoting economic and social development in the areas where it operates.
In 2025, 82% of the volume of purchases was made from local suppliers.
Repsol also promotes social inclusion and sustainability in its supply chain by hiring special employment centers and training in ESG aspects for its suppliers.

Repsol has defined objectives for the coming years with the aim of strengthening good sustainability practices along the supply chain, while promoting the inclusion of safety and environmental criteria in the supplier management process and in all contracts for goods and services carrying a high safety and environmental risk.
See Appendix IV, section 3.2 Workers in the value chain.

Affected groups Repsol incorporates respect for human rights as a core principle of its Human Rights and Community Relations Policy, which defines its actions in all the countries where it operates and along its value chain, and recognizes its responsibility to prevent, mitigate, and remediate the possible negative impacts of its activities on communities that may be affected.

During the year, the objective of developing an action plan to comply with the EU Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive was redefined, extending the scope of due diligence processes to encompass a greater number of activities, including those considered low risk.

Implementing an Action Plan 2025-2028 to strengthen due diligence processes The communities in the area of influence of the Company’s activities constitute one of the main stakeholders with whom Repsol maintains solid relationships through a proactive and transparent dialogue, seeking shared benefit and contribution to local, socioeconomic and environmental development.
Before starting any new activity, a social baseline study is conducted to understand the context and specific circumstances and needs of the local community.
Notably, indigenous communities are considered to be especially vulnerable to the Company’s activities.

Repsol respects and values the uniqueness of these communities and their rights to lands and natural resources and applies Convention 169 of the International Labour Organization (ILO) in the relationship with these communities, whether or not it is incorporated into the legislation of each country.

The Company conducts social impact assessments and implements action plans through participatory and consultative processes, while also enabling grievance mechanisms, speak-up and dispute resolution channels.
It also commits to remediate any impacts that may occur as a result of its activities.

With the aim of generating a positive impact on the environment, in addition to promoting local hiring and economic development through the acquisition of local goods and services, Repsol implements social investment projects that benefit the communities in areas of influence of its activity, as well as society as a whole.
Repsol aims to increase to 50% the average voluntary social investment associated with just transition in the 2026-2030 period See Appendix IV, section 3.3 Affected communities.

Consumers and end customers For Repsol, the people-centered multi-energy strategy is a key element in promoting the energy transition and decarbonization, integrating criteria of efficiency, profitability, and sustainability.

In 2025, the Company renewed its brand identity, unified protocols across all customer service channels, and carried out verifiable actions that provide a transparent and functional customer experience.

≡ Summary of the year 2025 Our Company Environment Financial performance and shareholder return Performance of our businesses Sustainability Outlook Appendices Customer service is key to strengthening trust and materializing the multi-energy strategy.
Repsol offers support through telephone and digital channels, relying on a voice of the customer platform that allows Repsol to measure customer satisfaction and apply continuous improvements.
In 2025 alone, more than 4.3 million calls were handled in Spain and Portugal, with a service level of 89% and an average waiting time of 116 seconds.

Furthermore, efforts were made to simplify communication, through the use of clearer and more relevant messages that consider the customer’s perspective, an evolution of customer service protocols and guides and a clearer and simpler transition to digital channels.

The digitalization process implemented by Repsol is a core pillar for optimizing the customer experience, combining quantitative and qualitative analysis, continuous research, and advanced systems such as the voice of the customer program and multi-energy tracking, which monitor preferences, behavior, and perception.

With regard to the safety of the products sold by Repsol, and in compliance with the European REACH and CLP regulations, products intended for end users have a 24-hour emergency telephone number on their labeling, intended to manage security incidents.
In 2025, 18 calls to the emergency telephone number have been registered, of which one related to a medium-severity incident and 17 related to incidents of no severity.
No calls were made regarding incidents of low or high severity.
See Appendix IV, section 3.4 Consumers and end customers.

Safety In 2025, Repsol took further positive action to improve its safety performance, in response to the objectives set out in its Safety and Occupational Health Policy to improve the safety of its operations and protect the physical integrity of its employees and contractors.

Repsol is working towards the launch in 2026 of the Life-Saving Rules campaign by IOGP to improve levels of prevention in critical activities Repsol has reinforced its strategy for preventing serious accidents and reducing fatalities through the Safety Excellence Program.

Among the main measures adopted, the progressive abandonment of the now obsolete "zero accidents" concept, the elimination of targets based on the total frequency rate and its replacement by indicators linked to fatalities and serious or permanent injuries, as well as the updating of the Company’s risk matrix to contemplate low-probability and high-impact scenarios.

Moreover, greater awareness has been achieved regarding the reporting of high- potential incidents, and the quality of investigations has improved through the incorporation of systemic root- cause analysis and human factors.
The Life-Saving Rules campaign by IOGP is also being rolled out to strengthen prevention in critical activities.

In parallel, the Company continues to carry out specific diagnoses of the safety culture in its assets and businesses every four or five years, with independent assessments that allow the identification of areas for improvement not detected in traditional audits.

These diagnoses are considered key to preventing major accidents and were applied in 2025 in the Venezuela business unit, in the Mobility Division of Spain and Portugal, and in the industrial complexes of Cartagena (Spain) and La Pampilla (Peru).
The earnings obtained facilitate the development of action plans adapted to the cultural reality of each business unit.
In relation to safety and security for people and facilities, Repsol has reviewed and updated its plans across all countries.
These plans include risk mitigation measures and are based on ongoing analysis of the security situation at each site.
The Company has worked in collaboration with its partners in the assets and, where necessary, with local security forces for the protection of its assets, operations, and people.
See Appendix IV, section 3.5 Safety and security.

6.4 Governance information 6.4.1. Business conduct Repsol believes that ethical behavior and responsible practices are essential elements for the sustainable development of its activity and for the generation of long-term value for its shareholders and stakeholders.

Code of Ethics and Business Conduct Repsol’s Code of Ethics and Business Conduct applies to all directors, managers and employees, regardless of the type of contract that determines their professional or employment relationship, and establishes the framework of reference for understanding and implementing the behaviors and expectations that the Company has for each of them.

Repsol’s commercial partners are an extension of the Company itself and, for this reason, must act in accordance with the code and any applicable contractual provisions when working on behalf or in collaboration with the Company.
In 2025, a total of 22,126 employees completed the new annual mandatory online training on the Code of Ethics and Business Conduct.

Ethics and Compliance Committee and Channel The Ethics and Compliance Committee is a collegiate, high-level and multidisciplinary body whose remit includes managing the system for monitoring and ensuring compliance with the Company’s Code of Ethics and Business Conduct.
In going about this task, it has standalone authority to take action where it sees fit and oversees compliance.

≡ Summary of the year 2025 Our Company Environment Financial performance and shareholder return Performance of our businesses Sustainability Outlook Appendices The Committee makes the Ethics and Compliance Channel available to employees and any third party who has something to report or share.

This channel is an easy-to-access, confidential and, if desired and permitted by applicable legislation, anonymous medium through which suspected illegal or unethical behavior or potential non-compliance with the Code of Ethics and Business Conduct, internal regulations or applicable legislation can be reported.
In 2025, a total of 311 communications were received through the Ethics and Compliance Channel, of which 75 were investigated at the initiative of the Committee.

Comprehensive Compliance Model The Compliance function has a framework for action and a specialized team that helps to embed a culture of compliance and improve the identification and monitoring of ethics and compliance risks, especially on matters of integrity, money laundering and terrorist financing, crime prevention, international sanctions and embargoes, competition, and data privacy and protection.
Ethical culture, communication, and awareness are essential to strengthen Repsol’s Compliance Model.
In this sense, communication and dissemination actions are carried out on internal regulations related to ethics and compliance, facilitating employees’ understanding and knowledge.
More than 360 employees are part of the People Compliance network, who act as compliance ambassadors and promote the ethical culture within the Company See Appendix IV, section 4.1 Business conduct.

The resilience of the global economy has been greater than expected, but its future strength will depend on restoring a predictable trade framework, strengthening public finances, and sustaining investment in innovation and productivity.

Energy sector outlook In its latest monthly report for February 2026, Oil Market Report, the International Energy Agency (IEA) estimates an increase in global oil demand in 2026 of 0.85 mbl/d, to 104.9 mbl/d.
The set of non-OECD economies would account for the total growth estimated for this year, even offsetting the marginal decline expected in the OECD region.
After European OECD countries maintained their consumption in 2025 compared with the previous year, the IEA estimates a drop of just 70 kbl/d in 2026.
The IEA notes that Chinese demand looks set to grow by 220 kbl/d in 2026, having increased its consumption by just 210 kbl/d in 2025.
In fact, the stagnation in Chinese oil demand growth is one of the reasons leading the IEA to project an oversupplied market in 2026.

The other reason for this scenario is the health of non-OPEC supply, which in 2026 is expected to grow by 1.17 mbl/ d, an increase similar to that expected for OPEC crude oil and natural gas liquids (+1.22 mbl/d), placing global supply at 108.6 mbl/d, which would leave the global balance at an oversupply of around 3.68 mbl/d on average in 2026, the highest level in the agency's historical records (going back to 1986).
Global supply/demand balance in 2026 7.

Outlook 7.1 Outlook for the sector Macroeconomic outlook According to the IMF's latest projections (WEO October 2025), the global economy has continued to show remarkable resilience despite rising trade and geopolitical tensions.

After growing by 3.3% in 2024, global GDP is expected to moderate to 3.2% in 2025 and 3.1% in 2026, just two tenths below the previous year's forecasts and well above the estimates made after the announcement of the new US tariffs.

The impact of these measures has been more limited than expected, thanks to the extensive exemptions applied by Washington, the rapid reconfiguration of supply chains and the boost in private investment, especially in artificial intelligence and energy.
Even so, the IMF warns that considering the tariff episode closed would be premature.
The effective rate of tariffs in the US remains high and part of the costs could be gradually passed on to the consumer.
In addition, the cyclical boost from AI and public spending on defense and energy transition could sustain inflationary pressures for longer than expected.

In this context, the IMF forecasts a sharper slowdown in the second half of 2025, with growth of 1.6% among advanced economies, 2.0% in the United States, and 1.2% in the euro area, while emerging economies would grow by 4.2%.
Global inflation would continue to ease, albeit more gradually and with notable regional divergences.
After reaching 5.7% in 2023 and slowing to 4.6% in 2024, the IMF projects 4.2% in 2025 and 3.7% in 2026.
In most advanced economies, headline inflation would converge around the 2% target, while in emerging markets it would remain slightly higher due to currency depreciation and higher food prices.

≡ Summary of the year 2025 Our Company Environment Financial performance and shareholder return Performance of our businesses Sustainability Outlook Appendices In the longer term, the IEA notes in its 2025 World Energy Outlook document that geopolitical tensions, climate risks and the vulnerability of the energy system are placing energy at the center of economic and national security.
It also states that, despite global fragility, oil prices remain contained due to a large supply surplus.
Countries are seeking to strengthen their energy security in different ways: While some are focused on renewables and efficiency, others are prioritizing an abundant supply of traditional fuels.
At the same time, energy trade is becoming even more important in a context of geopolitical fractures and high uncertainty about international trade.
The IEA notes that global energy demand continues to grow strongly.
In 2024, both renewable energies and the consumption of oil, gas, coal and nuclear production reached record levels.
China has especially boosted the use of coal since 2019, contributing to the continued rise in emissions.

Thus, according to the scenarios published in the WEO 2025 by the IEA, under the STEPS baseline scenario, total global primary energy demand increases from around 15,623 million tons of oil equivalent (Mtoe) in 2024 to 17,839 Mtoe in 2050.
This corresponds to an average annual growth rate of 0.5%, just under half the growth rate of energy demand over the past decade.
Demand will continue to increase from 2024 to 2050, with 24% growth in emerging economies more than offsetting the 7% decline among advanced economies.
Under the STEPS scenario, demand for each of the major fossil fuels—coal, oil and natural gas—would peak before 2035, retreating from that point forward.
Accordingly, in 2035 oil demand would stand at 100.4 mbl/d, while natural gas demand would reach 4,770 billion cubic meters (bcm).
All renewable energy sources increase their share over time.

At present, modern bioenergy (renewable fuels, liquid biofuels and solid bioenergy, excluding the traditional use of biomass) accounts for more than half of renewable energy demand, though solar photovoltaic and wind energy will expand rapidly over the coming years, especially in the electricity sector.
Installed capacity across all renewable energy sources will more than double under the STEPS scenario.
STEPS forecasts a peak in energy-related CO2 emissions before 2035, and they would fall from 38.15 Gt CO2 in 2024 to 29.63 Gt CO2 in 2050.
This would bring the global average temperature increase to approximately 2.4 °C by 2100.
In this edition, the IEA reintroduces the Current Policies Scenario (CPS) and abandons the Announced Pledges Scenario (APS).

In the CPS, which considers only the policies that have been adopted in legislation and actually implemented so far, global energy demand would reach 20,012 Mtoe by 2050, almost 15% above the level reached under the STEPS scenario.
In this scenario, oil demand would not fall throughout the analysis period, reaching 112.8 mbl/d by 2050.
The same is true of natural gas, which would reach 5,596 bcm.

In tandem with this, work will continue on improving the efficiency of assets and on an investment program aimed at optimizing the use of capital, with a disciplined and selective approach.

In this context, Repsol has announced that it will hold a Capital Market Day in March 2026, at which it will present an update of its strategic projections for the 2026–2028 period, in light of developments in the macroeconomic, regulatory, and competitive environment.
This will steer the performance of our businesses, with a focus on enhancing competitiveness and efficiency through transformational initiatives that support sustainable cash generation over time..
In 2026, the Company's transformation and investment effort in all businesses may continue, provided that macroeconomic and commercial conditions are conducive to this.

The allocation of capital will be carried out rigorously, prioritizing the most profitable and transformative projects in the portfolio, with the aim of making solid progress on the transformation path toward decarbonization.
In the Customer area, efforts will aim to focus on improving profitability and cash generation, leveraged on a multi-energy proposal that adds value to the customer.

Repsol will aim to promote growth in electricity and natural gas customers, as well as the development of multi-energy and low-emission platforms , while promoting new businesses and the traditional business.

Within this framework, our points of sale will continue to be transformed and the distribution and marketing of renewable fuels will continue to be promoted, as a sustainable solution to face the challenge of decarbonization of transport.
In addition, we will accompany our customers in their energy transition, offering solutions that balance competitiveness, service and sustainability.

The Industrial businesses will aim to continue to drive transformation and the circular economy, through the development of initiatives aimed at increasing the capacity to produce renewable fuels and low-carbon materials, always promoting a culture of safety.

Among the vectors of progress, the start-up in 2026 of the advanced biofuels plant in Puertollano, which will produce 100% renewable diesel from waste, and the e- fuels plant in Bilbao, aimed at manufacturing synthetic fuels using green hydrogen and captured CO₂, stand out.
In addition, the expansion of the Sines petrochemical complex in Portugal will strengthen the production of polymeric materials.

On the other hand, after the approval of the projects in 2025, the construction of the Ecoplant in Tarragona, a pioneer in Europe in converting urban waste into renewable methanol through gasification, and the electrolyzer in Cartagena, which will allow the production of renewable hydrogen, will begin.

All this will be complemented by the consolidation of the production of the renewable fuels plant in Cartagena, operational since 2024, and with electrification and energy efficiency projects to decarbonize industrial processes.
The Low Carbon Generation business will continue to play a crucial role, with the organic development of a portfolio of solar and wind projects.
The new generation capacity will be especially focused on Spain and the United States.

Excellent project execution, together with the optimization of the financial structure and systematic rotation of the asset portfolio, will maximize the profitability of new developments and strengthen the growth profile of the business.

Alternative Performance Measures Repsol's financial information contains amounts and measures prepared in accordance with applicable accounting standards, as well as other measures prepared in accordance with the Group's reporting model known as Alternative Performance Measures (APMs).

The APMs are considered “adjusted” figures with respect to those presented in accordance with IFRS-EU or with the Information on oil and gas exploration and production activities, and, therefore, should be considered by the reader as complementary, rather than as substitutes for them.

APMs are highly useful for users of financial information as they are the measures employed by Repsol's Management to evaluate its financial performance, cash flows, or its financial position when making operational or strategic decisions for the Group.
For quarterly historical information on the APMs, see www.repsol.com.

Repsol’s business segment reporting model In the fourth quarter of 2025, in the context of the growing importance of partner participation in the E&P and GBC businesses and the expansion of the joint business model, the Group has changed the way it manages and evaluates its segments and has therefore modified the financial and performance measures that are reviewed internally for decision-making.

Specifically, the results and financial figures of the joint ventures are now integrated by the equity method22 into its segment results presentation model, and the measurement of segment results is modified to reflect the result attributable to the parent company.

In this way, in addition, the Group's financial information is provided with greater clarity, aligning it with the presentation of the financial statements prepared under IFRS-EU and with common practice in the sector.

Additionally, and in line with the change implemented in the last quarter of 2025, the 2026 budget, the update of the projections to be presented at the Capital Markets Day following the publication of the annual results in February, and the update of employee remuneration targets and plans have all been prepared in accordance with the new reporting model.
The information reported on segments corresponding to previous periods has been modified to reflect these changes to allow adequate comparability between periods.

The result metric for each segment is known as Adjusted Net Income, which contains the income from continuing operations at current cost of supply (CCS), net of taxes, the result of investments accounted for by the equity method (Profit from investees) and the result attributed to minority interests (Non-controlling interests) and excluding certain income and expenses (“Special items”) described below.
The financial result of the consolidated companies and the inter-segment consolidation adjustments are assigned to the Corporation and other results.
More precisely, the operating results at CCS considers the cost of volumes sold to be that corresponding to the costs of Supplies and production for the same period.

It is the criterion commonly used in the sector to present the earnings of the businesses of the Industrial or Customer segments, which must work with significant inventories subject to constant price fluctuation.
This facilitates comparability with other companies and the monitoring of the businesses, regardless of the impact of price variations on their inventories.
However, for accounting purposes, in accordance with EU-IFRS, the weighted average cost method is used.
The Inventory effect reflects the difference between the the results at CCS and the result at weighted average cost.
It is presented independently, net of taxes and minority interests.
Special items includes certain material items whose separate presentation is considered appropriate in order to facilitate analysis of the ordinary business performance.

This includes gains/losses on disposals, restructuring costs, impairments (provisions/reversions) of assets, provisions for risks and expenses and other relevant income/expenses outside the ordinary management of the businesses.
These results, which include those relating to investments accounted for using the equity method, are presented separately, net of taxes and non-controlling interests.
For more information, see Note 4 “Business segments” to the 2025 consolidated Financial Statements.

Financial performance metrics Adjusted net income Adjusted net income is the primary measure of financial performance that the Chief Operating Decision Maker (CODM) reviews when making decisions.23 It is a useful APM as it allows investors to evaluate the performance of the operating segments and allow better comparability with other companies operating in our sector.

It contains income from continuing operations at current cost of supply (CCS) and net of taxes; earnings corresponding to investments accounted for using the equity method (Earnings of investees), and those attributable to non-controlling interests (Non- controlling interests), and do not include certain income and expenses (Special items) described below.
The financial result of the consolidated companies and inter-segment consolidation adjustments are assigned to Corporation and other results.
The adjustments included between the Adjusted net income and profit attributable to the parent are therefore as follows: • Inventory effect.
In profit at CCS, the cost of volumes sold is determined according to the supply and production24 costs for the same period.
and is presented net of taxes, excluding the effect of Non-controlling interests, which are not attributable to the parent company.
This effect mainly affects the Industrial segment.

These correspond to significant items whose separate presentation is considered convenient to facilitate the monitoring of the day-to-day management of business operations and allow for a reliable comparison between financial years and companies operating within the sector.
It includes capital gains/losses due to divestments (capital gains and losses due to transfers or disposals of assets), restructuring costs (severance costs, etc.
), impairment (provisions and reversals resulting from the impairment test on fixed assets, tax credits, etc.

and other relevant income/ expenses unrelated to the day-to-day running of business (provisions for dismantling and remediation; exchange rate impacts on tax positions in foreign currency; costs and indemnities arising from claims; penalties and fines; valuation of derivative financial instruments due to accounting asymmetries, etc.).

Specific results, which include those pertaining to investments accounted for using the equity method, are presented net of taxes, excluding the effect of non-controlling interests, which are not attributable to the parent company.
