Risks Repsol’s Enterprise Risk Management System - ERMS Repsol has an Enterprise Risk Management System which, through the coordinated action of all the units involved, enables the main risks associated with the Group’s activities to be identified, measured, managed and monitored in a manner consistent with the Risk Management Policy, and monitors whether the existing management systems effectively mitigate them to the established levels.

Repsol’s Enterprise Risk Management System provides a forward-looking, comprehensive and reliable view of all the risks that may affect the Company and is based on the Risk Management Policy approved by the Board of Directors, the principles of which are set out in the Integrated Risk Management standard approved by the Executive Committee.
The core pillars of the ERMS are: • Senior Management heads up the enterprise risk management process.
• The risk vision is integrated into the management and decision-making processes.

• Businesses and corporate areas participate, with different levels of responsibility and specialization (risk management units, supervisory units, and auditing units, in accordance with the Three-Line Model29), while the Risk and Control Assurance Unit coordinates and governs the system.
• Risks are identified, assessed, and addressed in accordance with ISO 31000.
• It promotes continuous improvement to gain efficiency and responsiveness.
Another key element is the risk tolerance declaration, which is set out in the Risk Management Policy along with the above principles.

Repsol aspires to a profile typical of an energy company, integrated and diversified, differentiating between those in which the Company will actively manage and maintain within the defined tolerance thresholds, and those for which it declares its high ambition for control, minimizing its probability of occurrence and its impact, including reputational impact.
The latter include accident rate, environment, health, safety, ethics, and conduct and compliance (including tax) risks.

ISO 31000 Risk Management – Principles and guidelines 29The Three Lines model was developed by the Institute of Internal Auditors (IIA) in 2020 with the aim of helping organizations to identify the structures and processes that best facilitate the achievement of objectives by promoting robust risk management governance.

Audit and Control Committee Directly oversees the activity of the Audit, Control and Risk Department, to which the Risk and Control Assurance Unit is attached, while also periodically reviewing the effectiveness of the internal control systems, Internal Audit and Risk Management systems so that the main risks are properly identified, managed, and disclosed.

Sustainability Committee The Sustainability Committee attached to Repsol's Board of Directors supervises and advises the Board of Directors on corporate social responsibility, the environment, health and safety, climate change, and energy transition.

Executive Committee Approves the necessary governance elements in the field of risk management, monitors their correct application and tracks the Company's performance in terms of risks, including the integration of the risk vision into decision-making processes.
Risk Management Units Risk Supervision Units Risk Audit Units Responsible for direct risk management in daily operations (identification, analysis, evaluation and treatment of risks).

Evaluate the design and operation of risk management and control systems so that they are adequately identified, measured, prioritized and controlled, in accordance with the Risk Management Policy and other current regulations and good industry practices.

Supervisory Units: Supervise risk management in their field of expertise, i.e., they define the management and governance model, advise the front lines on its application, monitor its performance and support compliance with the criteria of the established model.

Risk and Control Assurance Area: It defines the model for planning, identification, valuation and treatment of the ERMS, advises on the application of this model, monitors the entire process (which includes reporting to the governance bodies) and supports compliance, alerting the management and supervisory units of any deviation from the Risk Management Policy as identified.
It also promotes that the vision of risks effectively influences decision- making processes.

A I II Conversion table and abbreviations Alternative performance measures III Risks IV V VI ≡ Information Statement Non-Financial Consolidated and Sustainability Information Annual Corporate Governance Report Annual Report on Director Remuneration According to the ERMS, the identification, assessment and consolidation of risks is carried out at least annually.

However, the processes are prepared such that if at any time it appears that the Group’s risk profile may have been substantially altered due to a change in the exposure to any particular material risk, the analysis of such risks is updated.

Where appropriate, the head of the management unit sets in motion appropriate actions or control mechanisms to align the risk profile with the organization’s expectations, in line with the risk tolerance declaration set out in the Risk Management Policy.

In this way, the company reasonably supports the compliance of the objectives of each organizational area that could be impacted by risks of a strategic, operational, financial, regulatory and legal nature, relying on information and internal control systems based on the principles of the COSO Reference Framework30.

The Risk and Control Assurance unit consolidates the individual risk maps to obtain the consolidated Risk Map of the Repsol Group, as well as any partial consolidation that is agreed to report to the executive and governing bodies, which issue, as appropriate, guidelines regarding the treatment of certain risks, in view of the risk profile, the maturity of its management systems and the declaration of risk tolerance contained in the Risk Management Policy.
The resulting mitigation actions are driven forward by the supervision units and, when they involve management units, converge with the treatment strategies that the latter have in place.
Supervisory units include technical and specialist functions in business and corporate areas.

In the course of the activities described above, the Risk and Internal Controls Assurance unit collects from the managing and supervisory units information on their performance and expectations in relation to the activities carried out to achieve the objectives of the ERMS.
This collection of information is complemented, when deemed necessary, with campaigns specifically designed for the collection of certain data, whether surveys, studies of backtesting or any others.
Based on this information, the area responsible for the ERMS reviews its effectiveness and so that the findings result in its continuous improvement.

At all stages of the integrated risk management process, and in accordance with the scope defined for the management system, audit units are responsible for assessing the reasonableness and adequacy of the design and operation of the management systems.

control and management of the Repsol Group’s risks with the aim, among others, so that that risks are adequately identified, prioritized, measured and controlled, within the tolerance levels established by the Board of Directors in the Risk Management Policy, with reference to current regulations and good industry practices.
As part of their functions, the audit unit plans its annual engagements based on various aspects, including the state of risks, preferably focusing their actions on the most significant ones.

Lastly, it should be noted that the variables of the economic models on which the key decision-making processes are based, such as the preparation of the annual budget and the preparation and periodic updating of the strategic plan, are subject to risk analysis.

Going beyond single-scenario approaches, these analyses provide a probabilistic view of the achievable result by simulating multiple scenarios in which these variables, which may be prices or margins and may be loosely or more heavily correlated, take different values depending on their prior statistical modeling.

System of Internal Control over Financial Reporting (ICFR) and Sustainability Reporting (ICSR) Repsol has a System of Internal Control over Financial Reporting (ICFR) whose proper functioning provides reasonable assurance as to the reliability of the Group’s financial reporting.

The ICFR model is defined on the basis of the methodological framework set out in COSO 2013, and its objective is to contribute to supporting that the transactions carried out are faithfully recorded, in accordance with the corresponding accounting framework, providing reasonable assurance in relation to the prevention or detection of errors that could have a significant impact on the information contained in the Consolidated Financial Statements.
The Audit, Control and Risks department annually evaluates the design and functioning of the Group’s ICFR and draws conclusions on its effectiveness.
Additional information can be found under section B 8.2.
Internal Control and Risk Management Systems in relation to the financial information issuance process (ICFR) of the Annual Corporate Governance Report.

The Repsol Group has established a system of Internal Control over Sustainability Reporting (“ICSR”), designed to provide reasonable assurance regarding the reliability of the sustainability information reported in accordance with the applicable regulatory framework, CNMV regulations, the Spanish Companies Act, and the Code of Good Governance of Listed Companies issued by the of the Spanish National Securities Market Commission (see details in Appendix IV, section 1.2 “Governance”).
30 COSO Internal Control – Integrated Framework, 2013.

Repsol has an Integrated Internal Control model, inspired by the Committee of Sponsoring Organizations of the Treadway Commission (COSO) reference framework, which includes, among others, the Group's formally developed Internal Control and Compliance Systems, including the Financial Reporting Internal Control System, the Sustainability Information Control System, the Crime Prevention Model and other compliance and criminal prevention models in force.
The price of CO2 emission allowances may be affected by the macroeconomic and regulatory environment, as well as the emission reduction scenarios associated with the energy transition process.
Note that price deviations from the Group’s projected figures may be favorable or unfavorable.
In 2025, the price of a barrel of Brent averaged $ 69.1 /bbl, lower than that of the previous year (80.8 $/ bbl).
However, it was marked by high volatility due to geopolitical factors as well as OPEC+'s production strategy.
The price of U.S. Henry Hub natural gas averaged 3.43 €/MBtu, higher than that of the previous period (2024: 2.27 $/MBtu).

In the case of electricity, the wholesale price averaged 66 €/MWh, compared with 63 €/MWh in 2024 , influenced by growth in demand that is offset by the increase in self- consumption and photovoltaic generation.
The price of CO2 emission allowances on the ETS1 market averaged 74.9 €/t compared with 66.5 €/t in 2024, mainly due to the increase in the price of gas in Europe.

The reduction in crude oil and gas prices has a negative impact on the profitability of the Exploration and Production activity, the valuation of its assets, the ability to generate cash and investment plans.
Rising prices have the opposite effect.
A potential drop in investment could adversely affect Repsol’s ability to replace its crude oil and gas reserves.
In addition, changes in the prices of crude oil and its derivatives may affect the margin of the Refining and Chemical businesses and the value of the inventories stored in the Industrial segment.
In 2025, the impact of price fluctuations on inventories is reflected in the so-called Inventory effect (see section 4 of this document).
The price of finished products can also affect demand for them.

The price of gas and electricity has a fundamental impact on the low-carbon generation business and on the electricity and gas marketing businesses and the price of CO2 emission allowances in the ETS1 market on the margins of industrial businesses.
Likewise, the price of CO2 in the future ETS2 market may affect the margins and sales of commercial businesses.

For more information on the impact of changes (positive and negative) on the calculation of the recoverable value of the assets from the impairment test (see Note 6.1 of the 2025 consolidated Financial Statements.
For more information on the trend in the price of hydrocarbons, derivative products, and other commodities in 2025, see section 3.2 of this report.
For more information on the effect of prices on the earnings and performance of the business, see sections 4 and 5 of this document.

Strategic risks Deviations from expected results in investment/divestment processes The Repsol Group is exposed to negative impacts on the management of investment, divestment or partnership processes that may be caused, among others, by inadequate capture and selection of alternatives or an incorrect analysis of each of them.

The current context is marked by high volatility and uncertainty, which can influence the number and quality of the opportunities available or lead to an inadequate estimation of the evolution of the main variables of each initiative, such as demand, commodity prices, margin forecasts, exchange rates and interest rates, the efficiency of production factors, operating costs, etc.

This heading includes risks related to the management of the asset portfolio in accordance with Repsol's strategic lines, to the management of the portfolio of new and existing asset development projects, to the uncertainty associated with the commercial scaling of projects based on emerging technologies, as well as risks related to inadequate forecasting of fiscal conditions or the appearance of contingencies in the purchase/sale processes of assets or companies.

Repsol’s industrial and commercial assets (refineries, petrochemical complexes, regasification plants, electric power generation plants (cogeneration, combined cycles, hydroelectric plants, wind or photovoltaic installations), bases and warehouses, port facilities, pipelines, ships, tankers, service stations, etc.
), as well as the facilities of Repsol's Upstream business (exploratory or production wells, surface facilities, oil platforms, etc.
), both onshore and offshore, are exposed to the risk of accidents, such as fires, explosions, leaks of toxic products, as well as environmental incidents with a potentially significant impact.

Such accidents may cause death and injury to employees, contractors, residents in surrounding areas, as well as damage to the assets and property owned by Repsol and third parties, as well as damage to the environment.
These accidents can occur when operating the facilities and when carrying out construction projects or decommissioning assets.

Repsol is exposed to impacts from any type of damage or temporary interruption of service associated with accidents in operations or involving vehicles for land, sea-river and air transportation of persons, substances, goods, or equipment.

Deviations in organizational and employee management Repsol is exposed to negative impacts arising from the management of the organization and its employees, which constitute a key asset for the Group and which, in certain business contexts, may prove inadequate for achieving its objectives.

Among the triggering factors of such impacts are aspects such as the recruitment and retention of talent, the organizational structure, both in terms of design and sizing, rising wage costs, cultural adaptation and transformation, protecting the health of our workers, and labor relations.

Deviations when undertaking investment projects The project performance phase is exposed to various risks, such as geological, technical, economic, commercial, legal and regulatory, in addition to those related to safety, environment and sustainability, all of which can compromise the delivery of goods and services, compliance with pre-agreed budgets and deadlines, and compliance with defined specifications and/or operational reliability of projects.

In addition, since the execution of the project also depends on the performance of third parties (including suppliers of goods, services and equipment, partners in joint agreements, associates, governments and other parties) that are not under the direct control of Repsol, the Group is exposed to execution and reputational risks through such entities.

Supply chain Repsol is exposed to negative impacts associated with the unavailability or scarcity of market goods and services, price and cost fluctuations, as well as interruptions and deviations in time and form in the supply of goods or the provision of services, including the supply of raw materials, which may eventually force the interruption of the affected business activities.

Specifically, part of the processing, transportation and marketing of crude oil and gas production from Upstream assets is carried out through infrastructure (pipelines, processing and purification units or liquefaction terminals) operated by third parties.
This infrastructure is exposed to various risks, such as unscheduled shutdowns or accidents, which may affect the provision of these logistical services by the suppliers concerned.

In addition, in certain countries where the Group operates that are prone to socio-political instability, there may be a shortage of qualified suppliers or contractors, which could in turn affect business.

The Company is also exposed to risks arising from its relations with other firms along the supply chain, or where Repsol is held jointly liable for their actions, including non-compliances with applicable laws and other regulations, external policies and standards or administrative requirements.

In addition, there may also be restrictions on the supply of certain raw materials and/or equipment, in particular those linked to low-carbon electricity generation technologies and the production of renewable fuels.
Operational continuity depends on some critical services and supplies provided by third parties, such as those in the IT environment or those related to essential supplies, such as electricity.
Increasing exposure is detected related to the lower availability of skilled labor in the different links of the supply chain, which can lead to interruptions and delays in projects.

Finally, it should be mentioned that a certain impact on the Group related to possible logistical restrictions arising from situations of geopolitical instability or armed conflicts that affect the main global transport routes and that result in cost overruns or delays in deliveries cannot be ruled out (see Note 27 “Geopolitical risks” to the 2025 consolidated Financial Statements).

Attacks on people or assets In general, but especially in certain countries where it operates, Repsol is exposed to potential impacts arising from acts of direct violence that may endanger the integrity of both the Company's assets and the persons linked to it as a result of actions taken by persons or groups partisan to any interests, governmental or otherwise, including, without limitation, acts of terrorism (including cyber origin), asset blocking, crime, piracy, kidnapping, extortion, theft, and acts of vandalism.
For more information, see Note 27 “Geopolitical risks” to the 2025 consolidated Financial Statements.
Various risks materialized in 2025, including those related to the regulatory landscape and litigation.
In relation to litigation risks, see Note 20.2 to the 2025 consolidated Financial Statements.
During 2025, there were several power supply interruptions affecting Repsol's industrial assets for reasons beyond the Company’s control.
On April 22, the Cartagena refinery suffered a general blackout due to voltage instabilities in the grid, which disrupted the operation of the complex for five days.

Subsequently, on April 28, the mass blackout that struck the Iberian Peninsula and disconnected it from the continental electricity system caused the total shutdown of all Repsol's refineries and chemical complexes in Spain and Portugal.
Lastly, on June 16, the Puertollano refinery experienced another blackout due to a breakdown at a substation of the electricity supplier.
These incidents have had an impact on the Company's operations and results (see section 4.1 Results).
The Company is currently weighing up the merits of pursuing legal action if and when liability for the blackout events is officially determined.

A I II Table of conversions and abbreviations Alternative performance measures III Risks IV V VI ≡ Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information Annual Corporate Governance Report Annual Report on Director Remuneration Board of Directors Repsol’s Board of Directors is the highest supervisory and decision-making body, holding the Company’s organic representation and responsible for steering and managing the Company’s business and interests, in all matters not reserved for the General Shareholders’ Meeting.

The specific rules relating to its remit, composition, tenure on the Board, the calling and quorum of meetings, the manner of passing resolutions and the distribution of positions within the Board are set out in Articles 31 to 36 of the Bylaws of Repsol, S. A.1 (hereinafter, the "Bylaws") and in the Regulations of the Board of Repsol, S. A.2 (hereinafter, the “Regulations of the Board of Directors”).

The greater efficiency and transparency in the exercise of the powers and fulfillment of the functions attributed to it, justify the creation by the Board of Directors of: (i) committees to which decision-making powers are attributed on matters whose immediacy and importance make it inadvisable to refer them to the full Board; (ii) specialized committees, with functions only of supervision, report, advice and proposal; as well as (iii) those others that, within the internal scope of their powers, are attributed to them by law, the Bylaws or the Regulations of the Board of Directors.

That is why, without prejudice to the statutory capacity of the Board of Directors to create other committees, the Company currently has an Delegated Committee, an Audit and Control Committee, a Nomination Committee, a Compensation Committee and a Sustainability Committee.
The specific regulation on the powers and functions of the committees of the Board of Directors is contained in Articles 33 to 37 of its Regulations and in Articles 37 to 39 bis of the Bylaws.
Composition The Board of Directors is made up of 15 members, in compliance with the range set by the Bylaws3, of which 66.67% are independent4.
The detailed composition of the Board of Directors, as of December 31, 2025, is reported in Section 2.4 Corporate Governance.

Without prejudice to the statutory capacity of the Board of Directors to create other committees, the Company currently has a Delegated Committee, an Audit and Control Committee, a Nomination Committee, a Compensation Committee and a Sustainability Committee.

Board of Directors and the Selection of Directors5, approved in 2015 and last updated in 2021 in accordance with current regulations and recommendations, sets out the diversity criteria, in the broad sense, that the Board must meet.

In accordance with this Policy, the process of selecting and appointing directors at Repsol is designed to support the election of candidates who bring a diversity of skills, knowledge, experience, nationalities, age and gender, as well as the appropriate competencies to the Board of Directors, enriching decision-making and providing plural points of view.
The Nomination Committee begins with a pre-assessment to identify the needs of the Board, determining the competencies, knowledge and experience required.

Based on this evaluation, the Committee defines the functions and skills necessary in the candidates to fill the vacancies, and may rely on external advice to carry out the prior analysis of the Group’s needs and for the validation or proposal of candidates.

Subsequently, the Committee formally submits to the Board of Directors the proposals for the appointment and re-election of independent external directors, and informs the Board of Directors of the proposals for the appointment and re-election of the proprietary and executive external directors.
The Board of Directors reviews and approves the Committee’s proposals and, lastly, the approved appointments are submitted for ratification by the Annual General Meeting.

% 1 4 2 2 2 12.5 80 67 67 50 1 4 2 2 2 12.5 80 67 67 50 Repsol’s Board of Directors, with the support of the Nomination Committee, has defined a matrix of competencies that reflects the skills, knowledge, and experience that are considered necessary for the proper performance of its functions.
This matrix is periodically reviewed to adapt to the needs and challenges of the environment and the business.

Board skills matrix Sustainability is among the competencies identified, understood as the ability to contribute to the sustainable development of the Company, taking into account the economic, social and environmental impacts of its activities and the expectations of its stakeholders.
This competence entails a focus on the principles of corporate social responsibility and to the environmental, social, and governance (ESG) criteria that govern the energy sector.

To identify directors who meet this competence in terms of sustainability, the following aspects have been considered: • Knowledge of the main plans and policies of other companies in the sector: experience in monitoring corporate policies, updating on matters of reputational significance and knowledge in corporate social responsibility and ESG.
• Knowledge of the relevant regulations related to ESG matters.
• The ability to think strategically: evaluate strategic options and business plans and budgets.
• Membership of the sustainability committees on the Boards of Directors of other large companies, especially in the field of energy.
• Knowledge of environmental, social (safety, human rights, labor aspects) and governance (ethics, compliance and business conduct) matters.
Remit over matters of sustainability encompasses the different environmental, social, and governance aspects, and therefore it relates to the Company’s material impacts, risks and opportunities.

Among them are the Chairman of Naturgy, environmental and safety experts who participate in ESG and sustainability committees, senior executives from Petronor, Sacyr and Petrobras, and other professionals who have advised governments and international regulators on energy and sustainable development affairs.

A I II Table of conversions and abbreviations Alternative performance measures III Risks IV V VI ≡ Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information Annual Corporate Governance Report Annual Report on Director Remuneration Sustainability Committee The Sustainability Committee of Repsol’s Board of Directors supervises and advises the Board of Directors on environmental, social, and governance issues, including climate change and energy transition, security, human rights, biodiversity, etc.
Its functions and composition are regulated by Article 37 of the Regulations of the Board of Directors.

In accordance with the provisions of these Regulations, the Sustainability Committee performs supervisory functions related to the energy transition, personal and process safety, management and control systems for non-financial risks and the reporting of non-financial information.

This Committee is also responsible for guiding the Group’s policy, objectives and guidelines in ESG areas; reviewing and reporting to the Board on the expectations of the Company’s stakeholders; and proposing a Sustainability Policy to the Board.
• Monitoring compliance with 2025-2030 decarbonization targets within the framework of 2024-2027 Strategic Update.
• Company scenario analysis 2023-2050.
• Sustainability information in the 2024 Consolidated Management Report.
• Analysis of the European Commission’s proposals for simplifying sustainability reporting.
• The greenhouse gas emissions map for the 2024 fiscal year.

• Analysis and periodic monitoring of safety performance: accident rate, scorecard of indicators, safety management in contractors and change of safety metrics and objectives to improve the learning cycle (Safety Excellence Program).
The Committee also regularly7 monitors, inter alia, the following matters: • The summary of investments aligned with the energy transition (end of 2024).
• The sustainability strategy and compliance with related actions and objectives, together with the global and local annual sustainability plans and their objectives and the sustainability risk map.
• The results of the double materiality assessment of ESG matters.
• The decarbonization roadmap and the fulfillment of related actions and targets, as well as emerging risks related to the energy transition.
• Performance in natural capital and biodiversity, as well as in water management.
• Strategic environmental and safety projects.
• Performance in community relations and human rights and its contribution to the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs).

The Sustainability Committee and the Executive Committee regularly monitor information on energy transition objectives, risk management, and the degree of fulfillment of corporate goals in environmental, social, and governance matters, as well as other strategic issues related to sustainability.
Specifically, the Sustainability Committee met five times in 2025 and reviewed, among other matters: • Fulfillment of the energy transition targets by the end of 2024.
• The Group’s risk map: short and long term.
• The consolidated map of the Group’s sustainability risks at the end of 2024 according to material ESG matters.
• The energy transition and climate change risks to which the Company is exposed in the 2030 and 2050 horizons.
• Review of advances in digitalization in the field of sustainability.
• The ratings of the main ESG analysts and rankings (ESG performance).
• The Global Sustainability Plan (closing of the 2024 Plan, and the 2025 Plan) and the Local Sustainability Plans.
• The 2024 Sustainable Development Goals Contribution Report.
• Progress in the circular economy strategy.
• Proposal and monitoring of the Company’s sustainability objectives (2024 Closing and 2025 Proposal).
• Monitoring of progress in human rights, as well as the updating of the regulatory framework, including the evolution of the Standard for Relations with Communities and Human Rights.
• Updating of the energy transition and decarbonization metrics and targets.
• The reporting frameworks and regulatory framework for the sustainability information of the Consolidated Management Report.
• The process and results of the materiality assessment (impacts, risks, opportunities, and environmental dependencies8).
• Progress on environmental issues: circular economy, biodiversity and water management.
• The main implications for the Company of the COP30 agreements.

In accordance with the provisions of the Regulations of the Board of Directors, the Chairman of the Committee shall report periodically to the Board of Directors on the Committee’s actions.

Throughout 2025, issues were jointly presented to the Sustainability Committee and the Audit and Control Committee that, in accordance with corporate governance best practices, must be reviewed by both bodies.

In this regard, the two committees were briefed jointly on the following matters: (i) short- and long-term risk map of the Group; (ii) consolidated map of the Group’s sustainability risks at the end of 2024 according to material ESG matters; (iii) energy transition and climate change risks to which the Company is exposed in the 2030 and 2050 horizons; (iv) reporting and regulatory frameworks of the Consolidated Management Report; and (v) the map of emerging and climate change risks.
Executive Committee This body is in charge of proposing and executing the global strategy, as well as implementing the basic policies established by the Board of Directors.

The Executive Committee is responsible for implementing the Strategic Plan and, in the field of sustainability, has among its functions: • Overseeing the Company’s policies, including those of sustainability, safety, the environment and human rights.
• Supporting the progress of and defining the Global Sustainability Plan and the Safety Excellence Plan.
• Overseeing and proposing to the Board of Directors the Company’s strategy in alignment with the energy transition, as well as analyzing long-term scenarios (2031-2050).
• Approving and evaluating objectives, including those of sustainability, environment and human rights, annual budgets and investment plans.
• Approving, monitoring and evaluating objectives, including those of sustainability, which are linked to the variable remuneration of the Company’s people.
• Approving the investment qualification methodology for its alignment with the energy transition and the evaluation of investment proposals and their impact on the CII.
• Approving any changes in decarbonization metrics and the monitoring, at least once a year, of progress towards achieving the energy transition and decarbonization targets.

Sustainability Coordination Committee In 2025, Repsol has defined a new body in charge of coordinating, facilitating and aligning ESG matters between the different businesses and expert areas in environmental, social, and governance matters with the aim of integrating sustainability into the Company's operations and achieving more agile management of material impacts, risks and opportunities.

This committee has the participation of the four business areas and more than 15 corporate areas, with the capacity for internal mobilization so as to more diligently and efficiently facilitate decision-making.

Integration of sustainability-related performance in incentive schemes (GOV-3) To help ensure alignment across the organization, employee remuneration is reviewed annually by the Executive Committee, the Compensation Committee and the Board of Directors, the body that approves it, and is linked to compliance with the sustainability and decarbonization targets.
In the case of employees with annual10 variable remuneration, this remuneration is based on individual targets of each employee and on targets of the business units.
In 2025, targets related to environmental, social, and governance (ESG) matters were integrated into all the targets of each business unit, with a weight of between 20% and 45%.
In the particular case of the CEO, the weight of the targets related to decarbonization and sustainability represented 25% of their total variable annual remuneration in 2025.

In addition, there is a Long-Term Incentive (LTI) for the 2025–2028 period in which 40% of the targets are linked to the energy transition, which affects all management staff, including the CEO, and other employees.
Each type of target is associated with different metrics for its measurement, and each metric has an associated scale of achievement defined according to its variability and strictness.
These scales have a minimum threshold of compliance below which no incentive entitlement is generated and a maximum level set at 100%11.

Likewise, pursuant to the provisions of Article 529 novodecies of the Capital Companies Act, it is the responsibility of the Annual General Meeting to approve the Remuneration Policy for directors12 at least every three years.
Thus, on May 30, 2025, the Annual General Meeting approved, at the proposal of the Board of Directors, Repsol’s new Remuneration Policy for Directors to be applied in 2025, 2026, 2027 and 2028.

The general principle that defines the Remuneration Policy is the pursuit of the reciprocal generation of value for the Group and the directors and the alignment of their interests in the long term with those of the shareholders, for total transparency.
Repsol’s Remuneration Policy sets out the principles and criteria applicable to the remuneration of directors in their capacity as directors and for the performance of executive functions.

Statement on due diligence (GOV-4) Repsol has a due diligence process in place to identify, prevent, and remediate actual or potential negative impacts on people or the environment throughout its value chain.

This process is underpinned by the Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights of the UN, and the OECD Guidelines for Multinational Enterprises on Responsible Business Conduct, as well as other international agreements and current human rights regulations.
For detailed information on the implementation of the due diligence process, see the Index of essential elements of the due diligence process.

Risk management and internal controls over sustainability reporting (GOV-5) The Repsol Group has been working in recent years, since the entry into force of Law 11/2018, to strengthen the Internal Control System over Sustainability Reporting (ICSR), the aim of which is to support the reliability of the sustainability information reported.
In 2025, this control model continued to be refined, with advances made in the deployment of controls in the processes identified as a priority.

The ICSR is defined at Repsol following the same principles as the Internal Control System over Financial Reporting model and the guidelines of the COSO framework (mainly the COSO Integrated Framework and COSO ESG).

It has the following elements: a governance model, a risk identification and assessment process, materiality assessment and scope model, inventory of controls, including those of information technology, etc.

With regard to the differentiated responsibility of the Company’s units and bodies involved in risk management, the ICSR is based on the three-line model, the standard recommended by the Global Institute of Internal Auditors.

The Audit, Control and Risk Department evaluates the design and functioning of controls, processes and other elements of the Group’s ICSR, reporting on these activities to the Audit and Control Committee (A&CC).
Responsible bodies The Board of Directors (BoD) reserves the power to approve the risk control and management policy, and oversight of internal information and control systems.

In accordance with the Regulations of the Board of Directors, the Audit and Control Committee supervises and evaluates the process of preparation and the integrity of the financial and non-financial information of Repsol, S. A. and its Group, periodically reviewing compliance with regulatory requirements, the definition of the consolidation perimeter, the effectiveness of internal control systems, internal audit, and risk management systems, financial and non-financial,so that the main risks are identified, managed and made known.

Procedures for reviewing and authorizing sustainability information and the description of the ICSR Repsol’s ICSR system aims to contribute to the reliability of the Company’s sustainability information reported in accordance with the current regulatory framework, as well as the regulation of the National Securities Market Commission (CNMV), the Capital Companies Act, and the Good Governance Code of Listed Companies of the Spanish National Securities Market Commission.

A I II Table of conversions and abbreviations Alternative performance measures III Risks IV V VI ≡ Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information Annual Corporate Governance Report Annual Report on Director Remuneration This model of internal control over sustainability information is articulated through an integrated process that consists of five components developed through seventeen principles in accordance with the provisions of the COSO 2013 framework.

The ICSR is integrated into the Organization through the establishment of a scheme of roles and responsibilities for the different bodies and functions, included in the procedures approved and disseminated within the Group.

Governance model At the Sustainability Department (SD), there is a reporting unit on sustainability information that governs the sustainability information reported, under the supervision of the A&CC.

This unit: • Defines the criteria to be used in the reporting of sustainability information after analyzing the different regulatory frameworks in this field, and informs the A&CC of any significant changes in the reporting strategy.
• Prepares the sustainability reporting risk map in coordination with the unit that manages the integrated risk system and the economic- administrative unit.
• Supports the reasonableness of the reporting criteria by verifying compliance with applicable international standards.

• Defines procedures and/or guidelines that regulate, among other aspects, the roles and responsibilities of the areas involved in the sustainability information reporting process and the annual reporting milestone calendar.
• Assures, in coordination with the Audit, Control and Risk Department, that this information has processes and systems for adequate identification of risks and an adequate set of controls.

The economic-administrative unit governs the financial information reported by the Company and is responsible for preparing, among other documents, the financial statements and the Group’s Management Report and, therefore, coordinates the reporting of the financial and sustainability information that must be presented publicly and so that it is disclosed in compliance with the deadlines and other requirements established by applicable regulations.
In addition, it is responsible for monitoring and internally coordinating all the requirements of Spanish and European regulators (CNMV and ESMA, respectively).

In addition, the economic-administrative unit and the SD define, in coordination with the business units and corporate areas affected, the processes and controls that support consistency between the financial and sustainability information presented in the Group’s financial statements and Management Report, as well as with other documents made public by Repsol.
Supervision units The Internal Audit unit reports to the Audit and Control Committee of the Board of Directors of Repsol.

This unit has the mission of improving and protecting the value of the Organization by providing objective assurance, advice and risk-based knowledge, thereby supporting the committee in the performance of its duties.

Among other tasks, this unit oversees the evaluation of the design and operation of the controls of the Group’s Internal Control System over Sustainability Information, reports its results to the Audit and Control Committee and the Board of Directors of Repsol.

Business units and corporate areas identified as “owners of the controls” Within the Group, the different business units and corporate areas identified as "owners of the controls" are responsible for the validity, execution and proper functioning of the processes and controls associated with them.
Model components The documentation making up the ICSR comprises the following components: • The sustainability reporting risk map (reviewed at least annually).
• Scope model: corporate perimeter, systems, user- developed programs, processes, service organizations.
• Documentation of the processes within the scope of the ICSR.
• Inventory of controls identified in the different processes.
• Result of the evaluation of the design and operation of the controls.
• Certifications of the validity and effectiveness of the controls, issued for each year by those responsible for each of them.

The ICSR is articulated through a process in which, based on the identification and evaluation of the risks of reporting sustainability information, a scope model is defined that includes the companies covered, the set of relevant processes for preparation, the systems and the user-developed programs, the review and subsequent disclosure of sustainability information, and control activities aimed at the prevention and detection of any errors.
The Repsol Group has an integrated risk management process.
This process establishes a uniform methodology for the identification and assessment of risks by the responsible areas in the Organization.
As a result of this process, the Repsol Group’s risk map is drawn up, of which sustainability risks are a part.
For more details, see Appendix III Risks in this report.

A I II Table of conversions and abbreviations Alternative performance measures III Risks IV V VI ≡ Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information Annual Corporate Governance Report Annual Report on Director Remuneration The main risks that could affect the Group’s sustainability information objectives are reflected in the sustainability information risk map: • Leakage of confidential or privileged information.
• Inadequate control environment.
• Unavailability and lack of security in information systems.
• Errors or omissions in the reporting of sustainability information.

Scope and updating The process of identifying and assessing the causal factors of sustainability reporting risks covers all the objectives of sustainability information, which could impact its reliability.

The inventory of risks and their assessment in terms of impact and probability is reviewed annually in accordance with the Repsol Group’s integrated risk management process, as indicated in Appendix III Risks in this report.
When defining the scope of the model, both the risks and the exercise of double materiality of the Company are considered in order to prioritize control efforts in the Company.
Control activities As indicated above, one of the components of the ICSR model is the control activities aimed at mitigating risks related to the reporting of sustainability information.

In this regard, the following types of controls have been identified in the ICSR: General controls • Entity-level controls: these operate throughout the Organization and allow us to achieve global control objectives, and cover the risk of “general environment” and comply with the COSO 2013 framework.

The types of general controls include those related to the existence and disclosure of a Code of Ethics and Business Conduct, a Communications Channel; Strategic Company Management; Management of the Organizational Structure; People Management and Training; Preparation and Management of the Group’s Regulations; Corporate Governance; Corporate and Power of Attorney Management; Risk Management; Audit, Risk and Internal Control Functions.

• General IT Controls: oriented toward supporting the reliability, integrity, availability, and confidentiality of the information contained in the applications considered important to the control model.
They are grouped into the following typologies: development of information systems, modifications to information systems, operations and control of access to programs and data.
Cross-sectional controls, which in turn can be: • Company level: primarily governance controls exercised by the second lines.
• Reported data: related to the validation roles and the calculation methodology used.
Specific controls at the process level from the source of the data to its reporting.

Repsol’s policies related to sustainability Repsol has a Code of Ethics and Business Conduct in place, approved by the Board of Directors following a favorable report from the Audit and Control Committee, the Sustainability Committee, and the Ethics and Compliance Committee.
The Code applies to all directors, managers, and employees of the Repsol Group, regardless of the type of contract determined by their professional or employment relationship.

Business partners (non-operated joint ventures, contractors, suppliers, and other collaborating companies) are part of Repsol’s value chain and, for this reason, must act in accordance with the Company Code, as well as any other applicable contractual provisions when they intervene on behalf of or in collaboration with Repsol.

The Code establishes the minimum standards of conduct that must guide the behavior of all its recipients as they carry out their professional activity and lays down a set of procedures in the event of non- compliance.

Repsol has fourteen policies, which define the Company’s public goals and management fundamentals, establishing principles and guidelines for action for all Repsol employees with the purpose of fostering relationships, processes, and decision-making that are aligned with the Company’s values.
These policies are approved by the Executive Committee, the body responsible for the application of these standards.
for the deployment of policies.

Internal regulations are a management tool, the main objective of which is to strengthen control systems and promote compliance with external and internal requirements; provide criteria for action that make it easier for employees to understand their responsibilities; to assist in the management and control of risks and to contribute to more agile and orderly execution of the Company’s activities.

The People and Organization Department is responsible for keeping the Company’s regulatory body up to date and supports the internal dissemination of regulations and their availability to all employees.
It also supports the review or ratification of policies at least every five years.

1.3 Business model and strategy Strategy, business model and value chain (SBM-1) Repsol is present throughout the energy value chain and markets a range of products in more than 90 countries.

The main products or services offered by the Company are detailed below: • Supply of crude oil, intermediate products, chemical raw materials and lipid residues consumed by the Group’s industrial complexes and third parties.
• Production and marketing of fuels, biofuels and petrochemical products in international markets.
The Company’s projects include the production of renewable hydrogen.
• Development, production and marketing of lubricants, aviation fuels, asphalts and petroleum-derived specialties.
• Marketing and trading of liquefied natural gas.
• Generation of low-emissions electricity in Spain, the USA, Chile and Italy.
• Marketing of retail Electricity and Gas in Spain and Portugal.
• Maritime transport for the Group's activity.
Repsol markets wholesale (B2B) and retail (B2C) products1 and services in the first segment.
The main group of customers includes industries and companies from various sectors (automotive, food, chemical, packaging, aviation, etc.).

Customers of retail products and services are mainly consumers of fuels and other petroleum products (mainly marketed at service stations), natural gas, electric mobility, electricity and energy services.
For more information, see Consumers and end customers.

One of the key pillars of the Strategic Plan is the Company’s decarbonization, which mainly impacts issues of climate change and circular economy.
Social and governance issues support Repsol’s decarbonization plan.

Sustainability-related targets Repsol monitors the effectiveness of its policies and actions with respect to material sustainability-related IROs by defining their objectives in its Global Sustainability Plan.
The Global Sustainability Plan, which is approved by the Executive Committee and overseen by the Sustainability Committee, incorporates the main sustainability objectives.

This plan is the framework for action for their deployment at the local level in the different business units of countries and industrial centers of the Iberian Peninsula through the Local Sustainability Plans.
The details of the plan’s progress are published annually on the corporate website.
The Sustainability Committee of Repsol’s Board of Directors is responsible for monitoring and advising on policies and objectives in the areas of the environment, safety, and social responsibility.
Repsol implements certification, verification, and assurance processes through both internal and external audits to ensure compliance with national and international regulations.

In addition, it addresses voluntary processes, such as ISO 9001 quality certification, ISO 45001 safety, ISO 14001 environment, ISO 50001 energy management, and circular economy such as ISCC EU and OSC Europe.

Repsol is also evaluated by different ESG ratings (MSCI, Sustainalytics, CHRB, among others), which analyze its objectives and management practices, and have made a positive assessment of the Company’s performance and strategy.

Business model and value chain Repsol’s activities are structured into four segments: • Upstream: exploration and production of crude oil and natural gas reserves, as well as development of low-carbon geological solutions, especially CO2 storage.

• Industrial: petroleum refining, petrochemical and trading, transportation and wholesale marketing of crude oil, natural gas and fuels, including the development of activities related to new products such as hydrogen, renewable fuels and synthetic fuels.
• Customer: mobility (service stations) and marketing of fuels (gasoline, diesel, aviation kerosene, liquefied petroleum gases, biofuels, etc.
), electricity and gas and lubricants and other specialties.
• Low-Carbon Generation (LCG): generation of electricity from renewable sources (solar, wind, hydroelectric and storage) and through combined cycles of natural gas.
The Upstream business aims to develop and exploit the Company’s crude oil and gas reserves.
The main inputs of industrial complexes are crude oil, natural gas, intermediate materials, chemical raw materials and raw materials for the production of biofuels.
The trading activity aims to support the supply of these inputs to industrial complexes (including combined cycle plants) and the sale to third parties.
Further information on the products and services offered can be found in Strategy, business model and value chain (SBM-1).

Also, information regarding the current and expected benefits for customers, investors, and other stakeholders of the Company’s business model and value chain is included in Chapter 5 – Business performance.

In the upstream phase, the activities cover a broad base of suppliers that are responsible for the provision of raw materials, manufacturing goods and construction services necessary for the development of facilities and projects within the scope of the Company’s own operations (for more information see S2 Workers in the value chain).
In relation to the aforementioned operations, the Company carries out Upstream activities for oil and gas, where it operates directly or in alliance with partners.
These activities may be supplemented with decarbonization initiatives involving carbon capture and storage.
Repsol transforms crude oil and various alternative raw materials, like residual plastic and other renewable material, through chemical and refining processes, and also produces renewable fuels.

These activities are complemented by transport, storage and trading functions—both proprietary and supplier—that allow intermediate and final products to be managed and mobilized throughout the supply chain.
These operations are integrated with the production and sale of electricity, an activity in which the Company participates both as a direct operator and through agreements with strategic partners.
The presence of these actors reflects an operating model based on cooperation and the shared development of energy solutions.

Finally, the marketing and sale of energy products and derivatives are managed through different channels, which include the distribution of LPG, petroleum derivatives, and the network of service stations where fossil fuels, renewables, and electricity are primarily sold.
In the downstream section, industrial and private customers, together with society as a whole, constitute the final destination of the products and services offered by the Company.

Key actors are involved throughout the value chain: suppliers, who are essential for the supply and execution of projects; partners, who participate in strategic activities in multiple links of the business; shareholders and investors, who support the Company’s development and transformation; and customers who use the products and services.

In this analysis, detailed information is reported with a description of the impacts, classified by each ESRS topic and subtopic, and indicating whether it is positive or negative, current or potential, also detailing the effect on the different actors and businesses in the value chain.

The results show a greater number of positive impacts, most of which are concentrated in the social sphere (company personnel, workers in the value chain and affected groups) and in governance (corporate conduct).
Most of the negative material impacts are in the environmental field, focused on the issues of climate change, pollution, water and biodiversity.
For all of them, the actions that the Company is carrying out to reduce negative impacts and enhance positive ones are specified.

The material risks that may affect the Company’s performance in terms of the environment are related to the mitigation of climate change and energy consumption, in the social sphere with industrial safety, and in governance with the possibility of having sanctions for regulatory non-compliance and risks associated with cyberattacks that affect operations.

1.4 Double materiality assessment Description of the process to identify and assess material impacts, risks, and opportunities (IRO-1) The double materiality assessment carried out at Repsol is based on the methodology established in the ESRS.
This analysis which covers the entire value chain is reviewed annually and consists of the following phases: 1.
Context and value chain analysis.
Identification of impacts, risks and opportunities (IROs).
Impact and financial materiality assessments.
Double materiality matrix development.
Determination of the list of data points to be reported.

LEGEND NI: Negative impact PI: Positive impact OPT: Opportunity RI: Risk Time horizon: Short-, medium- or long-term OO: Own operations US: Upstream DS: Downstream 1: Upstream 2: Industrial 3: Customer 4: LCG 5: Corporate Effects of risks and opportunities on the business model The risks associated with climate change mitigation can significantly impact production costs, especially those derived from regulatory changes (IRO-11), adoption of less efficient technologies (IRO-8), increased competition and decreased sales (IRO-7).
Likewise, decoupling of climate investment (IRO-12) could limit trade opportunities.
In addition, the scarcity of raw materials (IRO-10) can generate problems of access to supplies.
However, the energy transition also offers opportunities for the Company, such as energy efficiency optimization (IRO-3), which can increase profitability and competitiveness in the market.
In addition, access to aid for sustainable financing (IRO-13) contributes to improving alignment with sustainability objectives and facilitates the raising of capital under ESG criteria.
There are additional risks related to potential industrial accidents (IRO-25) or attacks on facilities (IRO-27), which could result in operational disruptions and financial losses.
In the area of governance, non- compliance may result in sanctions (IRO-49) and reputational damage.
Likewise, the impact on operations (IRO-53) could result in negative reputational consequences and production losses.

A I II Table of conversions and abbreviations Alternative performance measures III Risks IV V VI ≡ Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information Annual Corporate Governance Report Annual Report on Director Remuneration M METHODOLOGY Methodology of the double materiality assessment Description of the process for determining and assessing material impacts, risks, and opportunities The double materiality process is reviewed annually, in line with the requirements of the CSRD.

The process is jointly led by the Sustainability Department and the Audit, Control and Risk Department and relies on the collaboration of experts with technical, strategic and cross-cutting knowledge across all the sustainability areas established in the ESRS standards (hereinafter, ESRS).

The process considers both the impacts that the Company’s operations may have on stakeholders and the potential effects that the external environment may have on the Organization.The phases of the analysis are outlined below: 1.
Stakeholder mapping: Nine main stakeholder groups have been identified across the value chain.
Context and value chain analysis: A review of the internal and external context has been conducted to identify possible significant aspects.
The corporate map has been revisited and updated including all Repsol companies within the scope of the double materiality assessment.
The value chain has been defined using as reference the O&G value chain model, while adapting it to Repsol’s specific context and characteristics.
The double materiality process has been tailored to incorporate critical activities, sensitive areas and key commercial relationships.

Besides, environmental and social risks have been assessed across the entire value chain, using specific methodologies, such as the LEAP approach, to identify high-risk locations and prioritize adverse impacts, while enabling alignment with ESRS requirements.

Review of possible material topics and IROs: An expanded identification of IROs has been carried out, promoting alignment and traceability with the topics, subtopics and sub-subtopics defined in the ESRS.
In addition, Repsol has identified entity-specific topics of its activity, and added them to the expanded list of IROs.
For each IRO, the affected stakeholder, the value chain segment impacted and, where relevant, the geographical area have been identified.
Impact and financial materiality: IROs have been assessed from both an impact and financial perspective.
The materiality of each topic and subtopic is determined by the IROs associated with each of them.

For both impact and financial materiality, three time horizons have been identified—short (1 year), medium (1–5 years) and long (>5 years)—with specific adaptations for climate change (medium‑term 2030 and long‑term 2050), being the horizon with the greatest impact the only one which has been assessed.

For impact materiality, the following dimensions have been assessed: scale (severity or benefit of the impact), scope (number of people or geographical extent affected), the remediability to restore the situation prior to the produced impact (only for negative impacts) and the probability of occurrence (100% for current impacts and/or involving human rights).
The final value of the impact is the outcome of multiplying the severity (average of scale, scope, and remediability) by the probability of occurrence.
Where an impact involves risks to life or the possibility of causing a serious and irreversible injury or harm, severity takes precedence over probability in the impact assessment.
a panel of external experts supplemented the impact materiality assessment with additional insights.

For financial materiality, risks and opportunities have been assessed using three methodologies: risk map, quantitative estimations and qualitative assessment, prioritizing matters according to their magnitude and likelihood within the time horizon with the highest valuation or potential economic impact (based on historical data or expert judgment).
To determine the threshold value, Repsol has established a cut-off aligned with the value used in the financial materiality equivalent to a score of 3 on a 1‑to‑5 scale.
The same threshold has been applied to impact materiality following a sensitivity analysis across different scenarios.
Drawing up the double materiality matrix: The matrix results from consolidating the outcomes of the IRO assessments, identifying material and non‑material topics and subtopics.
The results are reviewed by the Executive Committee and by the Sustainability Committee.
List of data points to be reported: Based on the material topics and subtopics, the ESRS‑required data points disclosed in this report were identified.

The results of the 2025 double materiality assessment differ from those of 2024 due to methodological changes—such as revised time horizons and the alignment of the materiality threshold with financial statement parameters—resulting in a reduction of material IROs from 61 in 2024 to 54 in 2025.

Environmental information 2.1 Climate change 2.1.1 Governance Integration of sustainability-related performance in incentive schemes (ESRS 2 GOV-3) Repsol has set targets and metrics in relation to decarbonization and sustainability, integrating them into its annual variable remuneration system.
More precisely, 25% of the CEO’s annual variable remuneration is tied to sustainability and decarbonization objectives, with 15% tied to the latter.

Within this percentage, 5% is allocated to the increase in the installed capacity of renewable generation envisaged in the 2025 Annual Budget and 5% to the development of other low-carbon platforms and 5% to the reduction of the Carbon Intensity Indicator (CII)1.
The degree of achievement of these objectives is assessed by the Compensation Committee based on the evidence provided.
For each metric, a quantitative compliance level scale is established, depending on its nature.
The details can be found in Targets and metrics set out in the CEO's 2025 Annual Variable Remuneration in relation to sustainability.

In addition, Repsol has implemented a Long-Term Incentive (LTI) for the period 2025-2028, which affects all management staff, including the CEO, and other employees, in which 40% of the objectives are tied to the energy transition.
This LTI includes specific metrics such as the reduction of the CII, renewable generation capacity (GW), and renewable fuel production capacity (Mt/a).
2.1.2 Strategy The energy sector faces the challenge of supplying secure and affordable energy in a context of growing energy demand, driven global economic growth.
At the same time, energy efficiency and a transition to decarbonized energies are needed in order to meet the urgency of curbing the impact of greenhouse gas emissions on the climate.
The pace of this energy transition is mainly determined by technological development and the implementation of enabling policies on a global and regional scale.

Transition plan for climate change mitigation (E1-1) In 2019, Repsol began its transformation to align itself with an energy transition compatible with the global ambition to achieve net zero emissions.

Since then, it has made this commitment concrete through different strategic plans, the most recent being 2024–2027 Strategic Update approved by the Board of Directors on February 21, 2024, which reinforces the Company’s decarbonization objectives.

This strategic process was completed with the presentation of the Energy Transition Plan and its submission to an advisory vote at the Annual General Meeting on May 10, 2024, in which it obtained the support of 70% of the capital present or represented.
Transition metrics and targets are regularly reviewed to adapt them, where necessary, to the environment in which energy sector operates.

During the last few months, metrics and objectives for the 2026–2028 period have been reviewed, and are now included in this report and will be disclosed to the market in greater detail in March 2026.

Repsol reaffirms its focus on climate change mitigation and its ambition to reach net zero GHG emissions by 2050, in line with the Paris Agreement, which seeks to limit global warming to 1.5°C and achieve climate neutrality by the second half of the century2.

Repsol’s energy transition strategy enables the Company to progressively decarbonize its own activities (reducing its Scope 1 and 2 emissions) while also contributing to the decarbonization of the value chain, especially for consumers, through the development of low- or zero-carbon3 products (Scope 3, category 11).
In this context, Repsol set the goal of achieving net zero emissions by 2050, which involves reducing greenhouse gas emissions to net zero in Scopes 1, 2 and 3.

To make progress towards this goal, the Company set two targets for 2030: reducing direct emissions by 55% (Scope 1 and 2 under operational control) compared to 2016 and reducing total emissions (Scopes 1, 2 and 3) by 20% compared with 2018.

Scopes 1 and 2 include emissions from operated assets and Scope 3 the emissions associated with category 11 for the use of the products sold (gas and petroleum products).4 1 Under Repsol's criteria, low-carbon investments include all investments in projects that contribute to the Company's decarbonization, and refer to projects that do not generate emissions or do so in a very low proportion compared to their fossil energy alternatives.
It does not fully correspond to the European Taxonomy's definition of sustainable investments.

2 The goal of achieving carbon neutrality is to achieve a balance in the second half of the century between anthropogenic emissions by sources and anthropogenic removals by sinks, on the basis of equity and in the context of sustainable development and efforts to eradicate poverty, as set out in Article 4.1 of the Paris Agreement.
The Methodology for climate change reporting and metrics provides detailed information on the analysis used to determine the strategy’s alignment with the 1.5°C objective.
3 Low- or zero-carbon products refer to those that generate low or no greenhouse gas emissions throughout their life cycle, compared to conventional products.
4 In this metric, Scope 3 refers to Category 11 (use of sold products).
The absolute Scope 1, 2 and 3 emissions metric covers 91% of the Company’s total operated emissions.

A I II Table of conversions and abbreviations Alternative performance measures III Risks IV V VI ≡ Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information Annual Corporate Governance Report Annual Report on Director Remuneration Since the energy transition requires reducing greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions while simultaneously meeting society’s energy demand, the Company also uses the CII as a key metric to assess progress towards decarbonization per unit of primary energy consumption.
This indicator reflects the combined impact of the main pillars of decarbonization, which are key to its climate strategy, namely the development of electricity and renewable fuels.
Repsol has set targets to reduce its Carbon Intensity Indicator (CII)5 by 15% for 2025 compared to 2016: 25% ±1% for 2030, 55% for 2040 and 100% for 2050.
Repsol thus has two complementary indicators that incorporate Scope 3 emissions with different perimeters and that strengthen its decarbonization strategy.

It is noteworthy that by 2025 all the established decarbonization targets have been achieved: a) reducing the CII by 15%; b) reducing methane intensity to 0.20% in the operated assets of Upstream; and c) reducing emissions from routine gas flaring by 50% in the same assets compared to 2018.
These last two targets contribute to the 2030 Scope 1 and 2 emissions reduction target.

Decarbonization pillars and levers Repsol’s energy transition strategy is structured around five strategic pillars which represent the main areas of action in the energy transition towards net zero emissions.
For each pillar, decarbonization levers are deployed, which are concrete initiatives that generate effective and quantifiable reductions in Scope 1, 2 and 3 emissions.
Each pillar integrates both these levers and other mainly commercial actions that, although they do not directly reduce emissions, support the transition in the Company’s energy model.

This structure makes it possible to clearly distinguish between the strategic framework (pillars) and the operational mechanisms (levers) that drive the achievement of the 2030 interim targets, as set out in Actions and resources in relation to climate change policies (E1-3) and Targets related to climate change mitigation and adaptation (E1-4).

Operational excellence and asset portfolio management Energy efficiency, electrification of processes, reduction of direct CO2 and methane emissions from current operations and optimization of Upstream asset portfolio towards less carbon-intensive assets are the levers for achieving the target of a 55% reduction in Scope 1 and 2 emissions by 2030 compared with the 2016 baseline year.
Capital allocation to the Upstream business is consistent with stable production levels in the range of 550–600 kboe/d throughout this decade.

As previously indicated, investment is prioritized in higher‑margin assets and projects with lower greenhouse gas emissions, which will enable a reduction in carbon intensity over the coming years, reaching an intensity of 10 kgCO2e/boe in 2027.
At its industrial businesses (Refining and Chemicals), the Company plans to continue implementing measures, electrification of processes, and increasing the use of renewable electricity.
Renewable electricity generation Renewable generation is key to Repsol’s energy transition to reduce emissions per unit of energy produced.

Since the start of this activity in 2018, Repsol has built a portfolio of renewable electricity projects (wind, solar and hydro), with 5.8 GW of installed capacity in 2025 and projected to exceed 106 GW by 2030, especially in Spain and the USA.

Growth in the coming years has been adjusted to developments in the environment, marked by higher development and financing costs, as well as lower tax incentives in the United States, prioritizing investments according to the profitability thresholds established by Repsol.
The strategy contemplates the rotation of asset portfolios, with the incorporation of partners and the financing of projects to achieve better rates of return on net investments made.

Liquid and gaseous renewable fuels The competitiveness of Repsol’s refineries, in the top European quartile by economic7 margin, enables their transformation, in alignment with demand trends promoted by regulation and focusing on the circular economy, with the use of organic waste to produce renewable fuels and the recycling of plastics.

The refineries will continue to operate through to 2030, due to the resilience shown by demand in Spain and the selective expansion to adjacent markets, while increasing the production of renewable fuels.

These categories account for approximately 37% of total Scope 3 emissions.
Together, the CII covers 41% of the Company’s total operated emissions.

The targets for renewable fuel and biomethane production are detailed in Targets related to resource use and the circular economy (E5-3).
Multi-energy for customers Repsol is moving toward a multi-energy model focused on the supply from different energy sources to meet both the needs of its customers and European decarbonization goals.
This approach allows the Company to reduce emissions throughout its value chain (Scope 3) by diversifying its portfolio of energy products and services.

Repsol is a leading player in the liquid and gaseous fuels market in the Iberian Peninsula, and has expanded its offer to include renewable fuels, residential and mobility electricity, and other low-carbon products.

Repsol has developed in the Iberian Peninsula one of the most extensive 100% renewable fuel networks in Europe, with availability at more than 1,550 service stations, with 56% of the network in Spain offering multi-energy solutions that include 3,600 public charging points for electric vehicles.

The Company is also developing the sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) market and now has a 100% renewable fuel production plant at its Cartagena refinery, with a capacity of 250,000 tons per year for the co-production of renewable road diesel and SAF from oily waste.
In the field of residential energy marketing, Repsol has 3 million electricity and gas customers in Spain and Portugal, with the aim of reaching 5 million by 2030.
Development of carbon sinks Carbon sinks constitute a pillar that will not be material for the Company at least until after 2030.
Until then, Repsol’s activity is limited to reducing technical, regulatory, and business risks through its participation in certain projects at an early development stage.

Repsol’s decarbonization pathway considers the long‑term contribution of carbon sinks (CO₂ capture and storage projects and, eventually, natural solutions if needed) to complete net-zero GHG emissions by 2050).
By 2030, the Company's portfolio of marketed products will reflect a shift towards more decarbonized alternatives.

These will include renewable electricity and renewable fuels (liquid fuels, biogas and hydrogen), where 20% of the portfolio will consist of low-carbon products (based on energy content), together with a reduction in sales of natural gas and petroleum products, which are the levers associated with the Company's reduction of Scope 3 emissions.

Repsol assesses progress in the execution of its energy transition strategy through the various indicators detailed in sections E1-4 and E1-6, including absolute Scope 1, 2 and 3 emissions, operational emissions (Scopes 1 and 2) and the CII.
• In 2025, Repsol's total emissions (Scopes 1, 2 and 3, category 11)4 amounted to 184.7 MtCO₂e, down 17.4% on 2018, advancing toward the target of –20% by 2030.
• Scope 1 and 2 emissions stood at 13.9 MtCO₂e in 2025, down 45.3% on 2016 and on track to reach the -55% reduction commitment of these emissions for 2030.
• Meanwhile, the CII stood at 65.2 gCO₂e/MJ, which represents a decline of 15.1% compared with 2016, exceeding the target of –15% in 2025 and with a focus on achieving a reduction of 25% ±1 in 2030.

These results reflect sustained progress in the decarbonization of the Company's energy portfolio and the consolidation of its strategy to reach net zero emissions by 2050, as detailed in Targets related to climate change mitigation and adaptation (E1-4).

Investments Repsol has classified the following investments as low- carbon: energy efficiency and process electrification, renewable electricity generation, production and marketing of renewable fuels (biofuels, renewable hydrogen, synthetic fuels, biogas), chemicals (long-life polymers) and non-combustible products, CCS, marketing of renewable electricity, distributed generation and renewable electric mobility, and R&D and corporate venturing investments in low-carbon technologies.
