Based on this methodology, investments are classified into three categories: • Aligned with the energy transition, if they do not negatively affect achievement of the targets for reduction of the CII or if they contribute to achieving these targets.
• Enabling the energy transition when they have a negative impact of less than 1% on the CII and this can be offset with other initiatives.

For Upstream, investments are subject to additional conditions, such as the limitation of the useful life of exploitable reserves and the exclusion of extra-heavy crudes, tar sands and offshore projects in the Arctic.
• Misaligned, if they do not meet the requirements of the above categories.
This approach allows for investment decisions that are aligned with the CII reduction target.

In 2025, using the investment qualification methodology, a report from the Sustainability Department was incorporated into 16 investment proposals (8 from Upstream, 5 from Low-Carbon Generation and 3 from Industrial).
Of these, 75% were aligned, 12.5% facilitating, and 12.5% misaligned.
To promote investment decisions consistent with its decarbonization goals, Repsol applies an internal carbon price to all projects.

1.5 ºC alignment Repsol has assessed its energy transition strategy against scenarios of the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) that are compatible with limiting global warming to 1.5 °C by the end of this century.

In particular, the analysis is based on mitigation scenarios classified as C1, which represent global pathways with a probability greater than 50% of not exceeding this threshold by the end of the century, and with a transient and limited overshoot of temperature above 1.5 °C.
There are other scenarios, classified as C2, also compatible with 1.5 ºC, but involve a more prolonged temperature overshoot.
The C1 scenarios envisage a global reduction in greenhouse gas emissions of 40–50% in 2030 compared with 2019, with steeper declines in coal (≈ 75%) and smaller declines in oil and gas (≈ 10%).

Of the total of 97 IPCC C1 scenarios, those that achieve a net reduction of more than 95% of emissions from the energy sector by 2050 (19 scenarios) show a median reduction in greenhouse gas emissions from oil and gas of 14% in 2030.
Similar results are observed when restricting the analysis to the nine scenarios with assumptions that Repsol considers more realistic in terms of technological availability and carbon costs.

Meanwhile, the International Energy Agency’s (IEA) Net Zero Emissions (NZE) scenario, updated in the WEO 2025 and aligned with a limit of 1.5 °C13 and emissions neutrality by 2050, does not project a specific data for 2030.

By interpolation, a reduction of 22% in oil and gas is estimated for 2030 compared with 2019.14 From this analysis, it can be concluded that Repsol’s goal of achieving a 20% reduction in absolute Scope 1, 2 and 3 emissions by 2030 compared with 2018, which covers more than 91% of the Company’s total emissions, falls within the range of the set of IPCC and IEA scenarios compatible with limiting global warming to 1.5 °C by the end of this century and achieving net zero emissions by 2050.

A I II Table of conversions and abbreviations Alternative performance measures III Risks IV V VI ≡ Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information Annual Corporate Governance Report Annual Report on Director Remuneration Qualitative analysis of locked-in emissions Calculations of potential locked-in emissions associated with the main assets and products marketed by Repsol are based on an estimate of future GHG emissions over their useful life.

Oil and gas products could be subject to drastic demand reductions over the long term that would affect the value of the Company assets that produce them and could eventually require halting its activity, leaving the assets and their emissions “locked in”.

In its climate transition risks and resilience analysis, Repsol classifies the risk associated with potential locked-in emissions as significant and manages its portfolio of assets to strengthen its resilience to this risk.

The resilience of the assets for the production, transformation and sale of oil and gas products has been assessed, including both the emissions generated directly by those that are operated (Scope 1 and 2) and those derived from the use of the products sold (Scope 3).

The risk of locked-in emissions is taken into account when setting decarbonization targets: • With respect to assets, Scope 1 and 2 emissions from the facilities operated are part of the reduction trajectory detailed in Targets related to climate change mitigation and adaptation (E1-4).

• Regarding products, of the 20% reduction target for Scope 1, 2 and 3 emissions compared with the 2018 baseline year, 17.4% of the reduction corresponds to emissions associated with the use of the products sold.

The analysis of the Company’s future emissions is classified by business: Upstream or Downstream: Upstream The Company’s strategy is focused on optimizing its portfolio of exploration and production assets, giving priority to those with higher profitability and lower carbon intensity, with a greater geographical concentration to gain scale.

Priority is given to projects for the development of discovered reserves and resources that are profitable at moderate oil and gas prices and exploration adjacent to existing assets and infrastructure, excluding new exploratory frontiers, tar sands and the Arctic offshore.
As a result, Scope 1 and 2 emissions have already been reduced, reaching an intensity that is now within the first quartile of the sector.

This intensity will continue to decline in the coming years, with projections of reaching 10 kgCO2e/boe for 2027.15 For the Upstream business, Scope 3 emissions derive from the use of hydrocarbon production.
With the strategy described, Repsol’s production will remain at levels of 550-600 kboed throughout this decade, after having fallen from a peak of 709 kboed in 2019.

From 2035, a gradual reduction is expected reaching around 100 kboed in 2050 due to the natural decline of assets without undertaking new long-term developments in scenarios compatible with the warming limited to 1.5 ºC.

Downstream Most of Repsol’s downstream assets are located in Spain and Portugal, where the European regulatory framework on climate matters applies, in line with the European Green Deal and its implementation through the Fit for 55 legislative package with emission reduction targets for 2030, aimed at achieving climate neutrality by 2050.

Within this framework, Repsol manages the locked-in emissions of the products sold (Scope 3, category 1116) by progressively reducing refining activity from 2030 and the gradual replacement of conventional products with low-carbon products such as renewable liquid and gaseous fuels and synthetic fuels.

The EU regulation also promotes the reduction of Scope 1 and 2 emissions through greater energy efficiency, the electrification of processes with renewable energies and, in the longer term, through the capture, use and storage of CO2, or the trend to reduce the processing of crude oil in refineries.

Material impacts, risks and opportunities and their interaction with strategy and the business model (ESRS 2 SBM-3) The resilience analysis carried out in 2025 covers all the Company’s own operations, in all segments of the value chain where it operates, and in its four business lines (Upstream, Industrial, Customer, and Low-Carbon Generation).

The Company’s ability to adapt to the progressive decarbonization of the energy system in different transition scenarios is assessed, promoting operational continuity, economic competitiveness and sustainable development in all business areas.
This study has as its starting point the analysis of transition and physical risks17 from the double materiality assessment.

However, given that this analysis concludes that exposure to physical risks derived from climate change is not material in any of the scenarios and time horizons analyzed, (as they do not generate significant impacts on the operational continuity or economic competitiveness of the Company’s key assets), these physical risks have not been included in the scope of the resilience analysis.

Although they are based on the same scenarios and assumptions, resilience analysis is a scenario analysis, so it does not assess each risk individually, but rather analyzes in an integrated manner the capacity for adaptation and value creation of the Company’s businesses under different rhythms of the energy transition, based on aggregate variables, such as demand, prices or margins, with an effect on the projection of economic-financial metrics.

The analysis covers the period between the present and 2050, in correspondence with the temporal scope of the climate risk analysis and the Company’s decarbonization objectives.
Three reference climate scenarios from the expert consultancy S&P Energy updated in 2025 have been selected, which are applied differently in each business as explained in the following paragraphs.

These scenarios, recognized in the sector, unlike other reference scenarios, contain production, demand and price projections of different energy sources and products with sufficient granularity at the level of geography, seasonality and raw material/product to allow for developing a more complete and consistent analysis.

The results of the analysis show the performance of the different businesses, as detailed below, as well as the Company’s resilience to risks arising from changes in the pace of the energy transition: • In an environment for Industrial and Customer businesses determined by the European Green Deal and the Fit for 55 legislative package, investments are envisaged in renewable fuel projects that preserve their economic value, where they are currently positioned in the first quartile within the EU and with the largest market share in Spain.

• The Upstream business maintains its value‑creation capacity well into the next decade, and subsequently sees it decline in line with the reduction in production and the decrease in prices assumed due to lower global demand, with varying intensity under each scenario.

• Meanwhile, the Low-Carbon Generation and Electricity Distribution businesses increase their capacity to create value over time due to the growth and investment strategy proposed and the trend towards electrification in the scenarios posed.

The resilience analysis carried out, which projects the performance of businesses based on the assumptions of the scenarios used, shows that the proposed strategy is resilient in the event of changes in the pace of the energy transition.

As part of the resilience analysis, the capital allocation required under each of the long‑term scenarios considered has been estimated, as well as the proportion that should be directed to low‑carbon businesses.
The following material impacts have been identified in relation to climate change: • Repsol’s sale of the fossil fuels demanded by the market generates indirect emissions of CO2 (Scope 3) (IRO-9).

• Impact on global warming (IRO-4) of Repsol’s activities in the areas of Upstream and Industrial, as well as those related to mobility, which generate Scope 1 and 2 (direct company operations) and Scope 3 (mobility and supply chain) emissions.

• Repsol’s operating model involves an intensive use of energy from non-renewable sources, which contributes significantly to its direct greenhouse gases emissions (IRO-1) and can generate exposure to variations in energy costs and an increasingly demanding regulatory environment.

• Repsol’s leadership position in the market can serve as an example for other companies and institutions when adopting their energy transition measures (IRO-5), promoting a shift toward more sustainable operations and contributing to the reduction of greenhouse gas emissions and to collective progress toward decarbonization.
• Commitment and awareness of the workforce in terms of decarbonization due to the link of their variable remuneration to this target (IRO-6).

• Repsol’s renewable energy services, from wind, solar and hydro sources, contribute positively to the energy market (IRO-2) by increasing the supply and consumption of renewable energy, thus favoring the reduction of greenhouse gas emissions and the move towards a low-carbon energy model.

In relation to financial materiality, the Group’s general risk map, focused on a five-year period, is complemented by a specific analysis in the medium (2030–2040) and long-term (2040–2050), for these risks, including an estimate of their economic impact, in order to anticipate potential negative impacts and take advantage of opportunities.
Repsol assesses the physical risks of climate change and the risks of the energy transition separately through the methodologies described below.

Physical risk analysis of climate change In 2022, Repsol developed its own, semi-quantitative methodology for the detailed analysis of the physical risks of climate change, both in existing facilities and in investment projects subject to the Taxonomy Regulation.
These analyses are complemented, from 2024, by quantitative analyses of a broader scope, including both operated and non-operated facilities, carried out by an expert consultancy.
First, an identification is made of the climatic phenomena to which Repsol’s assets are potentially exposed because they are located in certain geographical locations.
The starting point is the list of phenomena offered by the current regulations, discarding those that, in the opinion of the experts, are less relevant to Repsol’s assets.
The following natural phenomena are included: 1.
Extreme wind (storms, tornadoes) 6.

A I II Table of conversions and abbreviations Alternative performance measures III Risks IV V VI ≡ Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information Annual Corporate Governance Report Annual Report on Director Remuneration The climate projections of certain physical variables related mainly to temperature, precipitation and wind speed that are associated with these phenomena have been studied.

The climate projections that have been used are, among others, those from Copernicus services (the EU’s Earth observation program, coordinated and managed by the European Commission) as well as external consultancy services that provide additional information on the behavior of these physical variables and their combinations.

In addition, account has been taken of the global warming scenarios described by the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC), with a wide range of emissions (SSP5-8.5, SSP2-4.5 and SSP1-2.6 scenarios) selected in the 2030, 2040 and 2050 time horizons.

Based on this information, simulations have been carried out to determine the potential increase in the impact on the Company’s facilities resulting of the increase in the frequency or intensity of these phenomena, with an estimate made of the economic value at risk for each of them in the different assets.

This study, carried out in the absence of mitigation or adaptation measures, is first carried out on a representative sample of the assets in the current portfolio of all the Company’s businesses, which is then extrapolated to other assets that are compatible in location and typology.
In addition, more detailed analyses of assets subject to the European taxonomy regulation have been carried out.
These studies were updated in 2025, including changes in the asset portfolio resulting from the Company’s investments and divestments during the last year.
The conclusion of these studies is that the increase in risk from acute or chronic climate change events is not material18 for the Repsol Group in any of the analyzed scenarios or time horizons.
Risk analysis of the energy transition Transition risks and opportunities are important, especially in the medium and long term, which is why Repsol reviews them on an annual basis.
The objective of this process is to identify, evaluate and make sure that these risks are known and taken into account for decision-making through resilience analysis and the Company’s strategy.

In the short term, these risks are part of the Company’s risk map, with a time range of five years, and are studied using quantitative techniques (determination of frequencies, probabilities and economic losses for each activity) and qualitative techniques (for the determination of reputational and people impacts), using the expert knowledge of the different areas of the Company.
In the medium and long term, a specific analysis of the transition risks is carried out for each of the four major business areas.

To this end, a taxonomy has been defined following the guidelines of the CSRD Directive, which includes regulatory, legal, technological, market and reputational risks, up to a total of 13 risks classified according to their nature, that could impact the Company across the different time horizons considered.
The most significant risks are identified and a level of probability and impact is assessed for each of them, through qualitative scales, as well as their duration and evolution over time.

Subsequently, these assessments are verified with other experts of the Company in the fields of strategy, markets, regulation, finance, reputation, legal affairs, technology and sustainability, in order to obtain a common vision on the potential consequences of the materialization of risks for the Company in the horizons 2030–2040 and 2040–2050.

These analyses are carried out taking as a reference the projections of the latest update of the Strategic Plan announced by the Company, as well as the decarbonization route, and as the environmental scenario the NZE scenario of S&P Energy20, aligned with limiting global warming to 1.5 ºC with limited overshoot, which enables the determination of the residual risk to which the Company would be exposed in these horizons and scenarios.

In addition, an analysis of the transition risks is carried out in the absence of mitigation measures, that is, without considering specific investments or decarbonization objectives, which allows for assessing the impact of the Company’s Strategic Plan.
The risk analysis indicates that the exposure to material economic impacts from the energy transition is moderate in this decade.

Policies related to climate change mitigation and adaptation (E1-2) The management of the impacts, risks, and opportunities arising from climate change is included in the Sustainability Policy, which establishes as priority objectives the promotion of a fair energy transition that takes into account its impact on workers, local communities and society in general while reducing and avoiding greenhouse gas emissions, and the transition to low-carbon business models that reduce or mitigate the adverse effects of climate change and integrate sustainability into all businesses.
21 Risks analyzed under a residual approach.
In this policy, Repsol also takes on the challenge of contributing to universal access to safe, affordable and sustainable energy.
Accordingly, it emphasizes that all reduction technologies will be necessary to achieve this goal, so that the energy transition is carried out in an orderly manner and at the lowest cost to society.

A I II Table of conversions and abbreviations Alternative performance measures III Risks IV V VI ≡ Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information Annual Corporate Governance Report Annual Report on Director Remuneration which underscores the need to take account of stakeholder expectations and to “provide energy and products to society to meet its needs efficiently, guaranteeing adequate protection of the environment and the sustainable use of resources without compromising the well-being of future generations”.

In this policy, the Company commits to identifying, evaluating, and managing, in accordance with the mitigation22 hierarchy, the impacts, dependencies, risks, and opportunities on the environment, in the activities and places where Repsol operates, throughout the value chain, as well as in the products and services offered to society.
Repsol is also committed to assessing the risks associated with climate change and implementing climate change mitigation and adaptation measures in its operations when such risks are material.

The Environmental Policy also includes Repsol’s commitment to integrate the principles of the circular economy into its processes, products, and services, optimizing the use of resources and raw materials, including energy; encouraging the use of alternative and renewable resources obtained in a sustainable way; and promoting the development of new products with less environmental impact.
This applies to all the activities carried out by the Company, all its products, all its businesses and all the locations where it operates.

In addition, Repsol undertakes to promote environmental commitments in accordance with those defined internally throughout the value chain, customers, partners, suppliers of products and services, and other stakeholders.

The commitments included in this document, together with the Sustainability Policy, contribute to achieving the Company’s goal of net zero emissions by 2050, in line with the Paris Agreement, as well as the other decarbonization objectives specified in the transition plan.
The Sustainability Department ensures the implementation of the Sustainability and Environment policies.

Actions and resources in relation to climate change policies (E1-3) Below are the actions that Repsol has implemented in 2025 to mitigate climate change and its impact in 2025, along with forecasts for 2030, as well as the resources allocated for their execution.

The information covers different lines of action depending on the scope of the emissions (1, 2 and 3) and the nature of the measures, considering both current investments and future projections (see Investments).

Energy efficiency23 In the industrial field, which includes the complexes located in Spain, Portugal and Peru within the value chain downstream, measures are implemented such as the cleaning of heat exchangers, the optimization of energy integration, the use of heat from high-temperature streams, the improvement of furnace efficiency and the optimization of steam networks.
Further, at Upstream, which covers assets in various geographies within the upstream value chain, notable initiatives include reducing methane emissions and minimizing routine flaring of gas.

• Reduction of intermediate diesel reprocessing at the Puertollano refinery.

Use of renewables25 Renewable electricity is a lever for decarbonization of Scope 1 emissions, if it replaces electricity generation in cogeneration; and Scope 2, if it replaces the purchase of electricity on the market that continues to have a fossil component.

Scope 3 emissions Repsol’s Scope 3 emissions are mostly associated with the use of the energy products it markets (petroleum- based fuels and natural gas), so their reduction requires action on the volume and mix of sales, in line with energy transition scenarios and European regulation.

Within the framework of the Scope 1, 2 and 3 emissions reduction target, the Company combines portfolio management decisions, evolution of the commercial offer and growth in lower carbon intensity solutions to progressively reduce emissions derived from the use of its products.

This regulatory environment sets the trend with respect to the evolution of the transport sector and the associated demand for fuels.

Nonetheless, this demand shows notable resilience in the Iberian Peninsula in the short term, with stable projections throughout this decade and point to a progressive decrease in the demand for conventional fossil fuels in the medium and long term, with the consequent increase in renewable fuels for all mobility and electric mobility, mainly in light road transport.

2.1.4 Metrics and targets Targets related to climate change mitigation and adaptation (E1-4) In 2024, Repsol set a new absolute emissions reduction target for Scopes 1, 2 and 3 as part of its strategy to address climate risks and opportunities, thus further attesting to its alignment with the global targets of limiting warming to 1.5 °C and net-zero greenhouse gas emissions by 2050.
This target is a response, to a large extent, to the demands of certain interest groups and the CSRD Directive.

Its approach is complementary to the CO2e emissions reduction target per unit of energy generated (carbon intensity) set in 2019, and to other specific targets for reducing Scope 1 and 2 emissions, methane and routine flare emissions.

The following describes the metrics that the Company uses to assess the progress of impacts, risks, and opportunities (IROs) related to the energy transition and decarbonization (see Materiality assessment).
All decarbonization goals are consistent with the Company’s business and operational projections, which are shared with stakeholders in their strategic updates.

In addition, they contribute directly to the objectives of the Sustainability Policy and the Environment Policy, directing actions towards optimizing the use of resources, improving energy efficiency and achieving climate neutrality.
(4) "Near Zero" methane intensity is defined as below 0.2%, according to the formulation identified by OGCI.

Absolute Scope 1, 2 and 3 emissions (MtCO2e)4,30 Repsol uses this metric31 of absolute greenhouse gas emissions of Scopes 1, 2 and 3, to show its progress in decarbonization and as the form most accepted by certain stakeholders, in the absence of a recognized standard, to demonstrate its alignment with the global objectives of limiting warming to 1.5 °C and achieving net-zero GHG emissions by 2050, as detailed in Transition plan for climate change mitigation (E1-1).
By 2030, Repsol has set a goal to reduce absolute Scope 1, 2 and 3 GHG emissions by 20%, compared with the 201832 baseline, with an estimated contribution of 5% in Scopes 1 and 2 and 15% in Scope 3.
Based on current projections, the Company estimates a reduction of more than 90% in absolute emissions with the mitigation levers and technologies currently envisaged.
The remaining percentage, around 10%, is expected to be addressed through greenhouse gas removal and abatement solutions, including carbon capture and storage technologies and natural sinks.

Carbon Intensity Indicator (gCO2e/MJ)24 In 2019, Repsol defined the CII in gCO2e/MJ.

This metric is calculated by adding Scope 1 and 2 (operational) and Scope 3 greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions (based on products obtained from Repsol’s oil and gas production) in the numerator, and the energy generated by the Company in the denominator (see Methodology for climate change reporting and metrics).
In the CII numerator, approximately 37% of the Company’s Scope 3 emissions and 41% of total emissions are covered, as the Company’s oil product sales are higher than its oil production33.
The denominator of the CII represents the energy that Repsol makes available to society as final products derived from the production of primary energy from oil and gas and low-carbon energy sources.
By 2050, the goal is to reduce the carbon intensity of the primary energy we produce to net zero, with interim targets in 2025, 2030 and 2040.

The CII contributes directly to Sustainability and Environmental Policies by offering an integrated and quantifiable metric that guides the Company’s progress towards net-zero GHG emissions, along with the Scope 1, 2 and 3 absolute emissions metric.
This indicator reinforces the commitment to reduce GHG emissions (Scopes 1, 2 and 3), aligning with the principles of continuous improvement set out in the Environmental Policy.

In addition, the CII facilitates the strategic management of our energy transition by providing a tool to assess the impact of decarbonization actions that are not identifiable through the reduction of absolute emissions (renewable power generation, renewable fuels), as noted, which supports the Sustainability Policy by prioritizing the integration of the energy transition and climate resilience into the Company’s operations.
In October 2021, the CII reduction targets were set at 15% for 2025, 28% for 2030, 55% for 2040 and 100% for 205034, compared to the base year of 2016.
These objectives were ratified in the February 2024 strategic update.

Over the last few months, and after meeting all of its decarbonization targets for 2025, Repsol has reviewed certain metrics and objectives from the latest strategic update (which are included in this report) in order to adapt them to the better understanding of the global energy context, regulation and demand trends, and the pace of the energy transition.
And it does so without departing from the path of decarbonization toward net- zero GHG emissions by 2050, and undertaking investments that are profitable in the current environment.
By 2030, as a result, the reduction target for the CII has been set at 25% ±1.
In 2025, the CII stood at 65.2 gCO₂e/MJ, representing a 15.1% reduction compared to 2016 (13.4% in 2024), thus meeting the 15% reduction target.

Absolute Scope 1 and 2 emissions (MtCO2e)24 These are the direct emissions from facilities in which Repsol is the operator (Scope 1) and the indirect emissions related to the consumption of electricity, heat or steam acquired for Repsol’s operations (Scope 2), where the Company has the greatest capacity to undertake decarbonization actions.
This metric is detailed in Methodology for climate change reporting and metrics.
84% of Repsol’s Scope 1 and 2 emissions fall under the scope of the EU ETS, which requires external verification, carried out after the publication of this report.
Additionally, 94% of Scope 1 and 2 emissions are certified under the ISO 14064 standard.
In 2021, the Company pledged to reduce these emissions by 55% by 2030 compared with 2016.

The base year, 2016, was selected in 2019 (the year the Company’s goal of net zero emissions 2050 was announced) as the baseline for this target due to its historical significance: it represents the first full year Scope 1 and 2 emission reduction levers following the acquisition of Talisman Energy, which significantly increased CO2 and methane emissions from the Company’s Upstream business and Scope 3 emissions.
In 2016, Repsol’s total Scope 1 and 2 emissions amounted to 25.4 MtCO₂e.

In 2023, Repsol reaffirmed its goal of reaching net zero Scope 1 and 2 emissions in its operated assets by 2050, adding to the commitment of the Oil and Gas Decarbonization Charter (OGDC), announced during COP28.
By 2030, it is estimated that most of the reduction in these emissions will be Scope 1, although a reduction in Scope 2 emissions of 200 ktCO2e compared to 2025.
These reductions also contribute to the objective of reducing absolute emissions of Scopes 1, 2 and 3, with Scopes 1 and 2 representing 8% of the total.
The reduction in absolute Scope 1 and 2 emissions in 2025 was 45.3% compared with 2016.

Routine flare emissions (tCO2e)24 This metric35 is based on the methodology applied by OGCI (Oil and & Gas Climate Initiative), in accordance with the 2023 reporting framework36.
This indicator is applied to upstream oil and gas assets operated by Repsol.
Repsol set the target of reducing methane intensity to 0.20% by 2025 in its operated Exploration and Production assets, with 2017 as the baseline.
This objective has become an international standard recognized by industry and other significant stakeholders (UNEP, EDF, CATF, European Commission).
In 2025, the methane intensity was 0.12%, reaching the target set.
Work will continue in order to improve the identification, quantification and reduction of methane losses by 2030.
Gas shipments to the flares (flaring) generate CO₂, CH₄, N₂O and water vapor in their combustion (see Methodology for climate change reporting and metrics).
The emissions reduction metric is applied to the Upstream oil and gas assets operated by Repsol.
In 2025, routine flaring emissions accounted for less than 0.3% of the Company’s Scope 1 and 2 emissions inventory.
However, its significance, like that of methane, is particularly significant because it helps minimize product losses and improve operational excellence and safety.

Since 2016, Repsol has been a member of the World Bank’s Zero Routine Flaring by 2030 initiative37, through which it is committed to seeking technically and economically viable solutions to minimize routine flaring of gas as soon as possible and no later than 2030.

Repsol accounts for the emissions of these assets up to the effective date of their sale.

In addition, and in order to be able to compare with the 2024 data, the most significant changes during the year are specified: • Divestment in the UK: In July 2025, the strategic union between Repsol Resources UK and NEO Energy is completed.
The joint venture has been renamed NEO NEXT.
This change in the portfolio has an impact of -0.5 MtCO2e on the operated emissions.
• The combined cycle plants of the Low-Carbon Generation business have increased activity notably following the blackout that occurred in Spain in April.
This has resulted in an increase of +0.8 MtCO2e.

In addition, energy efficiency measures, methane emission reductions and reduction of gas flaring have been implemented in Upstream assets, supplemented by optimization initiatives in the Industrial business.

A I II Table of conversions and abbreviations Alternative performance measures III Risks IV V VI ≡ Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information Annual Corporate Governance Report Annual Report on Director Remuneration GHG removals and GHG mitigation projects financed through carbon credits (E1-7) Currently, the Company does not have any tons of CO2 stored that are derived from CO2 removal projects from the atmosphere implemented in its own operations or in which it has contributed in upstream and downstream stages of its value chain.

As part of its commitment to the goal of net zero emissions of Scopes 1, 2 and 3, Repsol envisages that up to 8% of these emissions (around 20 MtCO2e) cannot be eliminated by 2050 through technologies applied to its energy39 production, in which case it would offset with its own natural climate solutions projects (NCS) or through credits.

These credits would be aligned with the most demanding integrity frameworks, such as Article 6 of the Paris Agreement and/or the European Union’s CRCF, guaranteeing transparency, additionality, permanence and methodological rigor.
Repsol undertakes to report in a clear and transparent manner any possible use of these credits.
In any case, Repsol’s decarbonization strategy prioritizes avoiding and reducing its own emissions and those in its value chain through technological development and operational efficiency.
At least until after 2030, Repsol will not use emission offset mechanisms.
Notwithstanding the foregoing, Repsol has implemented commercial initiatives that allow its customers to voluntarily contribute to the mitigation of their carbon emissions.

Although these credits finance projects that capture or avoid CO2 emissions, such as forestry projects, their use is not linked to Repsol’s40 global decarbonization strategy, but is offered as an additional and voluntary option for customers who wish to contribute to the mitigation of emissions that come from the use of our products.

In 2025, the Company has cancelled 20,340 tCO2 in the form of international carbon credits to cover the demand for these initiatives, entirely of the AFOLU, REDD+ type and verified by Verra’s41 VCS42 (Verified Carbon Standard), with additional CCB+ Gold certification43.
It should be noted that these credits are not used to offset GHG emissions under the E1-6 requirement, nor as a means to achieve the Company’s emissions reduction goals outlined in the E1-4.

Internal carbon pricing (E1-8) Repsol has internal mechanisms to promote the allocation of capital to low-carbon investments, such as the internal carbon price and the investment qualification methodology for their alignment with the energy transition.
The Company has established an internal carbon price for investment decisions for new projects.
This mechanism is included in the Company’s economic bases for investment evaluation and is applied in the investment decisions of all projects.
The internal carbon price applies to the analysis of new investments on the potential impact of the Company’s Scope 1 and 2 emissions even where there is no regulated carbon price.
It acts as a shadow price by incentivizing emissions reductions and investment in low-carbon technologies, so that a higher carbon price promotes projects with lower emissions over the rest.

The internal carbon price in the scope of EU operations is established based on the current price and future forecasts for the European Union Emissions Trading Scheme (EU ETS), which is currently set at around $92 /t in 2026 and $110/t in 2030 in nominal terms.
This price is aligned with the EU ETS price path used by the Company in its financial statements to perform the asset impairment test.
See Note 3.5.1 Estimate of the recoverable value of assets and Note 6 on asset impairment of the Consolidated Financial Statements.
For the rest of the world, in countries without more stringent specific regulation, an internal carbon price of $60/tCO2e is applied in the period 2026-2030.

This price applies mainly to Upstream projects and, when it refers to methane emission reduction and routine flaring projects, it especially facilitates investment, since the real cost of abatement is often less than $60/tCO2e.
Whenever an investment decision is made, the new project is analyzed with respect to the effect it could have on the Company’s direct emissions perimeter.
To this end, the internal carbon price is used in the valuation of any investment that may have an impact on the Company’s Scope 1 and 2 (which currently corresponds to a volume of 13.9 MtCO2e).

Scenario selection: The C1 family of scenarios published in the IIASA (International Institute for Applied Systems Analysis) database has been taken as a reference, which groups the trajectories with a probability greater than 50% of limiting global warming to 1.5 °C by 2100, without exceeding this threshold or doing so in a limited way (≤ 0.1 °C) during a transitional period.
This family is distinguished from others (C2– C7) by its level of climate ambition and its compatibility with the objectives of the Paris Agreement.

The C1 scenarios include emissions from all sectors—energy, industry, transportation, AFOLU, and land use—and all primary sources (coal, oil, gas, nuclear, and renewables), allowing for a comprehensive analysis of global decarbonization.

Statistical treatment and reference analysis: From the set of 97 C1 scenarios, the median of the projected reduction of oil and gas emissions to 2030 has been calculated, considering this as the most robust trend measure against extreme values or asymmetric distributions.
The median has been preferred over the arithmetic mean, as it offers greater stability in the face of the dispersion between underlying models and methodologies.

The dispersion analysis shows an interquartile range (Q1–Q3) of –25% to –11% and an absolute deviation of less than 9%, which confirms the statistical consistency of the whole and the representativeness of the median as a reference for the evaluation of corporate objectives.
Filtering Neutrality-Compatible Scenarios: Not all C1 scenarios are consistent with net zero in 2050.

Therefore, the subset of 19 C1 scenarios that achieve a reduction of more than 95% of emissions from the global energy system by 2050, considered compatible with net zero emissions trajectories, has been identified.

From this subset, a second filter has been applied to rule out assumptions considered technologically extreme or of low plausibility, including: • Scenarios with more than 1.5 Gt of CO₂ captured and stored (CCS) in 2030.
• Scenarios with high dependence on nuclear energy in the energy matrix.
• Scenarios with carbon prices above USD 750/tCO₂ in 2050, given their disproportionate impact on the economic and social viability of the transition.

The application of these filters has resulted in a final set of 9 representative scenarios, on which the median reduction in oil and gas emissions for 2030 has been recalculated, remaining around –14% compared to 2019.

Cross-validation and external consistency: As a verification exercise, the consistency of the results has been contrasted with the NZE (Net Zero Emissions by 2050) scenario prepared by the International Energy Agency (IEA), which is deterministic in nature and widely used as a sectoral reference.

This scenario, updated in the World Energy Outlook 2025, projects global reductions in oil and gas emissions of 22% and coal emissions of 32% by 2030 compared to 2019, reinforcing the consistency of the median obtained from the IPCC’s probabilistic scenarios.

Methodological conclusion: The methodology used combines scientific selection criteria (probability of meeting the 1.5 °C target), statistical consistency (median and interquartile intervals) and technological and economic plausibility (CCS technologies, nuclear and carbon prices).
This approach ensures that the comparison between corporate objectives and the trajectories of the IPCC is made on a scientifically grounded basis.

Absolute Scope 1, 2 and 3 emissions This metric includes: • Scope 1 and 2 (on an operational basis): Direct and indirect emissions from assets operated by the Company are accounted for following the GHG Protocol, considering the assets operated by the Company.
Repsol applies the operational control approach, including 100% of the emissions from the operations under its control, regardless of its shareholding or financial participation in such operations.
Emissions arising from non-operated Joint Operating Agreements (JOA) assets are not included and account for 0.7 MtCO2e in 2025.
• Scope 3, Category 11 (GHG Protocol): These are the emissions from the use of the fossil products sold.

A I II Table of conversions and abbreviations Alternative performance measures III Risks IV V VI ≡ Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information Annual Corporate Governance Report Annual Report on Director Remuneration M More precisely, the CII numerator includes: • Operational Scope 1 and 2: Direct and indirect emissions from Upstream, Refining and Chemical assets, as well as from the non-renewable electricity generation operated by Repsol.
For Scope 2 emissions, the market-based approach corresponding to the energy mix of the suppliers that supply steam and electricity is used.
• Scope 3: Emissions derived from the use of products that can be obtained from Repsol’s oil and gas production.
In the case of oil production, the yields in products are those of its own refining and chemical processes (category 11- upstream production based).
For the production of natural gas, all emissions resulting from its combustion (category 11) are counted.

In addition, emissions from third-party hydrogen plants supplying the Company's refineries (category 1- hydrogen purchases) are included, as well as emissions from final disposal after the use of chemicals (category 12).
• Carbon sinks: Emissions stored through carbon capture, use and storage managed by the Company.

Repsol’s decarbonization strategy does not rely on carbon offsets from natural-based solutions or carbon markets, except to address residual emissions once all available energy decarbonization options have been exhausted.
Carbon offsets have not been included in our decarbonization path for this decade and are only contemplated in the long term to offset residual emissions in order to reach carbon neutrality by 2050.
As for the denominator of the CII, it includes the following: • Energy products (Upstream production): It includes the energy contained in the production of oil and natural gas.
For oil, since we operate an integrated value chain, the energy of the products that can be obtained in our refining and chemical production scheme is included.
For chemicals and other non-energy products (such as lubricants and asphalt, among others), the energy contained in the oil used in their production is counted.

In the case of natural gas, given that we do not have an integrated value chain until final commercialization and that there is no net purchase of gas from third parties, the energy contained in the gas produced is included.

• Low-carbon energy sources: It includes energy produced from renewable electricity generation sources (solar, wind and hydro), energy from natural gas used in combined cycles and surplus cogeneration, as well as energy from renewable fuels (renewable hydrogen, biomethane, biofuels and synthetic fuels).

Renewable electrical energy is accounted for by the substitution method, as the equivalent amount of non-renewable energy needed to generate the same electricity in a conventional thermal plant, thus allowing consistent and comparable assessments of energy contributions.

For greater homogeneity of the financial information by business segments and non-financial segments, the calculation of both energy production and Scope 3 emissions is prepared using the same scope of entities and assets used by the Company, namely: • Companies/assets controlled by Repsol: 100% of energy production and Scope 3 emissions (category 11) are accounted for.
METHODOLOGY • Companies/assets under joint control: Energy production and Scope 3 emissions (category 11) are accounted for in proportion to Repsol’s percentage of ownership.

Interests in companies/assets in which the Company does not have control or joint control, constituting a mere financial interest: Energy production and Scope 3 emissions (category 11) are not counted.
As of today, Repsol does not have assets under this category that should be considered for the calculation of the CII.

Absolute Scope 1 and 2 emissions The perimeter of this target follows the operational criteria established by the GHG Protocol44, which entails the inclusion of all business units, assets and operations in which Repsol has direct control over management and/or operational responsibility, accounting for 100% of the emissions generated.
Emissions corresponding to non-operated JOAs are excluded from this objective.
Emissions are calculated using recognized methodologies, which are reported in Gross Scope 1, 2 and 3 and total GHG emissions (E1-6).

To reinforce the robustness and transparency of this data, Repsol collaborates with external organizations that provide methodologies and technical validation, such as Oil&Gas Methane Partnership (OGMP), which supports best practices in the measurement and reporting of methane emissions.
These external validations, together with international standards alignment, reinforce the operational excellence and credibility of the Company’s emissions reporting.
This metric is validated annually by an external auditor according to the ISO 14064 standard, covering more than 90% of Scope 1 and 2 inventory in this verification.
The coverage of this verification is subject to different portfolio changes throughout the year.

Methane intensity As most of the methane emissions from production operations come from natural gas production, this metric accounts for gas production in the denominator, while the numerator includes emissions associated with both gas and oil production.

Repsol’s methane emissions represent 4% of equivalent Scope 1 and 2 GHG emissions, but its relevance lies on the operational excellence, which should aim to reduce methane losses to practically zero, with the consequent environmental and economic benefit.

Emissions are accounted for "from the wellhead to the point of sale," in line with the IEA’s outlook for natural gas, which includes oil and gas production, as well as natural gas gathering and processing.
The methane intensity metric is, in sum, the quotient between methane emissions and natural gas output at the point of sale, both expressed in volume and as a percentage.

Additionally, the numerator calculation (m3 CH4) complies with the OGMP methodology by implementing innovative quantification technologies that have been validated by UNEP, having awarded Repsol the Gold Standard.
For more information on this methodology, see the technical guides on the OGMP website.
44 The GHG Protocol guides for Scope 1, 2 and 3 can be found in Corporate Standard; Scope 2; Scope 3.

Routine flaring emissions Metrics related to energy production Direct measurements of gas volume are made using flowmeters and sensors installed in flare systems in all operations.
The calculation of emissions is based on standardized formulas and factors, detailed in the Company’s internal emissions guidance.

For the segregation of emissions derived from routine operations and, therefore, likely to be reduced with more efficient operating strategies, the Company applies the definitions of the Global Flaring and Methane Reduction Partnership (GFMR) technical guide, which defines the different types of flaring in crude oil assets.
Energy consumption metrics The calculation of the energy consumed is obtained by multiplying the consumption of each fuel by its lower calorific value.
This comes from specific analyses of each fuel or from reference guides (IPCC, API and Concawe).
The bases for the calculation of energy and the scope are similar to those of section E1-6, where the origin of the emission sources and their calculation methodology are developed.

The energy consumed that is generated by third parties (Scope 2) is calculated from the purchase invoices and includes: • Steam consumed from outside: In the case of Repsol, it has fossil origins since it is generated in hydrogen production facilities by reforming natural gas or other hydrocarbons.
• Electricity consumed from outside: Depending on the marketer and the type of contract, this energy can be fossil, renewable or nuclear.

The consumption of electricity, heat, steam and cooling purchased or acquired from fossil sources corresponds to that indicated on the supplier's invoice, except for assets that consume electricity directly from the grid for which the electricity mix is used according to the geographical area.

Nuclear energy consumption corresponds to that indicated on the supplier’s invoice, except for assets that consume electricity directly from the grid for which the electricity mix is used according to the geographical area.

The consumption of electricity, heat, steam and cooling purchased or acquired from renewable sources corresponds to that indicated on the marketer’s bill, including the business units and assets that consume 100% renewable electricity, except for assets that consume electricity directly from the grid, for which the electricity mix is used according to the geographical area.
For all the cases described, the type of energy consumed is related to the supply contract of each asset.
This will depend on whether there are Power Purchase Agreements (PPAs) or Guarantees of Origin (GoO) certificates.

If there are none, the residual or "remaining" emission factor (where the energy generated and sold specifically with GoOs is discounted) of the marketer (or of the country in the case of Direct Market Consumer assets) will be used.

The energy consumption data for non-operated JOAs is estimated from 2024 emissions using emission factors corresponding to the geographical areas where they are located and using the production ratio between 2025 and 2024.
Non-renewable energy production includes energy generated at Repsol's assets in the Industrial, Exploration and Production, and combined cycle power plant areas.
Renewable energy production comprises energy generated from wind farms, solar plants, hydroelectric power stations, and renewable fuels produced at industrial facilities.

Non-combustible renewable energy is expressed in terms of equivalent fossil energy, considering the average efficiency of a conventional thermal power plant to allow comparability with the Company’s other energy outputs.
This procedure is the so-called substitution method, widely used and recognized by organizations outside the sector45.
GHG emissions Scope 1 and 2 emissions are those derived from operations and processes at the Company’s facilities.

These emissions can be classified into: Direct emissions (Scope 1) Those emitted directly at Repsol facilities, from the following sources: • By fuel consumption: The combustion reaction of any fuel results in heat, CO2, CH4, N2O, and water vapor.
They make up the bulk of Scope 1 emissions.
These emissions are calculated from direct measurements, such as fuel consumption and chemical composition, which means they can be accurately determined.
Combustion occurs in equipment such as engines, turbines, furnaces and boilers and other small-scale consumption such as pilot gas and purge gas in flares.
• By combustion of gas sent to flare.
• Process: Emissions from the process itself, characteristics of the Refining business such as, for example, the production of hydrogen from natural gas.
These are reported by direct and analytical measurement of the gas or by process unit balance.
• Vents: Emissions inherent in the equipment's design or operating practices, such as pneumatic devices or purges.
They are mainly methane emissions and are quantified by direct measurement.
• Fugitive: Fugitive emissions come from involuntary leaks in equipment used in operations.
This includes those from flanges, valve packings, compressor elements, pressure relief devices that vent to the atmosphere, etc.
They are mainly methane emissions and are quantified by direct measurement through Leak Detection & Repair (LDAR) campaigns.
Indirect emissions (Scope 2) Emissions from energy exchanges with third parties to or from Repsol facilities.
Emissions due to the import/export of steam and electric power are considered.

As it is energy exchanged with a third party, it is quantified through invoices, from which the emission factors of the marketing companies are also extracted and used to calculate the associated emissions.
In a manner similar to energy consumption, the residual mix emission factor of the marketer or the country in the case of Direct Market Consumers is used for this calculation.

The (location-based) Scope 2 methodology quantifies GHG emissions based on average emission factors of energy generation for given locations, including local, subnational or national borders (Annex I of Directive 2013/34/EU).
45 The substitution method is used by the USA Department of Energy, Science Based Targets initiative Energy Institute and the Our World in Data initiative which includes the University of Oxford.

Generally speaking, Scope 1 and 2 GHG emissions are quantified following the guidelines of international standards widely recognized in the industry, such as IPCC, API, and Concawe.

Similarly, the breakdown at the emission source level and by business can only be accurately determined for emissions from operated assets as they are under the Company's control and the source and usage of each fuel is known.

In the specific case of methane (CH₄), the Company reports these emissions using advanced quantification methods, such as direct measurements or equivalent alternatives recognized by the OGMP 2.0 initiative.
For more information on the methodologies used, you can consult the publicly available technical guides on the OGMP website.

As for the global warming factors for methane and N₂O, as of 2024, the IPCC's Sixth Assessment Report (AR6) is used as a reference, which establishes a global warming factor (GWP) of 28.9 for methane and 273 for nitrous oxide (N₂O) over a 100- year time horizon, highlighting the greater climate impact of these gases compared to CO₂.
JOA’s non-operated Scope 1 and 2 emissions are estimated based on information provided by third parties in 2024 and updated using 2025 equity production.

Indirect emissions (Scope 3) The reported Scope 3 emissions are aligned with the principles established by the GHG Protocol Corporate Value Chain (Scope 3) Accounting and Reporting Standard and reflect the methodological approach followed to quantify the Company’s indirect value chain emissions.

After analyzing the entire emissions inventory for 2024, including the 15 Scope 3 categories, and considering that no significant changes have taken place that could have altered the materiality of these categories, the material categories and the corresponding calculation methods are detailed below.

A materiality threshold has been defined for those categories that account for more than 1.5% of total Scope 3 emissions, while ensuring that more than 95% of the emissions within this scope are always considered.
1: Purchased goods and services It includes purchases of crude oil, other main raw materials for Refining and Chemicals (hydrogen, ethylene, benzene, etc.
), purchases of biofuels and services contracted by the Company.

For the calculation of crude oil, biofuels and raw materials emissions, primary activity data from material balances are used together with emission factors from IOGP 2024 (International Association for Oil & Gas Producers) by regions and Ecoinvent 3.12.
In the case of hydrogen, an emission factor is used based on the European Union’s BREF Best Available Techniques (BAT) Reference Document for the Refining of Mineral Oil and Gas (JRC 2015).

The calculation of contracted services is based on the methodology spend-based using data from the Company’s economic purchasing activity and emission factors from the Carbon Footprint 2023 Evolution 2019-2023 document, May 2025.
Ministry for the Ecological Transition and the Demographic Challenge, in which the Ministry for the Environment calculates and publishes its own carbon footprint.

METHODOLOGY Category 11: Use of sold products This is calculated taking into account the sale of the Company’s petroleum products (Customer, Mobility Peru, Operators and Exports businesses) and net gas production by the Exploration and Production business.

To obtain category 11 emissions, the lower calorific value (GJ/t) and emission factor (tCO2/GJ) of each of the products is applied (2006 IPCC Guidelines for National Greenhouse Gas Inventories, Volume 2 – Energy; Table 1.2 and 2.2).
All products are taken into account with the exception of chemical products, lubricants, asphalts and specialties, as they do not generate emissions in their use.
Biogenic emissions These emissions are related to the value chain of biogenic matter, both to feed Repsol processes and to be sold as biofuel.
Biogenic emissions associated with Scope 1 and 2 are not included as they are not material.
In the case of Scope 3, we distinguish: • Biogenic CO₂ emissions associated with the use phase of biofuels, calculated based on biofuel sales data and the required bio content in the marketed fuels.

For the calculation of CO2 emissions associated with the use of these fuels, the lower calorific value (GJ/t) according to the RED II directive and the emission factor (tCO2/GJ) of each of the products is applied (2006 IPCC Guidelines for National Greenhouse Gas Inventories, Volume 2 – Energy; 2.2).
The result is 4.2 MtCO2 and it is not included in the results presented for Scope 3.

• Methane and N2O emissions associated with the biomass combustion use stage, calculated by applying the lower calorific value (GJ/t) according to the RED II directive and the corresponding emission factor for each GHG and product (2006 IPCC Guidelines for National Greenhouse Gas Inventories, Volume – Energy; 2.2), with a result of 15 ktCO2e.

• Life cycle emissions of biomass not from combustion or degradation, already included in the results presented for Scope 3 and corresponding to the purchase of bio feedstocks with associated emissions of 1.1 MtCO2e and calculated with DEFRA factors.
Other considerations The methodology for calculating Scope 3, Category 11 emissions is based on the Company’s product sales.

The percentage of Scope 3 emissions calculated using primary data obtained from suppliers or other partners in the value chain is 91.7%, including data provided by flight travel agencies for category 6, category 15 emissions as data provided directly by the joint ventures, and considering category 11 emissions based on internal fuel sales data.

Methane (CH4) and nitrous oxide (N2O) are operationally relevant greenhouse gases, however, the detailed breakdown of these gases loses relevance for the quantification of Scope 3 emissions due to their materiality.
Despite this, most of the emission factors used to calculate Scope 3 emissions include both CH4 and N2O (DEFRA, IOGP, Ecoinvent).

Similarly, the breakdown at the emission source level and by business can only be accurately determined for emissions from operated assets as they are under the Company’s control and the source and usage of each fuel is known.
Scope 3 emissions, on the other hand, are indirect and therefore the only way to estimate them is by means of global emission factors in CO2e, which is why this breakdown is not available.

In this analysis, environmental experts from each business area have participated and the Company’s sites and businesses that are operated have been examined, excluding the rest of its own operations from the scope.

The amount of pollutants emitted, their dispersion into the environment, the legal limits established for emissions, the existence of pollution receptors and their vulnerability in relation to these pollutants were taken into account.

The following aspects have been identified as the IRO materials with a negative environmental impact in terms of pollution: • Emissions of pollutants generated in its own operations into water (IRO-14).
• Air emissions from both its production processes (IRO-15) and those generated by third parties due to the use of the fuel products offered by the Company (IRO-16).
• Emissions to the ground due to accidental releases or deviations from established procedures (IRO-17).

In addition to the double materiality assessment, a methodology has been developed to determine where the emissions of the substances that the Company considers a priority occur, as well as the business areas that contribute significantly to each IRO analyzed: • For air emissions, based on the volume of emissions recorded in Repsol’s internal environmental management tool, it has been identified that the Industrial and E&P businesses contribute significantly to the material IROs identified.

• For water emissions, based on local water-related risks, assessed with the Repsol Water Tool, weighted with respect to the relative volume of discharge from each facility, it has been identified that the Industrial business contributes significantly to the material IROs identified.
For more information on the Repsol Water Tool, see Impact, risk and opportunity management.

• For emissions to the soil, indirectly, given the technical difficulty to measure them due to their accidental nature, and based on the estimated costs to manage contaminated soils, it has been identified that the Industrial business and the Customer business contribute significantly to the material IROs identified.
In relation to air pollution, sulfur oxides (SO2), nitrogen oxides (NOx), volatile organic compounds (VOCs) and particles are identified as priority substances.
In water pollution, hydrocarbons, suspended solids and substances that cause an increase in chemical oxygen demand (COD) are a priority.

In soil contamination, in the event of an accidental release, priority is given to prevention by controlling all substances that may cause damage to health and the environment in case there is evidence of their presence, including hydrocarbons.

The Company also monitors other substances emitted in compliance with the requirements of applicable legislation, such as the European Industrial Emissions Directive (Integrated Pollution Prevention and Control E-PRTR).1 These assessments have not required consultations with potentially affected communities.

Policies related to pollution (E2-1) Repsol has embraced numerous commitments in its Environmental Policy, which governs its actions in all its activities and locations where it operates, throughout the value chain, as well as in the products and services it offers.

Specifically, the Company undertakes to prevent and control the impacts associated to air, water and soil emissions of pollutants derived from our operations and to reduce the potential environmental impact arising from the use of the products we manufacture, avoiding incidents and emergency situations.
This commitment relates to the material IROs mentioned above.
The policy also promotes and requires environmental commitments in line with one’s own in the value chain, partners, suppliers, contractors, distributors and other business relationships.
For more information, see Repsol’s policies related to sustainability.

Repsol also carries out other actions for which no specific goals have been set, but which help to prevent polluting emissions: • • It develops and markets a range of products that add to its performance the minimization of polluting emissions in its life cycle.
For example, renewable fuels and electricity, Blue+ fuels, LPG, AutoGas, LNG and low-emission diesel for boilers (bioenergy e+10), among others.

It minimizes potential environmental impacts on populations and natural spaces through a process of selecting the location of its new facilities, regulated by its internal regulations on the assessment of environmental, social, and health impacts, and also subject to the applicable laws on such impacts.

• • It implements comprehensive management of discharges through advanced wastewater treatment systems, which include the segregation of effluents according to their level of contamination and the application of specific and effective treatments, with the aim of maximizing internal reuse and minimizing the pollutant load discharged.

It establishes internal safety standards for the design and operation of its activities, minimizing the risks of accidents that may lead to air, water or soil contamination due to the unintentional release of polluting substances.
None of the above actions require significant capital expenditures (capex) or operating expenses (opex), according to the financial materiality criteria adopted by the Company.
2.2.2 Metrics and targets Targets related to pollution (E2-3) Repsol is making progress in the prevention and control of polluting emissions through the requirements of the applicable regulations.
This involves the adoption of the best available technologies (BAT) referenced by the European Union’s sectoral BREF3 documents.

In addition, Repsol has voluntarily set two targets4 to mitigate its material IROs in line with the commitments set out in the Environmental Policy regarding the prevention and control of impacts associated with emissions to the air, water, and soil of polluting substances derived from its operations.

3 The Best Available Techniques Reference (BREF) documents are the reference documents published by the European Union within the framework of Directive (EU) 2024/1785 on Industrial Emissions (IED), which include the best available technologies from the different industrial sectors and apply across Europe.

4 These targets are not based on conclusive scientific evidence and interest groups have not been involved in their development; there have been no changes in the target or metrics from the initial definitions.
As further information to the targets described: 1.– Define action plans for the management and remediation of soil or groundwater in the event of contamination within less than 12 months.
In 2024, Repsol established this goal to prevent and control contamination of soil or groundwater, aligned with IROs 14 and 17 (as described in Materiality assessment).
This objective is applicable to the Company’s own operations that contribute significantly to the identified material impacts.
Its definition is not based on the emission of specific pollutant loads.

By means of this target, the Company considers that the extent of contaminants present in the soil and/or groundwater is minimized, as is the potential impact that contaminants may have on existing receptors.
This target is monitored through the Company’s environmental situation reporting tool.
To assess compliance, the percentage (%) of action plans that have been defined within a period of less than 12 months from the confirmation of the contamination shall be determined annually.
The aim is to achieve a value of 100% in this indicator, starting from a reference value of 0%, which is established annually.
Progress toward achieving the 2025 target remains on track, according to the initial planning, and no significant deviations have been identified from the initially planned performance.
2.– Reduce emissions of nitrogen oxides (NOx) by 30% by 2035 compared with 2018 at the Refining and Chemical industrial complexes in the Iberian Peninsula.
In 2025, Repsol set this target for air pollution, in line with the IRO-15 (as described in Materiality assessment), which will first apply in 2026.
This target is not based on the emission of specific pollutant loads.

It is an absolute target linked to the decarbonization plan, the GHG emission reduction levers and the development of new production processes related to the use of renewable energy and renewable raw materials of the Industrial business.
In its definition, the projected reduction of NOx emissions achievable through the aforementioned levers has been estimated.
To this end, a correlation between CO2 emissions and NOx was used, based on historical emissions for both gases in the different facilities included within the scope of the target.

The target will be reviewed if there are changes in current regulations, technological advances and market trends, modifications in Repsol’s Strategic Plan, or changes in the facilities operated in the Industrial business that have a significant impact.
To monitor this target, the internal environmental parameter reporting tool will be used, from which NOx emissions data will be extracted annually.
In the case of water pollution, Repsol has not adopted specific targets beyond those established for water management, as defined in Water and marine resources.
Businesses that are material to these issues operate in highly regulated environments, where the applicable regulations define both the limits to be reached and the technologies to be implemented.

100% of emissions is accounted for, regardless of the percentage of shareholding.

Repsol Water Tool In addition to the double materiality assessment, an in- house tool called the Repsol Water Tool (RWT) has been used to identify and evaluate current and potential impacts, as well as material water-related risks and opportunities in the Company’s own operations.

The analysis conducted identified the businesses for which water is a material issue, namely the Refining and Chemicals business in the Iberian Peninsula and the Marcellus asset of the Upstream business in the USA.
Particular attention has been given to the amount of freshwater withdrawn from the environment, as this is considered to be the Company’s most significant impact in this topic.
For more details, see Methodology for water and marine resources reporting and metrics.
The results of the IRO assessment are taken into account when setting water-related policies and targets.
These, in turn, are aligned with the Company’s Strategic Plan for its transformation and an energy transition compatible with the global ambition to achieve net zero emissions.

Policies related to water management (E3-1) Repsol has established commitments in its Environmental Policy that govern its actions in all its activities and places where it operates, throughout the value chain, as well as in the products and services it offers.
These commitments give rise to the actions necessary to manage water-related environmental impacts.

The Company is focused on water management that includes the use and sourcing of water throughout the life cycle of its operations, products and services, and the long-term protection of available water resources, both terrestrial and marine.

Repsol will fulfill this commitment by increasing the use of sustainable supply from alternative water sources, including both internal and external reuse of water, thereby supporting the prevention and reduction of water pollution resulting from our activities through the treatment of effluents.
The Company has also set the ambition to reduce freshwater withdrawal from the environment, especially in areas at water risk.
These commitments are related to the material IROs associated with the reduction in the volume of available water resources, which is reported in this section.
The Environmental Policy includes in its scope all the Company’s facilities located in areas of high water stress and envisages practices related to the sustainability of the oceans and the seas.

The policy also pursues and requires environmental practices in the value chain that are in line with the Company’s own, including partners, suppliers, contractors, distributors and other business relationships.
For more information, see Repsol’s policies related to sustainability.
Actions and resources related to water management (E3-2) The following tables provide information on the actions taken and planned by Repsol related to water management.

Its implementation contributes to meeting the commitments established in the Environmental Policy, specifically to reducing freshwater withdrawal from the environment, particularly in water-stressed areas, in the vicinity of the Company’s operations.

In particular, actions 1.1, 1.2, 1.3, 2.1, 2.2, 2.3, 2.4 and 3.1-3.6 are aimed at fulfilling the ambition of zero net freshwater withdrawal from the environment for the industrial complexes of the Refining and Chemicals business located in the Iberian Peninsula.
Actions 2.5, 2.6 and 4.1 have been established in order to achieve the goal of zero net freshwater withdrawal from the environment for the Marcellus E&P asset.
