A I II Table of conversions and abbreviations Alternative performance measures III Risks IV V VI ≡ Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information Annual Corporate Governance Report Annual Report on Director Remuneration M Methodology for water and marine resources reporting and metrics Repsol Water Tool The analysis methodology of the Repsol Water Tool (RWT) includes the following features: • The degree of measurement of the volume and quality of water flows in the facilities.
• The type of water (fresh and non-freshwater) and the volumes associated with each indicator (withdrawal, discharge, reuse).

• The quality of the water withdrawn, the existence of alternative sources of water supply and water treatment technologies (withdrawn, discharged and reused), the potential alteration of water quality (physical, chemical and biological parameters) downstream of the facility.

• The characterization of the withdrawal sources: type of source, type of water, location, impact of the withdrawal in relation to the average volume of the water body, degree of dependence on that source of supply, location of the source with respect to protected areas and key areas for biodiversity, presence of threatened species, importance of the water source for local communities and current and future level of water stress.
• The characterization of the discharge: direct/indirect return to the original source, the reuse of the discharge by a third party.
• The degree of competition for the resource and the future availability of water (guarantee of supply in the coming years, projections of future water demand).
• Regulatory and legal changes, sanctioning and reputational aspects.
For this assessment, no consultations were necessary with the potentially affected communities.

For the identification of physical risks, the values of water stress (current and future) are taken into account both for the water withdrawal sources and for the points of discharge, as obtained from the Aqueduct Water Risk Atlas tool of the World Resource Institute (WRI).
To define the state of water bodies, the criteria established in the annexes of the European Directive 2000/60/EC (Water Framework Directive) have been used.
The input data entered into RWT is based on direct measurements and assessments made by those responsible for water management at each facility.
Water metrics The reporting criteria for water metrics are set out in a specific internal guide for the water vector.

It defines metrics and methodologies so that the reporting of these metrics complies with the legal requirements for sustainability reporting, supporting the homogeneity and traceability of the information throughout the Company.

Some analytical techniques reviewed are Standard Methods (SM) of the Examination of Water and Wastewater (from the APHA, AWWA, WPCF associations), series 2000 to 9000, Water Testing Standards (ASTM) and European Standards (EN) and standards published by the International Organization for Standardization (ISO).

These criteria are determined for the internal report and will not prevail over the criteria established by the competent authorities for the reports that must be issued by the operational sites in compliance with the applicable legislation.

METHODOLOGY For the calculation of the metrics, the information is reported by category, according to the type of water (fresh or non-fresh), type of withdrawal source and type of effluent receiver, additionally indicating the use of water for the particular cases in which it is reused or recycled or, for example, if it is used for cooling processes in specific circuits.
This segregation, based on the Global Reporting Initiative (GRI) water standard, allows the requested metrics to be calculated from the granularity of the Company’s water management.

The methodology used to calculate the metrics in Repsol’s operations under operational control establishes that 100% of the corresponding contribution is accounted for, regardless of the percentage of shareholding held by the Company, and is applied in accordance with the following criteria: • Water consumption: it is calculated as the difference between withdrawals and discharges, which includes both fresh and non-freshwater.

• Water consumption in areas with water risk: it is calculated by evaluating the level of water stress in the areas where both the withdrawal sources and the discharge points are located, using the Aqueduct Water Risk Atlas tool by World Resource Institute’s (WRI).
Regions with a high (40-80%) or very high (greater than 80%) percentage of water withdrawals are deemed to be areas at water risk.
This information relates to to areas where water has been identified as a matter of material importance.
• Recycled water: treated or untreated water that has been used more than once in the same process, before being discharged outside the Company’s boundaries.
• Reused water: treated or untreated water that has been used more than once in different processes of the Company or by a third party, before being discharged outside its limits.

• Stored water: water withdrawn from the environment that, without having been included in the report of the water consumption metric, is temporarily accumulated to enable water availability in operations and to be used when necessary for a certain operational purpose, whether present or future.
• Changes in water storage: the difference between the volume stored at the beginning and at the end of the reporting period.
• Water intensity: the ratio of total water consumption (m3) to net income.

In assets managed through Joint Operating Agreements (JOAs), where Repsol does not exercise operational control, direct information on the metrics is not available through the internal reporting process.
To obtain this data, information has been requested from the pertinent operators; however, the information received is incomplete and unverified.
To meet the reporting requirements, estimates of the relevant metrics have been made.

In the specific case of water metrics, it is only possible to estimate consumption, since other indicators, such as the volume of water recycled, reused or stored, depend on the particular way that each asset is managed, which is conditioned by operational factors such as location, type of operation (onshore/offshore) and the nature of the asset (conventional/unconventional).

Water consumption was estimated based on production, applying ratios from assets operated under comparable conditions and, where no equivalent information was available, using average ratios from assets with similar characteristics.

Given that the estimate of water consumption in these assets represents less than 5% of the Company’s total, it has been deemed non-material and therefore excluded from the Company’s consolidated reporting.
Stakeholders were not involved in the elaboration of this study.
The scope of the analysis includes the Company’s operated sites and businesses, leaving out of scope the rest of its own operations.
The time horizon used is five years.

The risks considered are those assessed in the double materiality assessment, where the annual economic impact is evaluated, generally based on the operating result before tax, and the probability of occurrence of the event, based on statistics of historical events or, failing that, on expert criteria.
The risks assessed are listed below.

None of them were deemed material in the double materiality assessment: • Potential risk of non-compliance with regulations related to biodiversity and the ecosystem protection in owned onshore operations or those located in sensitive marine environments, which may result in economic sanctions, suspension of activities, loss of licenses, reputational damage, and increased operating costs.

• Potential risk that onshore and offshore operations will be restricted, disrupted, or impeded in areas classified as sensitive or prioritized for biodiversity conservation, which could lead to delays in obtaining permits, project redesigns, or even to cancellation.
• The deterioration of coastal or river protective ecosystems may become an additional risk factor that could worsen potential damage to infrastructure in the event of heavy rainfall or waves.
• Loss of vegetation as a result of deforestation, construction activities and natural erosion may lead to a decrease in soil stability.
This can be an additional risk factor in exposure to landslides, which could lead to damage to infrastructure, increase operational downtime and raise repair costs.
Therefore, this assessment has identified transition and physical risks, and it has been determined that there are no systemic risks related to biodiversity and ecosystems that affect the Company.
Repsol’s strategy and business model could be affected by the following: • Greater complexity when selecting geographical locations to undertake new projects.
• • • • Increased regulatory requirements that may result in greater difficulty in obtaining environmental authorizations for new projects or for renewing/ maintaining existing ones.
Increased cost of capital for investments (capex).
Increase in management costs (capex and opex) of impacts related to biodiversity and ecosystems.
Impact on the Company’s reputation associated with lower investor attraction.
Repsol has considered several scenarios to assess the resilience of its strategy and business model, although it does not intend to establish which will be the most likely.
The Company has adopted the scenarios set out in the Taskforce on Nature-related Financial Disclosures (TNFD)1 reporting framework as a reference for developing its long-term forecasts.

TNFD’s approach focuses on two critical uncertainties: 1) the deterioration of ecosystem services, which is correlated with physical risks, and 2) the alignment of market and non-market forces, which is associated with transition risks.
Four different scenarios are thus envisaged: • Ahead of the game: moderate deterioration of ecosystem services and high alignment of market and non-market forces.
• Go fast or go home: serious deterioration of ecosystem services and high alignment of market and non-market forces.
• Sand in the gears: serious deterioration of ecosystem services and low alignment of market and non-market forces.
• Back of the list: moderate deterioration of ecosystem services and low alignment of market and non-market forces.

In the assessment of the potential level of risk, a scale has been considered ranging from negligible, when it does not affect the normal functioning of the business, to extreme, which would involve a total or substantial loss of the operation.

In no case does the potential level of risk obtained over five years exceed the materiality threshold, in other words., there are no situations that could affect the normal operation of the business, that would imply an impact or operational change, or a partial loss of the operation or prolonged closure.
For impact, risk and opportunities management, the Company undertakes commitments in its Environmental Policy and takes actions.

The Company is prepared for the most adverse scenarios thanks to its Strategic Plan, the decarbonization roadmap and the actions it carries out to manage its biodiversity- and ecosystem-related dependencies, impacts, risks, and opportunities.
Environmental experts from each business area have participated in this assessment and the Company’s operated sites and businesses have been examined, with other own operations left out of scope.
IRO-related metrics have been taken into account based on information from the sites and businesses.

The following material negative impacts have been identified in relation to biodiversity and ecosystems, which are associated with the operations of the Industrial and LCG businesses: • Temporary loss of ecosystems and the services they provide as a result of the modification of the terrain and/or waterbeds, and the physical presence resulting from Repsol’s activities that affect a sensitive area, which may alter local flora and fauna (IRO-19).
• Alteration of the presence and population size of species due to the Company’s activities (IRO-21).

The material positive impact identified is as follows: • Improvement of the conservation status of natural habitats and species populations, including those threatened, through the implementation of specific projects and nature-based solutions (NBSs) in the areas where the Company operates (IRO-20).

In addition to these material IROs, others have been identified that are related to other drivers of biodiversity loss (direct exploitation or climate change, for example) that can be consulted in climate change impact, risk and opportunity management and in water and marine resources impact, risk and opportunities management.
The double materiality assessment does not identify material negative impacts related to land deterioration, desertification or soil sealing.

LEAP Analysis Complementary to the double materiality assessment, a deeper understanding and identification has been achieved of the impacts and dependencies that result in different types of risks and opportunities for the Company with respect to nature, following TNFD’s LEAP approach.
For more details, see Methodology for biodiversity and ecosystems reporting and metrics.

A I II Table of conversions and abbreviations Alternative performance measures III Risks IV V VI ≡ Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information Annual Corporate Governance Report Annual Report on Director Remuneration The table above shows the main habitats2 (according to the IUCN3 classification scheme) where the Company’s sites with material impacts on biodiversity are located and an assessment of their ecological status.
To determine the latter, the Mean Species Abundance (MSA) has been used as an indicator, as modelled by GLOBIO4 and available on its website.

Its maximum value is calculated in the environment of each operation to which a semi-quantitative level has been attributed (Very low: <0,1; Low: 0.1-0.2; Moderate: 0.2-0.5; High: 0.5-0.7; Very high: 0,7-1).
The MSA is an indicator of the integrity of local biodiversity and represents the degree of alteration in the composition of species.

Repsol also accounts for the conservation and protection of biodiversity in the management of its own projects and operations by applying the mitigation hierarchy (avoid, minimize, restore, and compensate) during projects’ life cycle, especially in those located in protected areas.
Examples of these measures are reported in Actions and resources relating to biodiversity and ecosystems.

Repsol analyzes the potential presence of threatened species included in the Red List of the International Union for Conservation of Nature in the area of influence of its facilities using the Integrated Biodiversity Assessment Tool (IBAT) and has concluded that its operations may affect them.
The results of this analysis are made public on the corporate website.
To minimize and compensate for this impact, the Company implements different management measures which are also available in the actions and resources section.

Policies related to biodiversity and ecosystems (E4-2) Repsol has established commitments in its Environmental Policy that govern its actions in all the places where it operates, throughout the value chain, as well as in the products and services it offers.
These commitments give rise to the necessary actions to manage the impacts, dependencies, risks and material environmental opportunities related to biodiversity and ecosystems.

The Policy includes the following commitments that are related to material IROs of alteration of the presence and population size of species and temporary loss of terrestrial ecosystems and the services they provide as a result of the modification of terrain and/or waterbeds: • The sustainable use of resources.
• The protection and conservation of biodiversity, ecosystems and associated ecosystem services.
• The management of the impacts and dependencies on the above and the communities and stakeholders that may be affected.
• Special attention to impacts that take place within or near protected areas.
• Consideration of the different drivers of biodiversity loss, such as climate change, land/freshwater/sea use change, direct exploitation, the introduction of invasive species and pollution.

Therefore, the contribution to the direct drivers of biodiversity loss, impacts on the status of species, impacts on the extent and state of ecosystems, and impacts and dependencies on ecosystem services have been considered when establishing the commitments of the Policy and the Company’s management must take them into account when they are material.
In this way, Repsol seeks to contribute to goals 1, 4, 6, 7, 8 and 15 of the Global Biodiversity Framework (GBF), given the importance that Repsol attributes to this framework.
The Policy prioritizes the application of the mitigation hierarchy, which entails the use of ecosystem restoration as a practice to manage deforestation.
Examples can be found in Actions and resources relating to biodiversity and ecosystems.

The same policy has also made commitments in relation to the sustainability of the oceans and seas and promotes the sustainable sourcing and use of resources, although it does not explicitly address production, procurement or consumption from ecosystems managed to maintain or improve conditions for biodiversity, since most of the input resources to the Company are technical materials.
For more details, see Resource use and circular economy.

The policy also promotes and requires environmental commitments in the value chain that are in line with Repsol’s own commitments, including partners, suppliers, contractors, distributors and other business relationships.
The consequences of impacts related to biodiversity and ecosystems on affected communities are addressed following the guidelines established in the Human Rights and Community Relations Policy.
Actions and resources related to biodiversity and ecosystems (E4-3) The following tables include the actions undertaken and planned by Repsol related to biodiversity and ecosystems.
Specifically, those associated with the biodiversity loss due to land, sea, or freshwater use change, or the alteration of the presence and population size of species are included.
Actions related to activities whose impact on biodiversity is due to other drivers are reported in Climate change and Water and marine resources.

The implementation of these actions contributes to the achievement of the commitments undertaken in the Environmental Policy, specifically to the protection and conservation of biodiversity, ecosystems and associated ecosystem services and the management of impacts and dependencies on them.
Specifically, actions 2.1 to 2.8 contribute to the objective of improving the conservation status of endangered species through the financing, implementation and monitoring of specific projects.

These objectives are aligned with the commitments established in the Environmental Policy to protect and conserve biodiversity, ecosystems and Biodiversity and ecosystem-related objectives the associated ecosystem services, managing the impacts and dependencies on them and the communities and stakeholders that may be affected throughout the life cycle of operations, products and services, with special attention focused on impacts that take place within or near protected areas.
(2) The base value will be reassessed annually and will change depending on the evolution of the Company’s scope and the action plans already developed previously.
Further details on the targets described: 1.– Improve the conservation status of endangered species.
The Company undertakes conservation projects to minimize, restore and compensate for the magnitude of possible direct impacts on threatened species, improving their populations and habitats.

This objective, as part of the Company’s voluntary goals, supports the Global Biodiversity Framework (objective 4), which seeks to reduce the risk of extinction of threatened species through their recovery and conservation, although it does not entail external validation.
No ecological thresholds have been applied when setting the target, the first year of which will be 2026.
This objective applies to its own operations, with priority given to those that contribute significantly to the material impacts identified in Impact, risk and opportunity management.
Its compliance will be measured with an absolute value of the number of threatened species with documented improvement in their conservation status.

The improvement in conservation will be linked to parameters related to the increase in the local or regional population of a species, the improvement of the quality or surface area of the habitat available for it, etc.
To determine whether a species is threatened or not, the categorization made by IUCN in its Red List, as well as regional or national threat criteria, will be followed.
No intermediate milestones have been established for this objective, and the year 2020 has been set as baseline.
2.– Adopt action plans for biodiversity in assets located in sensitive areas for biodiversity.

Beyond the commitment defined by the Company years ago to observe voluntary exclusion areas9 , Repsol has established a relative (%) and continuous target in 2025 related to the management of impacts on biodiversity and ecosystems in sensitive areas.
The Company believes that it avoids, minimizes, restores and compensates for the magnitude of the possible impacts on biodiversity and ecosystems through the actions associated with this target.
Repsol will consider as equivalent plans different biodiversity impact management measures such as monitoring, surveillance plans, the implementation of complementary impact measures, etc.

This information is obtained and analyzed with the Integrated Biodiversity Assessment Tool (IBAT), which is recognized as a source of quality and reliable data, and is used by Repsol for decision making.

To remedy this deficiency, the 2025 report aims to break down the information on surface areas of each of the individual concession blocks making up to total in each county.
In this LEAP analysis, it has not been necessary to carry out consultations with potentially affected communities.
The first component of the LEAP assessment (L for Locate ) involves creating a list of assets located in sensitive areas or with potentially material impacts or dependencies.

Each of the activities has been evaluated using tools such as ENCORE13, and guides such as those published by the World Business Council for Sustainable Development14, which collect general information by type of activity.

Likewise, the activities have been analyzed according to their location, based on geo-referenced information, identifying biomes of importance for biodiversity and interaction with sensitive areas for nature.
According to TNFD, a site is sensitive to nature if it impacts on: • Areas of importance for biodiversity.
• Areas of high ecosystem integrity.
• Areas with a rapid decline in this integrity.
• Areas with a high water risk.
• Areas of importance for the provision of ecosystem services, including benefits to local and indigenous communities and other stakeholders.
A conservative approach has been followed by taking into account areas of influence recommended by a third party in the determination of sensitive areas.
The second evaluation component of the LEAP approach (E for Evaluate) allows for the measurement of dependencies and impacts on biodiversity and ecosystems.
The ENCORE tool has been used for the dependencies, attributing a semi-quantitative level to them for each ecosystem service.

For impacts, the Company has developed the Reads15 tool, which can be used to assess the environmental impacts of projects and activities during the construction, operation and decommissioning stages.
Reads enables users to understand the effects of a project or activity on climate, water resources, ecosystem services and social well-being.

Based on these assessments, Reads calculates an indicator of impact on biodiversity taking into account the different drivers of biodiversity loss: land use change, greenhouse gas emissions, direct exploitation of natural resources, and pollution.
In addition, it can go beyond sectoral averages by incorporating site-level data to assess environmental impacts.
In recognition of the importance of the Global Biodiversity Framework (GBF), Repsol seeks to contribute to the GBF’s 2030 and 2050 goals through its efforts.

Thus, for example, the Company includes in this report (see table of Sites with a material impact on biodiversity and ecosystems) the impacts and dependencies on biodiversity and ecosystems, in line with Target 15 of the Global Biodiversity Framework.
Finally, the third evaluation component of the LEAP approach (A stands for Assess) aims to identify and assess nature-related risks and opportunities.
An identification of the typology of risks collected by TNFD has been carried out, which includes physical risks, related to dependencies, and transition risks, associated with the impacts.
It has been determined that there are no systemic nature-related risks affecting the Company.
The Company has been making progress over the past two years in the application of LEAP analysis, and intends to continue advancing and make it more robust year by year.

To this end, throughout this year, progress has been made in the number and types of assets modelled in Reads, as well as in the frequency of updating the data to have the most up-to-date quantification of impacts possible; the dependency analysis has been updated with the new version of ENCORE; the measurement of the main habitats where the Company's sites are located following the IUCN classification scheme has been incorporated; etc.
Despite the continuous effort to improve this approach, the analysis does have limitations, or challenges.
For example, ecosystem condition data are not available for offshore sites.
Although progress has been made in the last year, polygonal geographic information is not available for all sites, but only point information.
So manual and visual data curation is required.

For calculation of the total altered area (or disturbed area according to TNFD nomenclature) of the Company’s sites and businesses, account has been taken of the area occupied by the assets where there is a limitation on carrying out other types of activities, as well as possible exclusion zones for other activities in the vicinity of the facilities such as, for example, fishing exclusion zones around offshore platforms.

The metric is applied to sites belonging to businesses whose main driver of impact on biodiversity is land use change, change in freshwater use, or change in sea use, and facilities with a different driver of impact on biodiversity from the aforementioned, but that occupy a significant area.

Estimates of E&P’s non-operated Joint Operating Agreement spatial footprint The spatial footprint of non-operated Joint Operating Agreements of E&P has been estimated based on the Hectares/ Production ratio calculated for the operated assets.

The most appropriate value of the ratio has been used for the calculation according to the geographical location, the facility type (onshore/offshore and conventional/unconventional), and the production stage of the non-operated Joint Operating Agreements.
For smaller operations located in Spain, actual data from the cadastral registry were used.

The estimated spatial footprint for non-operated Joint Operating Agreements would account for 4.15% of the total owned operations, so they have been left out of scope due to their own weight in the total value of the metric, and uncertainty as to the estimation.
In carrying out this analysis, the Company’s sites and businesses that are operated have been examined, while other own operations are out of the scope.

This information makes it possible to determine that the contribution to the material IROs occurs in the Upstream and Industrial businesses, in which: • There is an influx of raw materials in the form of mineral hydrocarbons.
• The mineral hydrocarbons used can be replaced by other raw materials of renewable origin or derived from waste.

Likewise, the categories of products manufactured by Repsol have been reviewed, with the finding that some of those produced by the Chemicals and Customer businesses are related to the material IROs, as detailed in Product and material outflows (E5-5).
In this analysis, it has not been necessary to carry out consultations with potentially affected communities.

The following material IROs have been identified in the field of resource use and circular economy: • As a positive impact, the development of circular or bio-based products and renewable fuels from renewable and recycled secondary raw materials, as it reduces the adverse effects associated with the disposal of waste generated by society (IRO-22).
• As a negative impact, the extraction of non-renewable resources from nature, which contributes to the reduction of the world’s reserves of them (IRO-23).
• As a negative impact, the offering of products made with non-renewable nor recycled materials (IRO-24).

In addition to the double materiality assessment, own operations have been identified in which non-renewable resources are used to a greater extent and in which there are products and services for which circular economy criteria can be applied.
To this end, the quantities and types of resources that enter the activities and the circularity characteristics of the different families of manufactured products have been analyzed.

Policies related to resource use and the circular economy (E5-1) Repsol has established commitments in its Environmental Policy that govern its actions in all its activities and places where it operates, throughout the value chain, as well as in the products and services it offers.
The necessary actions to manage the material environmental impacts related to the use of resources and the circular economy arise from these goals.

In particular, Repsol is focused on integrating the principles of the circular economy into its processes, products, and services, optimizing the use of resources and raw materials, including energy and water; fostering the use of alternative and renewable resources obtained in a circular manner; and promoting the development of new products with less environmental impact.
This goals relates to the material IROs mentioned above.

In addition, it is aligned with the industrial transformation that Repsol is carrying out and could imply a relative reduction in the use of virgin and non-renewable resources, thanks to the fact that a portion1 of the demand for Repsol’s products will be met with products manufactured from renewable and/or non-virgin raw materials.

The policy also promotes and requires environmental commitments in the value chain in line with the Company’s own, including partners, suppliers, contractors, distributors and other business relationships.

Its implementation contributes to the achievement of the commitments established in the Environmental Policy, specifically to the integration of the principles of the circular economy in its processes, products and services, the optimization of the use of resources and raw materials, the use of alternative and renewable resources obtained in a sustainable way; and the development of new products with less impact on the environment.

Specifically, actions 1.1 and 1.2 contribute to the target of reaching a production volume of between 125,000 and 146,000 tons by 2030 of circular or bio-based products that incorporate alternative raw materials such as waste plastic or other renewable materials.

Lastly, action 3.1 supports the target of reaching a biomethane production capacity by 2030, from organic waste, of between 0.7 and 0.8 TWh.

2.5.2 Metrics and targets Targets related to resource use and the circular economy (E5-3) In 2024, Repsol updated its Strategic Plan for 2024-2027, defining several voluntary objectives2 to manage its impacts, risks, and material opportunities in line with the commitments set out in the Environmental Policy, namely: to integrate the principles of the circular economy into processes, products, and services; optimize the use of resources and raw materials; optimize the use of alternative and renewable resources obtained circularly; and develop new products with less impact on the environment.

These targets, which are of an absolute nature, are aligned with IRO-22, as described in Materiality assessment, and relate to both the inflow and outflow of resources, encouraging the use of circular materials, the minimization of primary raw materials, and the circular sourcing and use of renewable resources.
The calculation and tracking of these indicators is monitored through the project management tool of the Industrial business.
Its achievement serves as a lever for the reduction of the Company’s CO2 emissions in all its scopes.

Repsol has reviewed the objectives and eliminated intermediate milestones to adapt them to the global energy context, changes in regulation and demand, and the pace of the energy transition, without ceasing to make progress in achieving the commitments in the Environmental Policy, while making investments that are profitable in the new environment.
2 The defined targets are not based on conclusive scientific evidence and stakeholders have not been involved in their development.
(2) Own operations industrial business in which circular or bio-based products are produced.
(3) Own operations to the Industrial business in which biomethane is produced.
Further details on the targets described: 1.– Reach a renewable fuel production capacity of between 1.6 and 1.8 million tons in the Iberian Peninsula by 2030.
Renewable fuels are produced from sustainable sourced waste raw materials or from the capture of CO2 emissions.
This target has been set with reference to a renewable fuel production capacity value of 1 million tons in 2023.
It includes co-processing, ETBE, and renewable H2 as intermediates.

The objective originally defined in the 2024-2027 Strategic Plan set the objective of achieving a production capacity of renewable fuels in the Iberian Peninsula of more than 2.2 million tons by 2030.
Progress toward this goal in 2025 is in line with initial planning and no significant changes in performance have been identified.
The production capacity of renewable fuels stood at 1.25 million tons at the end of 2025 (1.2 million tons in 2024).

2.- Reach a production volume of between 125,000 and 146,000 tons by 2030 of circular or biological origin products incorporating alternative raw materials such as waste plastic or other renewable materials.

Repsol’s circular or biological origin products include alternative raw materials, such as plastic waste or renewable raw materials, where renewable fuels and biomethane do not count toward this objective.

This objective has been defined with respect to a reference value for the production of circular or biological origin products of 9,372 tons in 2023 and account is taken of changes in regulations, technology and markets associated with circular or biological origin products in the definition.

The objective originally defined in the 2024-2027 Strategic Plan set the objective of achieving a production capacity of circular or biological origin products incorporating alternative raw materials such as waste plastic or other renewable materials of more than 150,000 tons by 2030.
The target, which was initially communicated as circular or bio-based products, is adapted to the terminology in line with the current state of reference standards.

These products are obtained through mechanical recycling, which includes a percentage of recycled material content certified by RecyClass; chemical recycling, which includes a percentage of recycled material content, allocated by mass balance and certified by ISCC Plus; or recycling of bio-based materials, which includes a percentage of renewable or bio material content, assigned by mass balance and certified by ISCC EU (Renewable Energy Directive 2018/2001).

The strategy of the Company remains focused on growing in these markets, and regulatory support continues to be essential in order to develop them structurally and avoid exposure to decisions on voluntary goals in the end market.
The production of circular and biological-based products came to 25,137 tons at the end of 2025 (12,227 tons in 2024).
3.- Reach a biomethane production capacity in 2030, from organic waste, of between 0.7 and 0.8 TWh.
Biomethane is a renewable alternative to natural gas that originates from organic matter, such as agricultural and livestock waste.

In addition to its use as a substitute for conventional gas, it has other industrial applications, such as the production of renewable fuels and renewable hydrogen or the manufacture of chemical products, with an associated reduction in greenhouse gases.
This target has been set against a biomethane production reference value of 0 TWh in 2023.
The target originally defined in the 2024-2027 Strategic Plan set the objective of achieving a production capacity of biomethane from organic waste of more than 2.1 TWh by 2030.

2.6 Taxonomy Regulation As explained in Climate change, Repsol’s investments that directly contribute to meeting its decarbonization goals are called low-carbon investments and include energy efficiency, generation and commercialization of renewable electricity, production and commercialization of renewable fuels, renewable hydrogen, synthetic fuels, chemicals (long-life polymers), other circular economy projects, carbon capture and storage (CCS), distributed generation, renewable electric mobility, and investments in R&D and corporate venturing in low- carbon technologies.

This vision of the decarbonization of our activity is less restrictive1 than that laid down by the European Union in its framework for facilitating sustainable investments, called the Sustainable Finance Taxonomy.

The taxonomy defines a series of "eligible" economic activities, which are encompassed in 16 macro-sectors, and establishes the technical selection criteria to determine whether, if eligible, they are "aligned" activities: that is, those that contribute substantially to at least one of the six environmental objectives defined by the EU, do not pose significant damage to the other objectives and also comply with a minimum of social safeguards.
The percentage of these activities is quantified by three economic indicators: revenues, investment (capex) and operating costs (opex).

In 2025, the percentage of activities aligned according to the Sustainable Finance Taxonomy out of the Group’s total has risen to a percentage of 28% capex (40% in 2024), 0.8% of revenues (0.8% in 2024) and of 5% opex (4% in 2024).

In 2025, the greatest weight was held by investments in wind and solar photovoltaic generation, followed by investments in the production of biofuels for transport and biogas.

Capex aligned with the taxonomy decreased by twelve percentage points compared to 2024 as a result of the more intensive organic development of Repsol’s project portfolio in Spain and the USA and the organic acquisition of Connect Gen in 2024.

Social information 3.1 Own workforce 3.1.1 Strategy Interests and views of stakeholders (ESRS 2 SBM-2) Repsol has 24,679 employees, and the necessary capabilities to tackle the processes of energy transition and transformation of its business models.

Repsol considers its own workforce to be a key stakeholder, and it therefore systematically integrates their interests, views and rights, including their human rights, in the definition of the Company’s strategy and business model.

The Company has permanent channels of dialogue and listening, such as the People and Organization teams, the Employee Service, the Employee Voice, committees, task forces and digital surveys that help in identifying their expectations and needs.
See Interests and views of stakeholders (SBM-2).

The vast majority of Repsol employees are salaried (99.5%), that is, they maintain an employment relationship with Repsol under a permanent (90%) or temporary (10%) contract, the latter through the contracting modalities legally established in each country.

This distribution shows Repsol’s preference for the direct hiring of its own workforce, as opposed to external forms of labor through third parties or the hiring of self-employed workers, who are residual and not material1.

People management at Repsol is a pillar that contributes to the transformation of the Company’s business model and the achievement of its objectives, acting as a lever for the energy transition, innovation and competitiveness.

Accordingly, the Organization is focused on the development and well-being of its employees, aligning people management policies with corporate strategy and promoting a positive, safe and inclusive work environment, based on respect for human and labor rights and social dialogue, and cooperation with workers’ representatives.
All the information presented below relates solely to the Company’s salaried employees.

Material impacts, risks, and opportunities and their interaction with the strategy and business model (ESRS 2 SBM-3) Repsol has carried out a double materiality assessment in which the following impacts related to its own workforce have been identified: • Creation of jobs that cover various areas and levels of specialization, providing direct and indirect employment throughout the supply chain and associated services, improving the quality of life of individuals and their families and strengthening the economy by increasing purchasing power and stimulating consumption (IRO-29).

• Strong and continuous social dialogue with workers' representatives that fosters a positive work environment, facilitates proactive problem solving, the implementation of improvements in working conditions and allows decisions aligned with employee needs (IRO-30).

1 Less than 1.5% • • Improvement of the physical and mental health of employees, and increase of their motivation, engagement and productivity through initiatives that promote healthy habits and comprehensive well- being (IRO 31).

Improving the living conditions of Repsol employees by offering a comprehensive value proposition that includes competitive economic conditions, performance-based bonuses, health and well-being programs, and initiatives that facilitate work-life balance (IRO-32).

• Promotion of equality, diversity and inclusion beyond what is established by local legislation and market demands, improvement of working conditions and opportunities for professional growth, in a way that strengthens motivation and job satisfaction (IRO-33).
• Training aligned with market demands, which strengthens human capital and facilitates that employees have the necessary skills.
This enhances performance and adaptability, improves job satisfaction and enables meeting challenges effectively, contributing to innovation, growth, productivity and operational efficiency (IRO-34).

• • Improving the employability and inclusion of young people through Repsol’s young talent program, which fosters an inclusive workforce and promotes generational renewal in the future through the creation of a talent pool (IRO-35).
Improvement of working conditions, creating a fairer, safer and more equitable environment for all employees through the promotion of an environment of respect for human rights (IRO-36).
The positive impacts, as described in Adoption of measures related to material impacts, encompass the entire workforce.
No negative impacts, risks or opportunities of significant importance to the Company have been identified arising from the impacts or dependencies of the Company’s own workforce.
The Company has no operations at risk of forced or child labor in any country or business where Repsol operates.

3.1.2 Impact, risk and opportunity management Policies related to the Company’s own workforce (S1-1) All of the policies and standards listed below apply to all Repsol salaried employees and are global in scope.
They are reviewed periodically, taking into account the business needs and the concerns and needs expressed by employees through the different communication channels set up by the Company.

A I II Table of conversions and abbreviations Alternative performance measures III Risks IV V VI ≡ Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information Annual Corporate Governance Report Annual Report on Director Remuneration The Code of Ethics and Business Conduct, Repsol’s People Management Policy, the Human Rights and Community Relations Policy, the Sustainability Policy, the Safety and Occupational Health Policy and the Global Framework for Diversity and Inclusion are described in Repsol's policies related to sustainability2.
The Global Compensation and Well-being Framework establishes a total compensation system that integrates fixed and variable short- and long-term remuneration, along with a defined set of benefits.

This framework ensures compliance with the regulations applicable in all the geographies where the Company operates and is periodically reviewed to adapt to market movements and the macroeconomic context.
There are different remuneration schemes based on the activity performed, which include commercial incentives and variable compensation.
Variable remuneration is linked to both Company and individual performance, with sustainability indicators.
In addition, there are monetary and non-monetary recognition programs, and a benefits package that includes pension plans, medical and life insurance and food assistance, among others.

The occupational health norm sets out guidelines to promote and maintain a healthy, safe work environment, protecting the physical, mental and social health of employees and encouraging a healthy lifestyle both inside and outside the work environment.
The standard includes access to adequate medical resources and services and action plans for medical emergencies.
The labor management standard establishes the structure for the administration of labor relations, promoting efficient and organized management of employees.

It regulates aspects such as the processes of hiring and modification of the employment relationship, professional progression, mobility and international assignments, and sets out the criteria for the management of documentation and employee data while safeguarding privacy.
The implementation of the Global Compensation and Well-being Framework and the aforementioned standards is the responsibility of the People and Organization function.
All of them are available on the Repsol intranet where the person in charge is identified, and employees can provide comments or suggestions on it.
For more information, see Business model and strategy Processes for engaging with own workers and workers’ representatives on impacts (S1-2).

Processes to repair negative impacts and channels for own workers to express their concerns (S1-3) Repsol has its own channels to capture employees’ interests and perspectives, both to activate collaboration and to facilitate the remediation of incidents when appropriate.
These channels are common in both cases and allow any opinion, interest or incident to be managed through the established procedures.

Additionally, all Repsol employees have communication channels to capture and manage their needs and concerns, which are available on an ongoing basis: People and Organization (HR) teams in each business and area, Employee Service (SAE), etc.
In addition, the Ethics and Conduct Channel allows both employees and third parties to express concerns, report possible breaches and communicate negative issues confidentially.
Furthermore, as established by the Code of Ethics and Business Conduct, any type of retaliation is prohibited.
For more information, see Business conduct.
In 2024, the Our Voice program was launched, designed to collect, measure and analyze employee opinions on key aspects for the Organization, such as engagement and job satisfaction.

By 2025, this program has established itself as a tool for assessing employee engagement, well-being and critical moments of professional experience (onboarding, mobility, training, definition of objectives and communication of earnings).

In 2025, a survey was carried out with more than 20,000 employees, with a response rate of 64%, which reflected an overall engagement rate of 78% and a well-being index of 81%, indicators that show strong occupational health.
In addition, the intention to remain in Repsol (90%) stands out, 19 points above the external average, confirming a strong connection of talent with the Company.

Repsol maintains a constant dialogue and guarantees the participation of employees through their legal representatives, in the definition of working conditions and in the creation of an environment based on equality, respect and talent development.

This dialogue is articulated under the principles of mutual respect and open and transparent dialogue, through commissions, committees and negotiation panels (78 in Spain) and, in any case, through the continuous relationship of the social agents with the Labor Relations teams of each country/work center or with the head of People and Organization of each business unit/corporate area.
The People and Organization Department is responsible for leading the people strategy, managing actual and potential impacts and facilitating all channels of collaboration.
For more information on the processes of collaboration with the Company’s employees and workers’ representatives, see Stakeholder engagement processes.

Working conditions Repsol’s goal is to attract, retain and develop talent, providing all employees a competitive value proposition that includes attractive total remuneration, safe and flexible working conditions that promote comprehensive well-being and work-life balance.
In addition, it promotes healthy lifestyle habits and people’s motivation.
This goal takes the shape of the following actions and objectives aimed at fostering healthy lifestyles and boosting individuals’ motivation.
At the same time, they strengthen employability via specific plans and ongoing training that advances professional development and the ability to tackle the challenges of the future.

Since 2024, Repsol has deployed the Company's talent program as a strategic lever to anticipate future needs, attract and retain critical profiles, promote continuous learning, evolve leadership, and strengthen organizational culture.
All this is supported by data- driven management.

Work-life balance metrics (S1-15) In all countries where the Repsol Group operates, 100% of employees can take family leave (maternity, paternity, parental leave or leave for carers), whether under the country’s legislation, local collective bargaining agreements or the global minimum work-life balance rules that apply in all Group companies to improve on or complete local regulations.
In 2025, 4,241 employees took some form of leave (17.18% of the Group's total workforce), of whom 67.74% were men, 32.23% were women, and 0.024% did not provide information on their gender.

This means that of the total number of male employees, 19.92% took leave; of the total number of female employees, 13.34% took leave; and of the total number of employees who did not provide information on their gender, 10.00% took leave.

This means that of the total number of male employees, 19.31% took leave; of the total number of female employees, 12.98%; and of the total number of employees who did not provide information on their gender, 16.67%.

Compensation metrics: pay gap and total compensation (S1-16) Repsol operates in Spain, Peru and Portugal, in different sectors and under different corporate entities subject to different collective agreements, which means very different salary values, depending on the company and the sector.
In certain categories, employees receive the salary values directly determined by collective bargaining agreements, which in no case establish pay differences based on gender.
Analyzing together the wage realities of different sectors under different agreements can lead to inappropriate interpretations.

Comparing similar situations with each other, that is, employees with the same seniority/age and belonging to the same business and professional category, the salary difference not explained on the basis of objective criteria between men and women is reduced globally to 5%, 6% and 4% in 2025, 2024 and 2023, respectively.

Voluntary turnover Calculation of voluntary turnover is the number of departures/ number of active people.

Voluntary turnover is solely counted for employees with a permanent contract, given that they represent the structural workforce of the Company and they allow for more precisely measuring the effectiveness of the value proposition, loyalty of talent, and employment stability.

Including temporary employees would introduce an element of volatility that would be unrelated to people management owing to the seasonal or contractual nature of their relationship, and it would distort the internal and external comparability of the indicator.
Overall gross pay gap This calculation encompasses total cash remuneration per hour and stock bonuses on a cash basis, that is, sums actually received by active employees, in euros.
Employees on international assignment and employees who left before December 31 of the year in question are excluded.

Repsol operates in Spain, Peru and Portugal, in different sectors and under different corporate entities subject to different collective agreements, which means very different salaries, depending on the company and the sector.
In certain categories, employees receive the salary values directly determined by collective bargaining agreements, which in no case establish pay differences based on gender.
METHODOLOGY Jointly analyzing wage realities of different sectors and under different agreements leads to inappropriate interpretations.

Considering more comparable situations, that is, employees belonging to the same business, with the same professional category and similar seniority/age, the salary difference is reduced to 5%, 6%, 4% and 5% in 2025, 2024, 2023 and 2022, respectively.
The decrease in the gap in 2025 is due to the exit of Repsol Resources UK from the reporting scope and the gender distribution of employees.
Country-adjusted pay gap (%) The total cash remuneration per hour and stock bonuses received by employees and expressed in euros, has been considered on a cash basis.
Employees on international assignment and employees who have left before December 31 of the year in question are excluded.

In order to offer a value that allows a comparison between similar situations, the pay gap is calculated by comparing the remuneration of men and women with the same main determining metrics (business/company, professional category, age/seniority).
The pay gap has been calculated as follows: (average male remuneration - average female remuneration / average male remuneration) x 100.

Total annual remuneration ratio The methodology for calculating the total annual remuneration ratio (S1‑16) was revised to fully adapt it to the requirements of the CSDR Directive and to the specifications of ESRS S1.
As a result of this methodological adjustment, the 2024 figure is again provided in order to support comparability and fully document year-on-year changes.
The indicator shows the relationship between the total monetary remuneration of the person with the highest remuneration and the median of the population.
For the calculation, the total monetary remuneration plus stock bonuses have been included on a cash basis, that is, the amounts actually received by employees during the year.
The person with the highest remuneration has been identified considering all employees, excluding expatriate personnel from other origins and employees who left before December 31, 2025.

A I II Table of conversions and abbreviations Alternative performance measures III Risks IV V VI ≡ Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information Annual Corporate Governance Report Annual Report on Director Remuneration 3.2 Workers in the value chain 3.2.1 Strategy Repsol’s value chain includes suppliers, partners, distributors, and customers, marketing a wide range of products, and encompassing activities such as oil and gas Upstream, electricity production and sales, refining, chemicals, and product marketing.
Supply chain description Repsol’s supply chain is global, mirroring the scope of its operations.
It includes suppliers with registered offices in any region of the world, with a predominance of local purchasing and contracting.
The main suppliers, those considered key1, have their registered offices in Europe and North America.
Interests and views of stakeholders (ESRS 2 SBM-2) The map of interest groups, revised annually, consists of nine groups and 40 subgroups.

The suppliers, contractors and partners are the most significant stakeholders in relation to value chain workers, as described in Processes for engaging with value chain workers (S2-2), Business model and strategy, Materiality assessment and Affected communities.

Material impacts, risks, and opportunities2 and their interaction with the strategy and the business model (ESRS 2 SBM-3) The information reported in this section relates mainly to partners and suppliers of Tier 1 materials and services3.
Currently, partial information is available at Tier 2 level and, occasionally, at Tier 3 level, in which case it is specifically detailed.
Repsol is working to expand information on workers in the value chain in the future.
The double materiality assessment is detailed in Business model and strategy and Materiality assessment.
The material negative impacts identified are: • Industrial accidents that can negatively affect workers throughout Repsol’s value chain (IRO-37).

The positive material impacts associated with workers in the value chain are the following: • Inclusion of contractual clauses requiring respect for human rights in agreements with suppliers and contractors (IRO-38).
• Promotion of the improvement of the working conditions, health and safety of workers in the value chain through the inclusion of ESG criteria (IRO-39).

The types of workers who are potentially impacted by value chain activities (also VC) are the following (including particularly vulnerable chain VC workers):4 • Workers who are not part of the Company’s own workforce, but work at the Company’s site or at its facilities.
• Workers belonging to entities that carry out their activities in the previous phases of the value chain.
• Workers belonging to entities that carry out their activities in the downstream stages of the value chain, such as those involved in logistics or distribution activities or franchising.

All these workers are potentially exposed to different impacts, which may be generalized or systemic (countries and types of procurement with human rights and social risks), or of a one-off nature (for example, due to an industrial incident or the detection of reputational risk in a supplier).
With respect to systemic impacts, the countries at highest social risk in which Repsol operates are Peru, Venezuela, and Bolivia.

The activities with the greatest social risk are those related to security detection services and systems, textile production, and mineral extraction.5 More detail is provided in Adoption of measures related to material impacts on workers in the value chain (S2-4).

3.2.2 Impact, risk and opportunity management Policies related to workers in the value chain (S2-1) The policies related to workers in the value chain, which are detailed below, reflect Repsol’s respect for human rights, as well as to the main international standards and agreements in this area.
Repsol’s policies are developed in management processes, such as collaboration with workers in the value chain or those for repairing negative impacts.
The processes include measures that make it possible to remedy possible human rights incidents that affect workers in the value chain.

Among these measures are due diligence, the inclusion of the different requirements in all bidding processes through the 1 A key supplier is considered to be one with an awarded amount greater than 50 M€ in the reporting year.

2 In the assessment of impacts, risks, and opportunities related to workers in the value chain carried out through double materiality assessment, there are no material risks and opportunities, so the section focuses on impact management.
3 Tier 1 is the supplier that maintains the direct contractual relationship with Repsol; Tier 2 is the supplier of Tier 1; and Tier 3 is the supplier of Tier 2 and successive ones.

A I II Table of conversions and abbreviations Alternative performance measures III Risks IV V VI ≡ Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information Annual Corporate Governance Report Annual Report on Director Remuneration General Conditions of Purchase and Contract6 or standardized and/or specific clauses, and performance evaluation.
Repsol intends for third parties subject to the codes of ethics and policies detailed below to share such conduct and commitments with their own employees.

Repsol’s Code of Ethics and Conduct The purpose of Repsol’s Code of Ethics and Business Conduct is to establish the framework for understanding and putting into practice the conduct and standards that the Company expects from each of its employees.

Partners, contractors, suppliers, and other collaborating companies are an extension of Repsol and, for this reason, must act in accordance with this code and any other applicable contractual provisions when acting on behalf of or in collaboration with the Company.
Repsol also encourages business partners to develop and implement ethical programs that are consistent with its own standards, as detailed in Business conduct.

Code of Ethics and Business Conduct for Suppliers The Code of Ethics and Business Conduct for Suppliers7 establishes the minimum principles that the Company expects from its suppliers, promoting their knowledge and acceptance before starting the contractual relationship, as well as their compliance throughout the business relationship.

This Code articulates the minimum behavior guidelines that are reasonably expected of suppliers throughout their supply chain, reflecting their commitment to respect of human rights and the main international standards and agreements in this area: the International Bill of Human Rights8, the principles relating to rights established by the International Labour Organization (ILO) concerning fundamental principles and rights at work and the eight Fundamental Conventions9 that implement them.

The Code includes, among others, provisions on forced labor, child labor and human trafficking, gender equality, freedom of association, the right to collective bargaining, integrity and ethical behavior, measures against bribery, corruption and conflicts of interest, environmental protection and security, as well as the protection and transparency of information to promote a sustainable economy.

The Code of Ethics and Business Conduct for Suppliers, which is available on the corporate website, must be accepted by all suppliers who pass the due diligence phase and participate in a bidding process with any part of the Company, where the obligations undertaken by the supplier shall also applicable to its employees, subcontractors and subcontractors.
Human Rights and Community Relations Policy The Human Rights and Community Relations Policy is aligned with the main international treaties and standards in this area.

Repsol expects its partners, suppliers, contractors, distributors and other agents with whom it maintains business relationships to respect human rights of their workers in the carrying out of their activities anywhere in the world and, consequently, to address any negative impacts they have have a role in, as detailed in Affected communities.

Commercial Relations Policy with Third Parties The purpose of the Commercial Relations with Third Parties Policy is to establish relationships based on the law, ethical principles, integrity and values of the Company with the partners, suppliers, contractors and customers who collaborate with Repsol in the development of business activities and with whom it establishes commercial or business relationships.
It applies to all Repsol employees and to the third parties with whom the Company is related.

Safety and Occupational Health Policy The Safety and Occupational Health Policy establishes the objective of carrying out all the Company’s activities while preserving the integrity of people and avoiding any damage to the environment and surroundings, as well as supporting a healthy and safe work environment from both a physical and emotional point of view.
Repsol seeks to ensure, as far as possible, that its suppliers, contractors, and partners share the commitments set out in the Policy and provide a safe environment for their workers.
In the event of a conflict between safety or health and operational results, all employees and contractors have the responsibility to choose safety, with management always supporting this choice.
6 The General Terms and Conditions of Purchase and Contract are intended to regulate the relations between Repsol and its suppliers.
They apply to all suppliers and subcontracted companies.

They establish the obligation of the supplier to respect internationally recognized human rights that include, at least, the rights set forth in the International Bill of Human Rights and the principles relating to fundamental rights established in the Declaration of the International Labour Organization.

In addition, they establish the requirement to comply with all the provisions related to respect for human rights that are in force in the Repsol Group’s internal regulations and practice, such as the elimination of child labor, all forms of forced labor, human trafficking, slavery or servitude throughout the life cycle of the goods and services contracted and non-discrimination on the basis of nationality, race, ethnicity, religion, political affiliation, marital status, social status, age, disability, culture, sex, gender identity, sexual orientation, or any other status.

They also include the requirement for the supplier to comply with the current provisions relating to safety and the environment, as well as the applicable regulations and internal practice of the Repsol Group.
They are available on the corporate website, following this path: Home > Sustainability > Sustainability pillars > People > Human rights > Business relationships > Suppliers and contractors.

7 The Supplier Code of Ethics and Conduct is available on Repsol’s corporate website, following this path: Home > Sustainability > Sustainability pillars > People > Human rights > Business relationships > Suppliers and contractors.

8 The International Bill of Human Rights is known as the Universal Declaration of Human Rights (1948) along with the instruments that develop it: The International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights (1976) and the International Covenant on Economic, Social and Cultural Rights (1976).

9 The ILO's eight Core Conventions include: Freedom of association and protection of the right to organize convention (1948), Right to organize and collective bargaining convention (1949), Forced labor convention (1930), Abolition of forced labor convention (1957), Minimum age convention (1973), Worst forms of child labor convention (1999), Equal remuneration convention (1951), Discrimination convention (1958).
