Processes for engaging with value chain workers (S2-2) Collaboration with suppliers and contractors Repsol manages its supply chain by responding to both the expectations of stakeholders and complying with ethical, labor, environmental, safety and human rights standards.

The expectations of the different stakeholders, including the Company’s suppliers, are met through materiality assessment and the establishment of a collaboration process, as outlined in the internal regulations.

The collaboration process is carried out in two phases: initially, by the Purchasing areas and, subsequently, by the areas that use the goods or services requested through the person responsible for the contract.
For this reason, there is no single person responsible for the process.
Purchasing carries out a first phase of search, selection and due diligence of the supplier and, subsequently, the contracting process (bidding, awarding and contracting).
During the process, the supplier’s expectations are addressed, and are reflected in the commitment formalized by the two parties.

In order to comply with current legislation regarding the illegal assignment of workers10, Repsol’s internal Purchasing and Contracting regulations regulate the nature of the individual relationship with the suppliers’ workers, which should be conducted through the representative of the contracted company.
The regulations also provide that the supplier may not subcontract the execution of the service subject to the contract without the prior written consent of Repsol.

According to internal purchasing regulations, the person responsible for the contract must maintain a relationship with the supplier’s representative throughout the duration of the contract so as to ensure fulfillment of the expectations of both parties and the addressing of any new needs that arise during the lifetime of the contract.
The frequency of contact between the contractor and the supplier’s representative is defined by the parties according to the type of service.

The person responsible for the contract has the following responsibilities: • Act as Repsol’s technical representative with the supplier during the contract and keep a written record of any significant communications with the supplier.
• Perform material inspection/service verification, validating that the goods or services listed on the goods receipt have actually been received.
• Promote compliance with all contractual obligations.
• Periodically evaluate suppliers’ performance to identify risk situations that may require corrective actions, termination or suspension of the relationship.

The evaluation of the effectiveness of the collaboration process is carried out in two phases: for the duration of the contract, by the person responsible for the contract and the supplier; and in performance evaluation, by the person responsible for the contract, as described in Adoption of measures related to material impacts on workers in the value chain (S2-4).
Collaboration with suppliers on safety Within the framework of the Safety Excellence11 Program, Repsol maintains continuous collaboration with its contractors regarding safety.
In addition, group communication actions are developed on specific aspects of interest to relevant contractors, as detailed in Safety and security.
Collaboration with partners The scope of this section is limited to new Repsol partners.
Before establishing new relationships with potential partners through a Mergers and Acquisitions (M&A) process, Repsol conducts a complete due diligence process on each transaction.
The responsibility for coordinating and leading the M&A process lies with the M&A Department or the Business Development Department, depending on the nature of the transaction.
In this process, social, environmental and business conduct aspects, among others, are evaluated.

The process includes requesting information through an initial questionnaire, analyzing the information provided generally through a Virtual Data Room, question-and- answer sessions, and specific meetings with the workers responsible for the different areas.

Relations with partners are contractually regulated in the Shareholders’ Agreement, which includes clauses on issues related to governance, corporate policies, ethical compliance, sustainability, anti-corruption, and auditing.

In the event that risks or aspects for improvement related to social, environmental, or governance issues have been identified in the due diligence process, Repsol has the prerogative of introducing corrective or commitment clauses in the Shareholders' Agreement to achieve the necessary standards, along with the contractual mechanism, in order to verify corrective measures or commitments subsequent to the signing of the contract.

A I II Table of conversions and abbreviations Alternative performance measures III Risks IV V VI ≡ Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information Annual Corporate Governance Report Annual Report on Director Remuneration Processes to repair negative impacts and channels for value chain workers to voice their concerns (S2-3) Repsol has collaboration processes with suppliers and contractors that take into account the actual and potential impacts that may affect workers in the value chain.
In this way, it encourages suppliers, contractors and partners to collaborate with their own workers.
These processes are designed with the aim to include all the workers in the value chain, separately from workers engaged in industrial activity, which is where negative impacts have been identified.

Details are provided below on some of the main processes carried out to repair negative and potential impacts: Ethics and Compliance Channel Repsol is committed to the culture of compliance and ethical integrity and understands that, in this context, individuals who report possible irregular or unlawful behavior play a key role in discovering and preventing such violations, protecting the proper functioning of the Company and contributing to the well-being of society as a whole.
The Company has an Ethics and Compliance Channel, as well as an internal information system.
This channel is available on Repsol’s corporate website to anyone, including employees and third parties, suppliers, contractors and subcontractors.
The existence of the channel is included in the Code of Ethics and Business Conduct of Suppliers for its knowledge and acceptance according to the approved regulations.
It is shared at different stages of the purchasing and contracting processes.
For more information on the functioning of the Ethics and Compliance Channel and on the process of managing reports, see Ethics and Compliance Channel and Process of managing communications.

Operational grievance mechanisms Grievance mechanisms are a valuable source of information for detecting potential human rights impacts on communities, suppliers and contractors, partners, customers or other stakeholders.

In the event that the Company detects any adverse impacts caused by activities directly related to its business, it undertakes to address them in agreement with the affected parties in order to achieve an effective and satisfactory remedy.

In such a case, the affected population is identified, the nature and magnitude of the impacts are evaluated following internationally accepted standard methodologies and a remediation solution is proposed, which is discussed with the affected parties to obtain their approval and reach a satisfactory agreement between the parties.

A I II Table of conversions and abbreviations Alternative performance measures III Risks IV V VI ≡ Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information Annual Corporate Governance Report Annual Report on Director Remuneration Evaluating the results of due diligence measures is crucial in order to identify any significant evidence of compliance.
If any risk is detected, possible mitigation measures are identified and assessed.
The final decision on the formalization of the relationship with the third party rests with the contracting area.
In addition, a regular update of due diligence measures is required every two years or when there are significant changes in the circumstances of the third party.
For this reason, continuous monitoring of active counterparties is carried out as the case may require.
The time horizon tied to this action is not limited, given that it is integral to the ESG strategy.

Managing negative impacts in the supply chain Repsol has a supply chain risk management model to identify, assess, and minimize environmental, social, and governance (ESG) risks at different stages of the purchasing process.

Application of the due diligence standard The Repsol Group’s third-party due diligence standard establishes the necessary measures to allow adequate knowledge of the third parties with whom the Company engages or will engage, in relation to compliance risks.

This standard aims to provide sufficient information for decision-making and the evaluation and mitigation of potential compliance risks associated with third parties, both before formalizing the relationship and during its validity.

The standard is applicable to all employees of the Repsol Group, regardless of their type of contract, position, or place of work, provided that in the performance of their duties they interact with third parties (suppliers, partners, and customers, among others).

The due diligence process obliges contracting areas to familiarize themselves with the third parties and to apply appropriate due diligence measures based on the risk or indication of risk assigned to the third party.
Knowledge of third parties, their activity and background is facilitated through searches in public and private databases, integrity reports, and external questionnaires.
As a result of these analyses, additional measures may be applied according to the criteria of the Compliance department12 and in consideration of the risk for operations.

Managing supply chain risk 12 The Gas Trading and Wholesale businesses have integrated compliance departments, which have autonomy in decision-making regarding the due diligence of the third parties involved in their operations.

A I II Table of conversions and abbreviations Alternative performance measures III Risks IV V VI ≡ Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information Annual Corporate Governance Report Annual Report on Director Remuneration In the due diligence phase, suppliers are registered in the procurement management systems, having to pass an analysis related to aspects of integrity, anti- corruption, bribery, sanctions, international embargoes and social aspects.
In 2025, a reputational analysis was carried out on 25,25113 suppliers worldwide (in 2024, 28,899 suppliers were evaluated).
As a result, relevant information has been found regarding these aspects of 63 companies with which purchases or contracts have been made.
This process is continuous for suppliers registered in purchasing systems.
The relevant aspects identified in the reputational analysis are managed jointly by the Compliance and Purchasing areas, and help to make decisions in the different phases of the purchasing process.
After the reputational analysis, the supplier is assigned a rating with respect to operations that are significant to the Company, based on economic and financial criteria.

In the purchase and contracting phase, through specific contractual clauses, the Company requires a compliance with international standards, and provisions relating to safety, the environment, data protection, information security, cybersecurity, ethical conduct and respect for human rights in its internal regulations.
In addition, the supplier’s acceptance of the General Conditions of Purchase and Contract before or at the time of submitting its offer is encouraged.
Likewise, both the award letter and the order refer to these General Conditions of Purchase and Contract, and are considered a binding contract between parties.

Depending on the purpose of the award, it may be necessary to complete various compliance questionnaires that allow suppliers to share information on the control mechanisms they have put into place to mitigate different risks on the safety of operations and/ or compliance with additional legal requirements (data protection, illegal transfer of workers, cybersecurity, international sanctions on software and technology goods, etc.).

During the bidding process, in acquisitions of goods and services of high risk in safety and environment, it is required to introduce a minimum weight in the weighting criteria of the matrix of analysis, evaluation, comparison and alignment of bids, with a minimum acceptable value required.
In the tender process, safety and environmental reference persons evaluate suppliers in significant aspects of the new contract.
Subsequently, specific requirements are established in the contractual clauses.

In addition, in the Upstream business, a target for 2026 has been defined that perfects this process and is described in more detail in Targets related to managing material negative impacts, driving positive impacts, and managing material risks and opportunities (S2-5).

Supporting the effective implementation of the impact management process There is a process of periodic evaluation of the performance of suppliers in terms of safety, environment, quality and management in order to identify risk situations that may require corrective actions, termination or suspension of the relationship.
In the case of goods and services with a high risk in safety and the environment, an action plan is drawn up jointly with the supplier if the evaluation falls short of a certain threshold.
In 2025, 2,744 performance evaluations were carried out on 1,683 suppliers (in 2024, 2,381 performance evaluations were carried out on 1,569 suppliers).

Within the performance evaluation procedure, there is a supplier disqualification process, whereby any current and future business relationship with a given supplier can be blocked for a period of time or even indefinitely.
In 2025, no provider was disqualified for ESG reasons.
There were no severe impacts on human rights, nor breaches of contract due to social or environmental impacts in the year related to the previous and subsequent phases of its value chain.
The Company has auditing procedures, both internal and external, as well as internal control procedures to verify and oversee that the established requirements are met.

Different areas participate in the process of managing material impacts related to workers in the value chain, supported by financial and technological resources: • Contracting areas: Responsible for adopting the minimum diligence measures based on the risk associated with the third party prior to the formalization of the commercial relationship and for as long as it continues.

• Compliance function: Responsible for preparing a report on compliance, in the event that significant evidence is identified that may be indicative of conduct that is unethical, incompatible with or contrary to Repsol’s principles of action, identifying Repsol’s main risks of compliance and, where appropriate, including appropriate risk mitigation measures, in order to prevent or mitigate the materialization of the identified risks.
It is also responsible for managing the Ethics and Compliance Channel and, if necessary, for making a proposal of recommended improvement and action plans.

• Safety and Environment function: Responsible for defining which activities and purchases are of high risk in safety and the environment, as well as evaluating the risk and other significant aspects of suppliers prior to contracting.

Training and awareness for those involved in purchasing processes In order to promote ESG training for employees in the Value Chain (VC), Repsol has collaborated with the Spanish Network of the United Nations Global Compact to boost the participation of small and medium-sized companies in its global supply chain in a training program.

The training itinerary covers general aspects of sustainability, SDGs, the 10 Principles of the Global Compact (human rights, labor standards, environment and the fight against corruption), communication and sustainability reporting.

Repsol has collaborated since the beginning of the program in 2023 with the aim of training workers in SMEs integrated into the supply chains of large Spanish companies that act as promoters of the project.
Likewise, objectives are established annually to train employees in the Purchasing department in the areas of ethics and conduct, cybersecurity risks, and other pertinent sustainability topics.

In industrial complexes, LPG factories and renewable generation assets, mandatory training is provided on the risks of the facilities where workers are employed, regardless of whether they are company personnel or not.

The time horizon tied to this action is not limited, given that it is integral to the ESG strategy Positive impact on the supply chain Repsol promotes the participation of local companies in its network of suppliers and contractors and, in doing so, favors the generation of employment and the protection of workers’ rights in local communities, improving economic and social development in the areas in which it operates.
Local employment makes it possible to establish links that promote sustainability and joint growth.
Geographical proximity optimizes logistics efficiency and allows Repsol and its local partners to adapt quickly to changing market dynamics.
In addition to the direct contribution to employment, Repsol creates indirect job opportunities through its projects.

During 2025, 82% (in 2024, it accounted for 85%) of the total volume of the Company’s purchases has been made locally, covering a wide variety of services essential for operations across all geographies.
The number of local suppliers represents 85% (in 2024, it accounted for 85%) of the Company’s total.

Interaction with stakeholders is key to maintaining the strength of the Company’s business model.

For this reason, work is done to identify and understand their expectations regarding Repsol’s sustainability and human rights performance, and thus be able to incorporate them into decision-making processes.
The communities in the area of influence of the activities constitute one of the main stakeholders in the Company’s business strategy.
In order to define the area of influence, Repsol applies a criterion of physical proximity to its operations and the environment that may be affected by them.
In addition, resources and processes are allocated for managing human rights- related risks, impacts, and opportunities.

Before commencing any activity that may involve interaction or impact on the community, Repsol conducts a social baseline study that helps to learn about and examine the context and social, economic, and cultural realities of the territory.

Based on these studies and in collaboration with local authorities and social organizations, stakeholders are identified to find out their expectations, needs, concerns and aspirations, as well as their vulnerability to impacts.

These groups include neighbors, local authorities and associations, customers and suppliers, as well as all those legitimate rights holders on whom the Company may have an impact, with a special focus on the most vulnerable groups, namely those who, due to their nature or intrinsic or external environmental conditions, may find it more difficult to enjoy their fundamental human rights or see them compromised.
Notably, indigenous communities are considered to be especially vulnerable to the Company’s activities.

Repsol respects and values the unique nature of these communities, their right to participation and their rights to lands and natural resources, as well as the right to their autonomy and self-government and to promote their cultures.

Actions in environments with indigenous communities are governed by Convention 169 of the International Labour Organization (ILO), regardless of whether or not it is envisaged in the legislation of each country.

As a result of communication with key stakeholders, and especially those who may be considered vulnerable, actual and potential impacts are identified and the business strategy is defined in this regard.
Repsol takes into account the opinion of nearby communities about the impacts that its operations may generate.
Early engagement allows modifications to be incorporated preventively in the development of projects when necessary to limit impacts.
In particular, it is common in Low-Carbon Generation projects to reconsider the location of installations.
And, as a unique case, we have the no-go decision that was reached, in a culturally sensitive area for the Wayuu Indigenous Community during an exploration project in La Guajira, Colombia.
Under the Global Plan and the Local Sustainability Plans, annual objectives are established that take account of the expectations of local communities.

Material impacts, risks, and opportunities and their interaction with strategy and business model (ESRS 2 SBM-3) Repsol has carried out a double materiality assessment, in which the material impacts, risks, and opportunities in relation to sustainability have been identified.

This assessment is based on the findings made during the implementation of the human rights due diligence process to identify, prevent, mitigate, and remediate the risks and social impacts associated with its operations.

This internal processes management system has a preventive approach and is based on carrying out social impact assessments (on human rights), the implementation of social action plans, their monitoring and accountability.
Moreover, Repsol has consultation mechanisms in place to encourage the participation of local communities in the process, as well as channels for reporting grievances and conflict resolution.

This process includes the following actions: • Exhaustive analysis of the context and the social, economic and cultural realities of each area as well as potentially affected stakeholders, analyzing their degree of vulnerability.
• Identification and assessment of risks, and of negative and positive impacts.
• Design and implementation of mitigation plans for risks and negative impacts, or remediation when they have materialized.
• Development and implementation of consultation mechanisms, grievance mechanisms, and other communication tools.
• Identification of social opportunities to maximize positive impacts.

This engagement focuses on effective risk and impact management and on improving the quality of life and sustainable development of the communities in the vicinity of operations, thus contributing to obtaining and maintaining the social license to operate.

As a result of the materiality assessment, the following negative impacts derived from the Company’s activity have been identified as material: • Operations carried out by Repsol or its contractors may negatively affect indigenous communities.
These impacts may include the alteration of their traditional ways of life, cultural heritage, or effects on the use of lands and natural resources (IRO-40).

• The change in land use caused by our projects can generate disruptions in the quality, way of life and rights of communities (impact on the landscape, eventual increase in the cost of land, depreciation of assets belonging to local communities) (IRO-42).
• Repsol’s commitment to shared value and the communities in the area of influence of our assets and projects is promoted by hiring local labor, goods, and services (IRO-43).

If it occurs, or if it is identified as a possible occurrence, an avoidance or mitigation plan is drawn up, which may be incorporated into normal operating procedures, if applicable.

Usual potential negative impacts Affected groups Odors, noise, emissions into the atmosphere of gases and dust Local communities Indigenous populations and other vulnerable groups Landscape impacts (wind and solar projects) Local communities The analysis of human rights risks is part of the Company’s integrated risk management system, through which the different businesses and corporate areas identify, measure, manage and supervise the main material risks.
This system is based on a Risk Management Policy that identifies the risks of rejection, including ethical and behavioral risks, such as those associated with human rights abuses.

Their probability of occurrence or associated impact must be minimized, according to the commitment set out in Repsol’s Human Rights and Community Relations Policy and also its Code of Ethics and Business Conduct.

In accordance with the Enterprise Risk Management System, the risk profile is reported for formal validation at least annually to the units responsible for direct risk management in day-to-day operations.

These units are responsible for promoting, when appropriate, the corresponding actions or developing the necessary control mechanisms to align the risk profile with the expectations of the organization, according to the risk tolerance statement reflected in the Risk Management Policy.

Similarly, and at least annually, the Audit and Control Committee evaluates the effectiveness of the management and control systems for these risks and ESG risks are also presented to the Sustainability Committee.
In addition, the Executive Committee is part of the Enterprise Risk Management System.
Accordingly, its functions include supervising the Company’s performance in matters related to these risks.

A I II Table of conversions and abbreviations Alternative performance measures III Risks IV V VI ≡ Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information Annual Corporate Governance Report Annual Report on Director Remuneration Other supervisory units include the Sustainability Department, which promotes the integration of risk vision to effectively influence decision-making processes, and the risk auditing units, which evaluate the design and operation of risk management and control systems.
Repsol uses its influence to extend the implementation of human rights due diligence throughout the value chain.

In non-operated assets, commitments, policies and practices are shared with partners and they are offered the Company’s knowledge, experience and techniques necessary for alignment with corporate standards.

Among partners, suppliers, and contractors, Repsol also promotes respect for human rights through the inclusion of specific clauses in its contracts, the conduct of social audits, and training and awareness on human rights.
For more information, see Workers in the value chain.
The Company collaborates with other organizations and industry initiatives to share best practices and make progress in the implementation of international standards.
In particular, Repsol actively participates in different IPIECA working groups, including those on social responsibility and SDGs.
In addition, this year, for the first time, Repsol carried out a social impact measurement based on the Prosper4ALL methodology and received the Socially Committed seal.

The commitment to human rights is embraced by the Board of Directors and the Executive Committee, and it encompasses all the people who work at the Company, all of whom must respect human rights in all their decisions and activities.
This goal also applies to contractors, suppliers and partners.
For more information, see Material impacts, risks, and opportunities and their interaction with the strategy and business model.

3.3.2 Impact, risk and opportunity management Policies related to affected communities (S3-1) Repsol incorporates respect for human rights recognized in international treaties and standards as a core principle of its policy.

To this end, it defines its regulatory framework based on the most exacting international standards, such as the United Nations Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights, and on permanent and transparent dialogue with all its stakeholders.

The Human Rights and Community Relations Policy, approved in 2008 and updated in 2023 to adapt it to new regulatory standards, is the reference framework that embodies the commitment of top management and the guidelines for the management of material impacts, risks, and opportunities in this area.

It expresses the corporate commitment to: • Integrating human rights due diligence into all of the Company’s business activities and relationships, in all countries where it operates, and in all phases of the life cycle, including decommissioning.
• Remedying negative impacts on human rights generated by the activity.

• Building strong relationships and respect the human rights of all people potentially affected by the Company’s activity, including the communities in the areas of influence, the people who work at Repsol, as well as its customers, among others.
• Contributing to the sustainable socio-economic development of local communities and the preservation of their environment.
• Working with partners and suppliers in any activity to drive compliance with human rights due diligence and to remediate adverse impacts related to operations, products or services.

The implementation of the policy is the responsibility of the Company’s Executive Committee and the process of monitoring and oversight of its application and result is carried out by the Audit and Internal Control Unit.

The scope of application of the policy extends to all activities and business relationships, and in all phases of the life cycle of activities, from preliminary studies, design, construction, commissioning, production and decommissioning or transfer of assets.

The results of the double materiality assessment were taken into account when drawing up the policy, thus ensuring that the demands of stakeholders such as investors, the financial community and rating agencies, among others, are taken account of.
Knowing this policy and the processes derived from it is key to proper risk and impact management.
During 2025, in addition to the 22,126 people who completed the ethics and conduct course, 214 people took specific courses on human rights.

Furthermore, specific actions are carried out to disseminate the policy within the framework of dialogue with stakeholders at the beginning of the projects, in particularly sensitive contexts with the presence of indigenous communities, or in those with a greater risk of impact or significant impacts.

For example, in the Bolivia business unit, regular activities are carried out to raise awareness among contractors and communities about human rights and, in particular, about the commitments set out in our Human Rights and Community Relations Policy.
The Code of Ethics and Business Conduct, which is based on our values, regulates our responsibilities in terms of harassment and discrimination and equal opportunities, among other matters.
The Code of Ethics and Business Conduct for Suppliers, which is available on Repsol’s corporate website, lays down the minimum principles that the Company expects from its suppliers.
Prior to entering into a contractual relationship with a supplier, The Company seeks to promote their awareness of and compliance with these principles throughout the commercial relationship.
The code’s acceptance is required during the registration process in order to work with the Company.

The Code is oriented toward mutual benefit, and includes, in the social sphere, obligations such as the rejection of child labor and forced labor, freedom of association and the right to collective bargaining, as well as ethical behavior and measures against bribery, corruption and conflicts of interest.

A I II Table of conversions and abbreviations Alternative performance measures III Risks IV V VI ≡ Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information Annual Corporate Governance Report Annual Report on Director Remuneration Regarding the impact that suppliers may have on the communities in the area of influence, this code explicitly includes obligations set out in the Human Rights and Community Relations Policy: • Recognize and respect internationally recognized human rights and set out in this policy.
• Respect the rights, cultural diversity and customs of local communities, indigenous peoples and other vulnerable groups that may be influenced by their activities.

• Workers and any stakeholders must have at their disposal a grievance mechanism, be suitably informed of it, which allows them to communicate any complaint or breach of the code without risk of retaliation, and which allows its communication and management in a consistent, fair, accessible and prompt manner.

In 2025, a comprehensive review of the internal standard on human rights and the relationship with communities was carried out, with two objectives: • Incorporating consolidated good practices derived from the implementation of due diligence in the different businesses and those requested by different stakeholders.
• Fostering alignment with new regulations regarding sustainability reporting and due diligence in sustainability.

The degree of detail in the definition of the due diligence process has been increased, the section on partners and contractors has been reinforced, the need for stakeholder participation is emphasized, lessons learned in the remediation process derived from the La Pampilla spill have been included, as well as specific mentions of vulnerable groups such as children or women.

Repsol has been a signatory of the United Nations Voluntary Principles on Security and Human Rights since 2013, with the aim of guaranteeing the security of operations in sensitive or conflict zones through working procedures that support respect for human rights.

Processes for engaging with affected communities (S3-2) Repsol applies the Environmental, Social, and Health Impact Assessment (ESHIA) standard, which aims to ensure that environmental, social, and health impacts are properly identified and mitigated.

This process is carried out through a participatory and proactive dialogue with stakeholders, providing them with relevant information and engaging them in the actions to be undertaken as defined in the prevention, mitigation and surveillance plan derived from the impact studies.

Measures to repair environmental, social and health impacts are defined in a participatory manner with the people potentially affected, taking into account their needs and priorities, without being limited to direct monetary compensation.

Here are some examples of participation processes: • At the Low-Carbon Generation and Upstream businesses, there is the role of community liaison officer, who is responsible for establishing and maintaining a dialogue between the Company and the community in which it is operating, based on trust and respect.
• In the Industrial area, there are channels for direct dialogue, such as public advisory panels, in which the concerns and interests of the neighbors are addressed.

Each panel is a permanent channel of dialogue between society and the Company; an advisory body that seeks to improve the population’s knowledge of the Company’s activity and promote informative actions for a better awareness of the industrial operation and answering questions that are frequently asked by society about the industry.
• Collaboration with local institutions that safeguard the interests of the community in order to manage the risks, impacts, and opportunities of the activity.

In addition to these mechanisms for consultation and participation with local communities, there are local mechanisms and channels for reporting and resolving conflicts in the form of operational grievance mechanisms that allow us to anticipate potential controversies, by gathering stakeholders’ concerns, doubts, queries and complaints.
In the particular case of the management of indigenous communities, a key aspect is respect for their right to free, prior and informed consultation.
Repsol verifies the level of acceptance of indigenous organizations and actively seeks the consent of those potentially affected.

Repsol operates in areas featuring the presence of indigenous communities in six countries: Algeria, one operation; Bolivia, one operation; Chile, one operation; United States, two operations; Libya, two operations; and Peru, one operation.

All of the above-mentioned operations have one of the following elements: public consultation and consultation plans, baseline studies, social impact assessments and action plans, relocation plans, community development plans, grievance and complaint procedures and other community information center documents.
100% of significant assets have local community development programs based on local community needs and stakeholder engagement plans based on their geographical distribution.
As in 2024, no incidents involving violations of the rights of indigenous peoples were recorded in 2025 through the Company’s channels.

A key tool that Repsol uses to enhance positive impacts on the communities affected by its activity are social investment initiatives, of which entrepreneurship projects in local communities are prioritized to avoid future dependencies.
The goal is to promote sustainable socioeconomic development through planning based on dialogue and consensus with communities, which are factors that determine the scope of investment.

For more information on social investment, see Metrics related to affected groups.

In order to manage potential impacts arising from the relationship between the communities and the security forces that protect our assets, Repsol requires that 100% of the employees of private security companies who provide services at its facilities have human rights training.
In accordance with the Voluntary Principles on Security and Human Rights to which the Company adheres, this training is verified or courses are held for security personnel.

Processes to remediate negative impacts and channels for affected communities to raise concerns (S3-3) Repsol has grievance mechanisms designed with the participation of communities, taking into account their customs and traditions so that they are accessible and effective.

Relevant complaints are handled at the local level and transferred to the expert team in communities and human rights of the Sustainability Department, and, if necessary, they are escalated to the Executive Committee for further scrutiny and handling.
In addition, as set out in the Code of Ethics and Business Conduct, any form of retaliation is prohibited.

In 2025, a total of 56 complaints were received from local communities (excluding complaints relating to the incident at Terminal 2 of the La Pampilla refinery in Peru, of which 82 were this year) and 98% of these complaints were resolved.

Community complaints Moreover, there were no significant disputes during the period with local communities and indigenous peoples or non-technical shutdowns as a result of the Organization’s activities.
There were also no involuntary resettlements in the activities operated by the Company.
In the event of significant negative impacts, the Company conducts a due diligence process to manage remediation in accordance with the United Nations Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights.
Following the accidental spill that occurred at Terminal 2 of the La Pampilla refinery, a Social Management Plan was launched.
The remediation phase concluded with individual compensation provided to more than 99% of the affected persons identified by the Government.

The third phase of the Social Management Plan began in 2023, continuing in 2024 and 2025 with the ImpulsaRed program for the development and socioeconomic recovery of the communities affected by the spill.

The design of the Social Management Plan was fed with the results of the human rights impact assessment carried out in 2022 for the five districts affected by the spill: Ventanilla, Santa Rosa, Ancón, Aucallama and Chancay, and in permanent dialogue with the target population of the program.
It is an initiative born from collaboration with affected communities through field work with workshops held in the five districts and interviews with community representatives.
The program promotes training and the creation of enterprises.

Meanwhile, a grievance mechanism was designed and implemented with different channels: in-person (reporting desk, community liaison officers), telephone, electronic messaging, email inbox and Repsol Commitment portal.
In 2025, remediation management has continued, dealing with 82 new claims (all of which have been resolved).
In addition, Repsol makes itself available to national and international human rights protection bodies and institutions, whether judicial or extra-judicial, to work with them to seek out solutions.

Repsol not only commits not to hinder their normal operations, but also to establish a collaborative relationship that benefits the soundness of the process and, therefore, the reparation of the people affected.
Repsol also works closely with partners or contractors in the event of any impact, and supports them in ensuring effective remediation for all affected people.
In 2025, there were no severe human rights incidents.
The Company continues to carry out actions related to the remediation and management of opportunities related to the spill that took place at the La Pampilla facility in 2022.
For more information, see Processes for engaging with affected communities.

Before undertaking a new project or activity, a preliminary analysis is carried out: it addresses the social, environmental, and health situation, the legal requirements and the vulnerability of the environment and of stakeholders.

This is the starting process for identifying and evaluating the risks and impacts and designing the necessary mitigation measures, involving the relevant areas, in accordance with the Company’s internal regulations in force since 2011.

The social impact assessment takes into account, among other aspects, the right to land and its natural resources, the right to a healthy environment and the right to protection of the identity and culture of communities, as well as the special rights of indigenous communities.
Since 2014, a proprietary methodology for assessing human rights impacts has been available.

Before starting each activity, viable alternative designs that minimize land acquisition and restrictions on land and subsoil use are considered, to avoid resettlements and adverse impacts on communities and land users.
If the above is not possible, an assessment of potential impacts is carried out and consideration given to whether continuing with the project is appropriate.

Seven new impact assessments addressing social and human rights aspects were carried out in 2025 relating to the assets of the Renewables business in Alto Bierzo Sil, Benavieja, Bufarán, Oencia, Páramo del Sil and Ampliación, Eólica Ponte and Cerrato, all of them in Spain.
As in 2024, in 2025, no human rights violations of communities nearby our operations were reported.

In addition, Repsol undertakes social investment initiatives in order to share value with society in general and, in particular, through projects that benefit the communities near the operations, and especially vulnerable groups that may be identified.
These initiatives, including Impulsared, have contributed to achieving the goal of contributing to the 2030 Agenda and benefiting 10 million people from 2021 to 2025.

As a result of the collaborative study carried out alongside national and international communities and entities, the lines of action were identified of Impulsared, a social and economic reactivation program in the five districts of the area affected by the spill.

3.3.3 Metrics and targets Targets related to managing material negative impacts, advancing positive impacts, and managing material risks and opportunities (S3-5) Last year, the target was set to implement an action plan to comply with the Sustainability Due Diligence Directive.

In 2025, all scheduled activities were fulfilled and the decision was reached to redefine this objective by setting a more ambitious target that aims to expand the scope of due diligence processes to a greater number of activities, including those considered low risk.
Based on this, a due diligence review plan has been established that has the following milestones: • Digitization of the social reporting process.
• Evaluation and improvement of the effectiveness of grievance mechanisms.
• Implementation of the due diligence process in the value chain.
• Analysis of tools and methodologies to evaluate the effectiveness of engagement and the perception of communities in relation to our social management processes.
• Implementation of the due diligence process in low- risk businesses.

In addition, upholding the Repsol’s commitment to generate a positive impact on society through social investment, an objective has been defined aimed at promoting social investment associated with the Company’s commitment to a fair transition.

For Repsol, the multi-energy strategy is a key element in promoting the energy transition and decarbonization in a way that integrates efficiency, profitability, and sustainability.
This approach recognizes the transitional role that conventional energy sources, such as oil and gas, continue to play in the progress towards a more diversified energy system.
In 2025, the multi-energy strategy take the form of a brand upgrade, with a new visual and verbal identity.
New protocols were established across physical, telephone, and digital channels.

The aim is to measurably improve the user experience, not through aspirational messaging, but through verifiable actions that prioritize functionality and transparency, such as the creation of new spaces for customers at service stations or the new design of the Waylet front page with a new information structure that allows users easier access to all services.

Material impacts, risks, and opportunities4 and their interaction with strategy and business model (ESRS 2 SBM-3) The double materiality assessment carried out includes the assessment of impacts, risks, and opportunities based on their interaction with Repsol customers and consumers.
This analysis identifies both the positive and negative impacts of the Company.

Specifically, in relation to customers and end-consumers, after an analysis of all the risks, opportunities, and impacts, the following positive impacts have been deemed material: • Greater ease for customers to self-manage their incidents through platforms such as chatbots, online forms, etc.
• • Improvement of the customer purchasing experience through the transparency and availability of extensive information in digital customer contact environments (IRO-45).
Increased safety throughout the product life cycle owing to the identification of potential hazards and risks in the design phase (IRO-46).
These impacts affect all types of customers equally, and Repsol maintains similar communication channels with all of them.

The case of vulnerable electricity customers is noteworthy, for whom a specific protocol has been established for listening to and managing their incidents, as explained in Processes for engaging with consumers and end users (S4-2).

3.4.2 Managing impacts, risks and opportunities Policies related to consumers and end users (S4-1)5 The Sustainability Policy is a key Repsol policy that applies to all the Company’s customers, consumers, and end users.
This policy includes the following commitments: • Knowing, analyzing and managing the expectations of the Company’s different stakeholders, including customers and society in general.
Repsol has different communication channels, available and accessible, to inform, involve, and engage in ongoing dialogue with its stakeholders.
• Responding transparently to stakeholders through economic, environmental and social performance indicators that are published in corporate reports following international reporting standards.

• Promoting and requiring sustainability commitments in accordance/similar to those defined internally throughout its value chain: customers, partners, suppliers of products and services and other stakeholders.

In line with these commitments, and within the framework of Repsol’s relationship with consumers and customers, the Company has internal regulations in the field of product safety, specifically the standard for safe product management, the application of which is the responsibility of the Director General of Energy Transition, Technology, Institutional Affairs, and Deputy CEO.

This standard defines the requirements and responsibilities for the proper management of risks during the design and supply of products, and during the manufacture, handling, marketing and final use of products that may affect the health and safety of people, facilities and the environment.
The standard applies to all chemical products (hereinafter, "products"), whether substances or mixtures, designed, sourced, manufactured, handled, used, and marketed by Repsol.

Therefore, areas within the business and areas of research and development that are in charge of the design, supply, manufacture, marketing or use of the products are responsible for complying with the standard and with specific regulations (including legislation) applicable to product safety.
The main objectives of this standard are to guide risk management to avoid any harm to people and the environment.

In particular, the aim is to prevent major accidents through proper process safety management, and to involve employees, contractors and other stakeholders in continuous improvement and in the definition of health and safety management programs and systems.

Repsol is therefore committed to respecting the human rights of its customers, and rejecting any type of discrimination against customers and users of products or services and respecting their right to privacy at all times, while protecting and making proper use of their entrusted personal data.
In 2025, there were no severe human rights impacts related to consumers or end users, as indicated in Governance.
Processes for engaging with consumers and end users (S4-2) Repsol seeks a standardized and comprehensive service model with customers and consumers.
This transversal management is led by the Chief Customer Officer8, who is the senior operational role for managing its proper implementation.
Customer service is the responsibility of all the people in the Organization and the general management offers coverage to the different lines of business.
Specifically, it is the Customer Care function that carries out comprehensive management for the different businesses.

Customer service channels deployed Customer service is structured through various interaction channels where quality, listening skills and understanding of user needs are prioritized, while seeking personalization and customer satisfaction in every case.
The main contact channel in 2025 was the telephone, accounting for 58% of the service (2024: 57%), compared with 42% (2024: 43%) for all other channels (email, apps, web forms, chatbot, etc.).
This channel provides consumers with different free customer service telephone numbers which they can use to contact for inquiries related to the Company’s products and services.

In 2025, in Spain and Portugal, a total of 4,272,964 calls were served (2024: 4,418,326), with a service level (number of interactions attended to total interactions received) of 89% and with an average waiting time (average time that the customer waits before being served) of 116 seconds.

In addition, for those customers who require autonomy, Repsol provides digital channels, including automated telephone services to manage transactions at the Electricity and Gas business or requesting orders in LPG.
Meanwhile, further progress was made toward certain initiatives that had got underway in 2024.
For instance, customers of the Waylet app can use a Waylet chatbot, while LPG customers can request orders via WhatsApp.
Moreover, Electricity and Gas customers could make payments on electricity and gas bills via WhatsApp starting in 2025.
In addition to these digital functionalities, there are other mobile apps that Repsol makes available to its customers, such as Waylet, Vivit, Repsol Contigo, Repsol You and Pide tu Bombona.
The service in all these channels is multilingual, with the official regional languages of Spain, as well as English and French, to cover the spectrum of current customer needs.
This includes the provision of a special sign language interpretation channel for people with hearing difficulties, in line with Repsol’s principles of inclusion and social responsibility.
Customer service processes Regardless of the input channel, any interaction is recorded in the corresponding customer management platform.
This registration is done manually or automatically and includes a classification based on the reason for the contact by the customer.
Interactions are resolved at the front line of care and those requiring some processing or specific action are escalated to a specific management team.
Furthermore, customers of businesses such as Electricity and Gas, in order to avoid possible fraud, have to pass a security protocol or have an authorization if they are not contract holders.
The interactions that are recorded on the different customer service channels also include the rating and a satisfaction score with the key objective of monitoring and managing the interactions.
Voice of the customer platform These communication channels are complemented by a customer voice platform that is used across the different businesses.
The purpose ascertain in real time customer perceptions and assessments of the care they receive in order to manage and improve it.

Throughout 2025, a total of 2,574,700 responses were received through all the channels operating in the Iberian Peninsula.
From a weighted average of customer ratings at the main touchpoints, a global CSAT (Customer Satisfaction Score) is created that is part of the objectives of Repsol’s General Customer Department.
In addition, based on all this information or customer voice, the different teams implement experience improvement actions that allow the CSAT index to be improved by between 3% and 5%.

Services to vulnerable customers Within the Customer Service function, and specifically in the Electricity and Gas business, customer management covers vulnerable customers, severely vulnerable customers, and customers at risk of social exclusion.
The management of vulnerable customers is regulated, so these customers must meet a series of legal requirements to prove their economic vulnerability.
REGSITI, the regulated retailer of the Repsol Group that offers its customers the PVPC tariff, manages requests for information or requests for subscription to these types through customer service.
Consumers have channels such as telephone, face-to-face, email or post, among others, to make these queries.
In 2025, a total of 9,430 requests were handled (10,471 in 2024), of which 8,011 were resolved (8,666 in 2024).
Of these, 3,836 related to vulnerable customers (4,196 in 2024) and 4,175 to severely vulnerable customers (4,470 in 2024).
Product safety management Repsol has a 24‑hour emergency phone line for managing any potential customer incidents related to product safety.
This phone line, as well as the product labeling, comply with the requirements established in the REACH9 and CLP10 regulations.

The treatment of these incidents, as well as their management, control and response, is regulated by an internal procedure, by which all incidents are analyzed in order to proceed with the study and implementation of the measures deemed appropriate depending on the degree of severity of the incident.
If the incidents have a high, medium or low degree of severity, the affected business is notified so that it can proceed with their management.
In the case of incidents with a zero degree of severity, no immediate action is taken.

Likewise, to support the transmission of the information on the safety data sheets to the clients, in addition to the traditional sending by email or paper, an algorithm is being tested so that every time a new sale is made, the safety data sheet is sent to the email address provided by the customer.
Through the labeling of packaged products, transmission of information throughout the supply chain is ensured.
The information consists of safety instructions such as pictograms and safety information for the use or handling of the substances and a 24-hour emergency hotline.
This information reaches end users as long as the packaging is not tampered with and remains unaltered.
Processes to remediate negative impacts and channels for consumers and end users to raise concerns (S4-3) Repsol has multiple channels and procedures in place for managing complaints.
Each of the areas that have a relationship with customers has procedures that allow the collection and management of claims and complaints to process their resolution and redress.
These complaints, and other interactions, are managed and coordinated by an expert and internal Customer Service team.

Direct management with customers and users is articulated, with internal coordination and management, through teams external to Repsol, which are the ones who handle interactions with consumers and end customers.
Complaints management Complaints are received through the entry agents authorized by Repsol: customer service, technical support and development (ATD) technicians, commercial departments, etc.
In addition, customers can use the different available channels such as telephone, email, web, social networks, mail, in person, etc., to file a complaint.

The complaints handling process is as follows: • Logging of the complaint in the customer management systems, with detailed categorization of each case, by process, applicant, origin and levels of classification.
• Resolution of the complaint and/or claim at the first line of support or assignment to those responsible for managing, resolving, and following up on the most complex complaints.
• Gathering of information, analysis of the root cause, proposal and implementation of containment, corrective and improvement actions.

• Depending on the implementation of new processes or the results of root cause analysis, quantitative monitoring is carried out in terms of the volume of incoming different types of claims and the reason for them.
To this end, measurement systems through data panels or our own audits on these calls or contacts are decisive in understanding their evolution and degree of adjustment.
During 2025, complaints registered in Spain and Portugal totaled 217,470, of which 97% were closed.
Compared with 2024 (213,307 complaints), there was a 2% increase in claims received and registered this year.
Of these complaints, in Electricity and Gas in Spain, 25% were resolved in favor of the customer.

In Peru, a total of 2,500 cases were registered in 2025 (3,107 in 2024), by telephone (9%), email (79%) and in person (13%), through which customers make their queries, requests, suggestions and/or complaints/claims.

Managing the voice of the customer On the voice of the customer platform, both in the Service Station business in Spain and Portugal and in the Electricity and Gas business, where the largest number of points of sale and customer contact exist, there is a process for responding to customers who convey dissatisfaction and who express the desire to be contacted to be listened to and have their concerns resolved.
This process of managing dissatisfaction is gradually being extended to the points of contact of the different businesses in the Iberian Peninsula.

In 2026, it will be extended to Electric Mobility and LPG and work is underway to establish an internal protocol for its regulation with a comprehensive vision of customer responses, which will enter into force in 2026.
Complaints related to service stations in Mexico are monitored in real time thanks to an IT system that records all calls and emails that reach Customer Service.
This allows Repsol to consult cases in real time and monitor them.
This system is also capable of generating reports for the evaluation of the service.
In the case of Peru, monitoring case handling is carried out by the loyalty team in order to identify cases handled outside the service level and, in this way, identify areas for improvement.
These can address different aspects: improvements in the operation of the system, training of personnel, management of new resources, among others.

In this context, as a means of accessing the information that customers need, we are actively working to improve the experience of searching for information and managing or purchasing products or services through the transparency and breadth of information available on platforms such as Waylet, the corporate website or the Customer Area in accordance with the Company’s regulatory framework.
In 2025, Repsol undertook to enhance its brand.

One of the main levers was the simplification of communication through the use of clearer and more relevant messages that consider the customer’s perspective, an upgrading of customer service protocols and guides, and a clearer and simpler transition to digital channels.

In the quest to improve the experience offered to Repsol customers across digital channels, a continuous and comprehensive approach is implemented that combines both quantitative analytics techniques and qualitative research.

In addition, the Company has a specialized UX (User Experience) team dedicated entirely to improving the digital experience: • Quantitative analytics: Web analytics tools are used to collect data on user behavior.
This includes metrics such as conversion rate, time spent on site, and user journey.
By analyzing this data, patterns and trends are identified that allow us to understand how the Company’s customers interact with products and services.
This information is an aid to informed decision-making about improvements in the interface, content and functionalities.
• Qualitative research: The quantitative findings are complemented by qualitative research, including surveys, interviews, and focus groups.
These techniques allow for delving deeper into customers’ motivations, needs and expectations.
Through direct feedback, areas for improvement and opportunities to personalize the experience are identified.
By integrating both approaches, customized strategies are designed that respond to users’ individual preferences.
To measure the experience with the platforms, there are two main sources of information: 1.
For apps, with customer ratings and opinions in reviews on app marketplaces.

For some processes or specific points of contact through apps or websites, such as the purchase of services with Waylet, the Vivit app, or electric charging, customers have the opportunity to provide feedback through a satisfaction survey.

Both sources provide Repsol with important information to implement actions focused on the energy needs of our customers, which allow us to improve their experience at all points of contact with the Company.

In order to anticipate and promote transparency, Repsol has a “customer community” that allows it to carry out continuous qualitative research with a group of users or potential customers and to inquire about how these users understand the different value propositions, learn about their relationship with energy and, in this way, adapt or design the Company’s communication and information dissemination.

In addition, to deepen the understanding of the physical-digital experience, identify levers for improvement, detect market trends and optimize the multichannel strategy, the Multi-energy tracking system was implemented in 2025 and will continue to be used moving forward.

It is a continuous monitoring and analysis system that can track the behavior, perception and experience of consumers with respect to different sources of energy they use in their home or while mobile.

Through this tracking system, 1,000 managers responsible for home energy contracting and regular drivers are surveyed per month and KPIs are evaluated such as the Net Promoter Score (NPS), satisfaction by attributes, reasons for selection and brand awareness.

Customer management assessment With respect to efficient management of communication, active listening, and complaint channels, Repsol has established customer satisfaction control mechanisms so that the processes provide effective redress in their application and results.
Firstly, as noted, a record is made of interactions occurring in the different customer service channels, with a rating and a satisfaction score also obtained .
Based on an analysis of these assessments, improvement actions are proposed.

Customer Service and its internal teams carry out a daily operational monitoring of the management of external suppliers and their service provision with the aim of ensuring optimal levels of care and service for customers and consumers.

In the case of customer service in Mexico, meetings are held between the external service and the internal Repsol team, where analysis is focused on specific cases of complaints from service stations that could not be resolved by the supplier, with a view to improving processes.

Likewise, on a monthly basis, the operators of this service are evaluated through service indicators to check the quality of the service and the correct management of complaints related to service stations.
In Peru, in 2025 and with a main focus on the level of service, actions were implemented aimed at expediting the handling of registered cases.

In this regard, it should be noted that Repsol in Peru is now ranked first in the CX Index in the service station category, thus achieving recognition as the company that offers the best customer experience in the country.

A I II Table of conversions and abbreviations Alternative performance measures III Risks IV V VI ≡ Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information Annual Corporate Governance Report Annual Report on Director Remuneration Product safety assessment Managing the safety of the products sold is a priority for Repsol and is present through all stages of the life cycle of products.

In addition to the applicable legislation in force, the Company has internal11 regulations that set out the requirements to support adequate risk management, from the beginning of research to design a product until it is marketed.

During the design phase and following the REACH and CLP regulations, hazards are identified and potential risks arising from use are assessed, in order to take appropriate management measures, carrying out (eco)toxicological studies to identify possible adverse effects on people and the environment.
To remediate the potential impacts of the product use and ensure product safety, continuous monitoring is necessary to identify new or changing risks.
In this sense, product re-evaluations are carried out spontaneously or reactively in the face of a legislative change and/or at the request of ECHA12 on products already made available to customers.

Repsol participates in voluntary programs of the sector itself and European organizations that delve into specific knowledge about certain products, such as the propylene oxide and glycols sector group of CEFIC13 or the consortium focused on improving scientific knowledge of short-chain NLP polyols14 led by the main manufacturers of this type of product, as well as in the Concawe consortiums for petroleum products and LOA15 for products derived from catalytic cracking.

In compliance with internal regulations, Repsol uses safety data sheets and hazard labels to provide information on the hazards of each product it sells so that customers can appropriate measures to handle them safely.
The Company has a single repository that allows all employees and stakeholders to access all product safety documents.
In addition, it allows analysis of different parameters of the products handled for the entire company, by company or by product range.
These documents are available to Repsol customers in accordance with the laws of the country where the product is to be handled.
In the database, commercial and product data converge, which allows Repsol to automatically send safety documents to customers.
To respond to the possible negative impacts of the products sold by Repsol, there is a 24-hour emergency telephone number to manage safety incidents.

3.4.3 Metrics and targets Targets related to managing material negative impacts, advancing positive impacts, and managing material risks and opportunities (S4-5) Customer service-related metrics and targets Customer and consumer satisfaction is key for Repsol.
For this reason, the Company relies on various surveys to measure the degree of satisfaction in the interaction of users with customer service.
This indicator is regularly monitored, as it is a source of information for the improvement of customer service services and processes.

To obtain this indicator, an annual survey is carried out at different times and points of contact where the customer is asked for a rating from 1 to 5 (with 5 being the highest level of satisfaction).
In 2025, customer satisfaction (CSAT) on the service received was 4.216 out of 5 (4.3 in 2024) from a total of 20,969 customer surveys (20,878 in 2024) carried out.
For on-call surveys, the total number of surveys conducted was 1,102,039 (701,401 in 2024).

In 2025, based on the information collected at the different points of contact with the customer, an experience improvement program has been implemented, with a focus on improving the process of contracting, onboarding and billing for electricity and gas, brand experience in communication, interaction and service at physical points of sale, telephone and face-to-face, and in the optimization and simplification of digital communities.

In the case of customer service in Mexico, the level of service provided by the external provider is measured, with a requirement of 97%, as well as the service level, with a requirement of 90% of calls answered in under 10 seconds.
As for the average handling time (AHT), it varies depending on the service being managed: 170 seconds for calls, 229 seconds for email, and 200 seconds for social media.
For the evaluation of the service in Peru, this year’s indicators were as follows: 1.
Cases handled within the service level: 85%, in line with the annual target.
Percentages of incoming calls answered: 98% with < = 30 seconds.
Average call time of 2.87 minutes vs. target of < = 3 minutes.
For the following year, weekly meetings with the external service are being implemented, with the aim of improving productivity ratios and guaranteeing a better service.
