3.5 Safety and security Repsol carries out all its activities preserving the integrity of people and avoiding any damage to the environment, supporting a healthy and safe work environment from both a physical and emotional point of view, generating measures to address material IROs from Safety and Security.
In its double materiality assessment, Repsol has identified material IROs regarding safety and security.
In relation to risks, the following have been identified as material: • Improper management of safety can lead to accidents at facilities (IRO-25).
• Possibility of attacks, blockages in operational activity and accidents that could affect the Company’s facilities, resulting in operational interruptions and financial losses (IRO-27).
The following are material negative impacts: • Impacts on local communities due to industrial incidents that may occur at Repsol facilities (IRO-26).
• Accidents in higher-risk operations that could cause serious harm to workers (IRO-28).

3.5.1 Governance As indicated in Governance, the Company has a corporate governance framework that establishes, develops, promotes and evaluates its focus on to transparency, integrity, respect, and excellence.
Repsol’s Board of Directors and Executive Committee play a key role in promoting and overseeing the Company’s sustainability strategy, through the Sustainability Committee.
The Sustainability Committee performs, in accordance with the provisions of the Regulations of the Board of Directors, supervisory functions related to personal and process safety.
The Committee also regularly monitors strategic environmental and safety projects.

In 2025, the Sustainability Committee reviewed, among others, the analysis and periodic monitoring of safety performance: scorecard of indicators, contractor safety management and change of safety metrics and objectives to improve the learning cycle.
The incentive in terms of safety at the CEO level is related to safety rates, fatalities and incidents.
The Third-Party Due Diligence policy establishes the necessary measures for understanding the third parties with which the Company interacts or will interact, including safety and security risks.
In safety and security, due diligence is addressed with the stakeholders: suppliers, contractors, and partners, as detailed in Business model and strategy.

Risk management and internal controls Safety and security risks are assessed in conjunction with the businesses, and the management of these risks is incorporated into the Internal Control System over Sustainability Reporting (ICSR), the objective of which is to support the reasonable reliability of the sustainability information.
3.5.2 Strategy Repsol is present throughout the energy value chain.
Performance of the activities in the four business segments entails risks inherent to the integrity of workers.
The Company conducts an annual review of its stakeholders, and creates a prioritization map, taking into account the nature of the stakeholders.

It also seeks to improve safety indicators in operations through constant and coordinated communication with contractors, one of the main stakeholders, addressing challenges and best practices, and promoting continuous improvement in work processes aligned with the principles of the Safety Excellence Program, as described in Business model and strategy.

3.5.3 Policies related to safety Repsol has a Safety and Occupational Health Policy, which establishes that the Executive Committee leads the health and safety programs, with the chain of command being responsible for implementing them in their area of responsibility.

The Safety Excellence Program Management and the business units are responsible for developing the specific procedures necessary to comply with Repsol’s regulations and the legal requirements in force in each location, so that the necessary protection measures are adopted in each of its operations.

A I II Table of conversions and abbreviations Alternative performance measures III Risks IV V VI ≡ Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information Annual Corporate Governance Report Annual Report on Director Remuneration The Company will promote and require the principles set forth in this policy to its employees and, to the extent possible, will extend the policy to its contractors, partners, and collaborators.
The scope of application of the policy and the different regulations inherent to occupational safety reaches all activities and operations carried out by Repsol in which it has management control.

In order to comply with the commitments established in the Safety and Occupational Health Policy, Repsol develops the necessary regulations, which are adapted and updated periodically in accordance with best practices and analysis of their effectiveness.

In 2025, among others, the following regulations have been revised: • Safety and Environmental Risk Management: The mission of this standard is to ensure that hazards that may affect the safety of people, the environment, assets, and the Company’s stakeholders are identified, and risks are analyzed, evaluated, and minimized to tolerable levels.

In addition to integrating risk management into operational processes, the standard includes the requirements to be met in terms of analysis methodologies at each stage of the life cycle, the acceptability matrix and the actions, deadlines and authorizations for each of the risk levels.

• Life-Saving Rules: Repsol has decided to adhere to the IOGP Life-Saving Rules, evolving from its previous 10 Basic Safety Rules to an international, robust and recognizable standard for our contractors and partners.
The official rollout, training, and awareness campaign for all employees and contractors began in January 2026.

3.5.4 Actions and resources related to safety The Company has made available the financial resources required for carrying out actions that are considered minor or immaterial and are therefore not detailed.

This approach, the founding objective of the Safety Excellence Program, is based on recommendations from insurers and best practices established by industry associations, such as IOGP, which are already being adopted by different companies in the sector.

The evolution is justified by the exhaustion of some of the more traditional processes and messages linked to safety, which, although they have been useful to reach current levels of performance, have lost part of their effectiveness or are generating unintended negative consequences.
Under no circumstances should it be interpreted as renouncing the reduction of minor accidents, but rather so that higher quality and high-impact actions are taken to avoid major accidents.
The aim of managing all events in the same way, regardless of their potential, has proven not only inefficient in terms of the use of resources, but even counterproductive in terms of results.

In order for this change to represent an effective transformation and not just a declaration of intent, changes have been made to the key observation processes and metrics by which safety performance is evaluated, and the messages that are conveyed to the Organization.
a) Progressive abandonment of "zero accidents" messages.

Although it makes sense as an ambition or within the framework of a project, a shutdown or any other time-limited activity in the medium term, it can lead to a disincentive to report, a reduction in only the most trivial accidents and a feeling of stagnation in improvement.
b)Elimination of internal targets associated with the total recordable injury rate, and its replacement with an indicator linked to fatalities, permanent injuries, and serious accidents.
Similarly, the performance information requested from our contractors is reviewed on an ongoing basis and references to the frequency rate are eliminated.

A I II Table of conversions and abbreviations Alternative performance measures III Risks IV V VI ≡ Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information Annual Corporate Governance Report Annual Report on Director Remuneration c) Changes to the safety risk acceptability matrix (included in the Safety and Environmental Risk Management Standard) to accommodate scenarios of major consequences and very low probability of occurrence, such as loss of well control or air accidents.

d)Development of a specific program to better identify events that, without having caused significant damage, could have done so if an additional protective barrier had failed or the conditions had been slightly different, thus aiming to improve the assessment of potential incidents.

e) Investigation of incidents with potential major consequences, with the investigation requirements being limited to aspects related to the investigation methodology, composition and qualification of the investigation team and management of the resulting actions.
The rest of the incidents are analyzed and managed by the corresponding operational units, but without the above requirements.

f) Promote greater identification of systemic causes and human and organizational factors in investigations, working both on the technical competence of the teams responsible for the analysis and on the Organization’s capacity to manage the information that emerges from these investigations.

g)Organization learning, with the lessons learned from the incidents are transferred to other units and people where they can be applied, while new information is kept current and up to date in the learning portal.
This action was initiated in 2024 and is ongoing.
h)Deployment of the Life-Saving Rules, which replace the current 10 Basic Safety Rules.
The associated campaign will reinforce prevention measures in activities that are most likely to cause fatalities and permanent injuries.
In short, the strategy focused on reducing major accidents requires important changes in processes and the conviction that it has a positive impact on all safety management.

It is possible that initially there will be an upturn in the personal accident rate indicator, including the lost-time accident indicator, but Repsol acknowledges this for the sake of transparency in reporting, the psychological certainty that is generated in the Organization and confidence in safety processes.
There is no specific time horizon associated with this action, given its structural nature within the safety strategy.

2.- Safety culture diagnoses Repsol continues to carry out specific safety culture diagnoses in all its assets and businesses, so that each of them has a diagnosis in cycles of approximately four or five years.

The corporate area of the Safety Excellence Program conducts these assessments on a regular, independent basis and following a model of proven robustness covering the most decisive aspects of a solid safety culture.

Fair recognition Trust in the report Shared information LEADERSHIP Adaptability Feeling of vulnerability Learning Organization These assessments allow us to identify weaknesses and areas for improvement that are not apparent in other verification and audit exercises, as they are related more to perceptions and interpersonal realities than to formal and documented issues.

One of the main lessons learned from major industrial accidents is the need to pay attention to the weak signals that occur in organizations, so these exercises are a crucial lever to prevent major accidents.

This initiative has been gradually carried out in Repsol’s various operations, and in 2025 it was carried out in the Venezuela business unit, in the Mobility Department of Spain and Portugal, and in the Cartagena and Pampilla industrial complexes in Spain and Peru, respectively.
The results obtained allow business managers to develop action plans that are more in line with their cultural reality.
There is no specific time horizon associated with this action, given its structural nature within the safety strategy.

The actions reported in 2024 remain in force (except for those whose time horizon was 2024) and are aligned with the two general initiatives that are reported in 2025 and their result is translated into the safety metrics, which are shown in detail in Safety-related metrics and targets.

Safety keys 3.5.5 Security-related policies Repsol’s Protection of Tangible and Intangible Assets Policy brings together the Company’s commitments to comply with the legal requirements in force regarding asset protection in each location and country in which it operates, seeking to guarantee the integrity of people, prevent the commission of crimes, support the continuity of operations and prevent the loss of Company information, therefore minimizing the risk of attacks (IRO-27).

The Company provides the resources for the protection and safeguarding of Repsol’s assets, including persons, interests, assets, operations, knowledge, industrial and intellectual property rights, trade secrets, know-how, information and data, networks and information systems, as well as the Company’s operations and interests, that underpin Repsol’s activity.
This protection is carried out through management based on risk analysis and a process of continuous improvement, promoting a culture of asset protection, under the protection of corporate values.
The policy determines internal and external information channels that provide insight into the situation of asset protection and cybersecurity in the countries in which the Company is present.

The policy also promotes awareness of asset protection through training and dissemination activities among employees so that the latter will, within their own areas of responsibility, safeguard their own safety and that of Repsol’s other assets.

Repsol has established standards of conduct for the entire Company, disseminating asset protection criteria and a common framework for action, through the development of the internal security management standard.

The security regulations include the different security and risk mitigation measures implemented, which are part of the various security plans and cover all employees, contractors, and anyone present within Repsol’s facilities, allowing for protection of the Company’s assets.

The application of security regulations is the responsibility of the Intelligence and Security Department and the units in each country, which are responsible for developing the specific procedures necessary for compliance with local regulations and for Repsol, adopting the equivalent protection measures in the territories.

Risks in facilities/assets that do not belong to the Company, but in which it has an interest and in which Repsol personnel provide services, must be analyzed in a manner equivalent to the Company’s own facilities/ assets of a similar category.

Here,, Corporate Security is responsible for conducting the risk analysis or supervising the analysis carried out by the operator and determining the recommended mitigation measures to be taken to achieve the appropriate level of protection.
Through the intranet, Repsol makes available to employees the policy and regulations on the protection of tangible and intangible assets, as well as the security risk classification of countries.
The scope of application of the Policy and the metrics associated with security extend to all activities and operations carried out by the Company in which it has management control.

It also covers all employees, regardless of the type of contract that determines their employment relationship, the position they occupy, and the place of work, as well as all those people who provide professional services in our facilities on a temporary basis, the secondees and the interns.

A I II Table of conversions and abbreviations Alternative performance measures III Risks IV V VI ≡ Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information Annual Corporate Governance Report Annual Report on Director Remuneration M METHODOLOGY Methodology for safety and security reporting and metrics The incident management process managed with the Synergi Life digital tool allows for the recording, reporting, classification, and tracking of incidents, including investigation, actions, and lessons learned.
The automation of the tool is subject to an internal control for the correct functioning of the notification flows, as well as the different phases of the life cycle of the management of an incident.
Based on the information recorded in the tool, the accident rate indicators are obtained.
These are reported in accordance with the internal incident management standard based on international standards (IOGP and OSHA).

The reported accident rate metrics are obtained from the different fields that have been completed in the tool and are defined as: • Number of fatalities: number of deaths accumulated in the year, occurring within the Company’s control and due to work- related causes.
• Number of recordable work-related injuries: total number of personal consequences (fatalities, permanent injuries, loss of days, medical treatment, and restricted work) accumulated in the year.

• Total Recordable Injury Rate (TRIR): total number of personal consequences (fatalities, permanent injuries, loss of days, medical treatment, and restricted work) accumulated in the year, for every million hours worked.
• Number of days lost: number of days not worked due to an accident at work with sick leave, accumulated in the year.

• Number of Tier 1 and Tier 2 incidents: number of process safety incidents recorded and classified according to severity as Tier 1 or Tier 2 according to API754 (American Petroleum Institute) in its third edition updated in August 2021.
A process safety accident is one in which there is a loss of primary containment.
The following criteria must be met simultaneously: i) There is a process or a chemical product involved.

ii) It occurs at a specific location, that is, at a facility used for production, distribution, storage, auxiliary services (utilities) or pilot plants related to the process or chemical product involved.

(iii) It results in an unplanned or uncontrolled release of material, including non-toxic and non-flammable materials (for example, steam, hot water, nitrogen, compressed CO2, compressed air), with certain levels of consequences.
The process safety accident will be classified as Tier 1 or Tier 2, according to the defined thresholds.

Governance information 4.1 Business conduct 4.1.1 Governance The role of administrative, management, and supervisory bodies (ESRS 2 GOV-1) Repsol believes that ethical behavior and responsible practices are essential elements for the sustainable performance of its activity and for the generation of long-term value for its shareholders and stakeholders.

Therefore, as indicated in Governance, the Company has a corporate governance framework where its commitment to transparency, integrity, respect and excellence in all its actions is established, developed, promoted and evaluated.

In this regard, Repsol’s Board of Directors and Executive Committee play a key role in promoting and overseeing the Company’s sustainability strategy, as well as in fostering a corporate culture aligned with the values and ethical principles that inspire its Code of Ethics and Business Conduct.

4.1.2 Impact, risk and opportunity management Description of the process for determining and assessing material impacts, risks, and opportunities (ESRS 2 IRO-1) The complete description of the process that has been followed, as well as the result of said analysis, is reported in Materiality assessment.

In relation to business conduct, positive impacts have been identified that refer to: • Promoting good practices in relation to corruption, bribery, and money laundering along the value chain through the introduction of specific contractual clauses (IRO-47).
• Repsol’s leadership and good corporate governance, which encourages the rest of the sector to embrace ESG standards, promoting more responsible practices throughout the industry (IRO-50).
• Fostering an ethical culture within Repsol’s workforce by promoting greater alignment with the Company’s values, greater trust and collaboration, and a stronger reputation for integrity (IRO-51).
• Promoting a safe environment for employees and third parties, providing a channel to report incidents or complaints without fear of retaliation (IRO-52).

Likewise, a negative impact has been identified in relation to: • Poor management and control of integrity in Repsol, which can lead to corruption, affecting investor and shareholder confidence, which entails financial and reputational risks for the Company (IRO-48).

Finally, a risk has been identified relating to: • Regulatory non-compliance: reserve reporting, financial markets regulations, accounting standards, tax and sustainability reporting regulations, artificial intelligence, HSE legislation, intellectual property and other specific regulations of the markets in which we operate (IRO-49).
The assessment of these impacts encompasses all the activities and geographies where Repsol is present, including its value chain.

Business conduct and corporate culture policies (G1-1) Code of Ethics and Business Conduct The Code of Ethics and Business Conduct1 applies to all directors, executive personnel, partners2 and other employees, regardless of the type of contract determined by their professional or employment relationship.

It establishes the framework of reference for understanding and implementing the conduct and standards that the Company expects from each of them, thus helping to promote a culture of ethics and corporate compliance.

Ethics and Compliance Committee The Ethics and Compliance Committee is a collegiate, high-level, multidisciplinary body, with autonomous powers of initiative and control appropriate for the performance of its duties.

It comprises the General Counsel and General Secretary of the Board of Directors of Repsol, S. A., the Chief People and Organization Officer, the Chief Compliance Officer, the Head of Audit, Control and Risks, and the Head of Labor Relations, Legal-Labor Management and Safety at Work.
The committee is tasked with managing the oversight system and supporting compliance with the Company’s Code of Ethics and Business Conduct, among other duties.

Ethics and Compliance Channel The Ethics and Compliance Committee makes available to employees and any third party the Ethics and Compliance Channel, which is a robust, easily accessible, confidential and, if desired and permitted by applicable legislation, anonymous channel through which suspicions of unlawful, unethical behavior, or behavior involving a possible breach of the Code of Ethics and Business Conduct, internal regulations or applicable legislation, can be reported.
Communications received through the Ethics and Compliance Channel are handled by an independent company.
This channel is available 24 hours a day, seven days a week, by post, through the website and by telephone, serving in the local language from which the communication is made.

1 The Code of Ethics and Conduct is available on Repsol’s corporate website, in Spanish, English, and Portuguese, by following this path: Home > Sustainability > Sustainability Strategy > Policies > Code of Ethics and Business Conduct.

2 Partners (including non-operated joint ventures, contractors, suppliers, and other collaborating companies) are an extension of Repsol and, for this reason, must act in accordance with the code and any other applicable contractual provisions when intervening on behalf of or in collaboration with the Company.
Repsol also encourages business partners to develop and implement ethical programs that are consistent with its standards.

The Board of Directors of Repsol, S. A. has approved a single internal reporting system for the Repsol Group, and appointed the Ethics and Compliance Committee as its head, including the general principles applicable to the internal information system in its own Code of Ethics and Business Conduct.

The system, which includes an instruction manual, is designed to guarantee confidentiality throughout the process, and the people who carry out the investigation will be obliged to respect the confidentiality of all the personal data and other information to which they are privy.
Repsol has the obligation to preserve the identity of the person who submits the communication and the person about whom the report is made.
Investigators, interviewees or participants in the process sign confidentiality agreements whenever they take part in an investigation.

The communications standard of the Ethics and Compliance Channel regulates the management and processing of any communication received through this channel in relation to circumstances or events that could constitute breaches of the Code of Ethics and Business Conduct, the Crime Prevention Model or the possible commission of a crime or infringement of internal regulations and applicable legislation, as well as Repsol’s Internal Reporting System.
Verbal communications may be made by telephone or by requesting a face-to-face meeting with the Chief Compliance Officer (CCO).

• Analysis and processing: By delegation of the Ethics and Compliance Committee, the CCO or the person designated by the CCO carries out an initial analysis of the communication to evaluate its nature and content.
As a result, the communication may be admitted for investigation, or classified as inadmissible when the facts lack any plausibility or are unintelligible, among other reasons.
In those cases, the CCO informs the sender about the agreed course and may request additional information.
• Conducting the investigation: The CCO appoints one or more investigators who act under its coordination and maintain confidentiality throughout the process.
Investigators check the veracity of the information and can propose preventive measures.
Legal regulations and legal guarantees are observed, paying special attention to the rights of defense and protection of personal data.

• Resolution of the investigation: Once the investigation is complete, the investigator draws up a report of conclusions describing the investigation carried out and the conclusions reached and, where appropriate, makes recommendations for improvement and proposes an action plan.
The Ethics and Compliance Committee decides on the report and the closure of the investigation.
The CCO monitors the implementation of the action plans and keeps the Ethics and Compliance Committee informed of progress and completion.
It also informs the whistleblower and, as the case may be, any other concerned parties of the closure of the investigation.

Anti-retaliation measures and controls Repsol has controls in place to prevent and manage any retaliation against whistleblowers who report violations through the Ethics and Compliance Channel, including: 1.
Fostering a retaliation-free work environment through training and communication initiatives.
Establishing protection measures for the whistleblower and those affected, such as confidentiality clauses in investigations, preventing reprisals and promoting anonymity.
Implementing corrective action if retaliation is confirmed, and applying disciplinary sanctions to those responsible.

In addition, Repsol has a Whistleblower Protection Protocol as part of its Internal Reporting System, which aims to provide additional protection by prohibiting retaliation against those who make a communication in good faith through the Ethics and Compliance Channel.

Supplier Relationship Management (G1-2) Repsol, when managing relations with its suppliers, carries out activities to avoid or minimize the impact of disruptions in the value chain, while also interacting with vulnerable suppliers, as detailed in S2-4.
Repsol conducts screenings and evaluations of the social and environmental performance of suppliers and evaluates the inclusion of local suppliers in the purchasing process, as described in S2-5.
For more information, see Workers in the value chain.

Prevention and detection of corruption and bribery (G1-3) The Company has a global framework of action and a dedicated and specialized team in place, aimed at supporting the adequacy and observance of its obligations, both internal and external.

The Compliance function helps to strengthen the global culture of compliance and improve the identification and monitoring of ethics and compliance risks, particularly in the areas of integrity, money laundering and terrorist financing, crime prevention, international sanctions and embargoes, competition and privacy and protection of personal data.

The operation of the Compliance function is formalized in a Compliance Statute and a comprehensive Compliance Model approved by the Audit and Control Committee, as the delegated body of the Board of Directors of Repsol, S. A.: • The Compliance Statute sets out the mission, objectives and principles of the Compliance function and the obligation of the Chief Compliance Officer to support and report to the management and governing bodies so that they can exercise their responsibility to promote due control and good corporate governance.

• The Integrated Compliance Model (ICM) defines the structure and general operation with an integrative vision of the Company’s Compliance Model and establishes the approval levels for compliance risks.
In 2025, a total of 22,126 employees completed the new annual mandatory online training for all employees called the Repsol Code: Stories that teach.

In a digital, modern and interactive format, the Chief Compliance Officer and a leading journalist in Spain reinforce the key ethical concepts among all employees through fictitious cases that force them to make decisions in different situations and contexts.

For the first time in 2025, an innovative online training was developed targeting third parties in our value chain (suppliers, contractors and customers), in which the Company’s values and principles are reinforced through a journey that links Repsol’s businesses with its Codes of Ethics and Business Conduct (global and supplier).

Meanwhile, the dissemination plan for courses related to Repsol’s Ethics and Compliance Channel, the internal reporting system, and the management of investigations for stakeholders continued throughout the period.
Compliance Days at Repsol In October, 2025 Compliance Day was held at our headquarters.
On this occasion, the central focus of the event was the impact of the current geopolitical context on the strategic role of the Compliance function.

Repsol’s General Counsel, Pablo Blanco, interviewed José María de Areilza, a renowned expert in geopolitics, university professor, and Secretary General of the Aspen Institute Spain Foundation, who offered us his current view of the complex environment in which we currently operate and its impact on the Compliance function.

Subsequently, the 5th Compliance Awards were presented by members of the Company’s top management, in recognition of the best internal practices in ethics and compliance in the following categories inspired by Repsol’s new values: achievement, curiosity, courage and care.

Special recognition was also given to the most outstanding member of People Compliance, a volunteer network of more than 360 employees who act as compliance ambassadors and promote the culture of compliance across different businesses and countries.

In addition, in line with previous years, other similar events were also held at the regional or local level in various countries, including Compliance Day in Latin America, with the involvement of the regional front line, the Chief Compliance Officer and external experts; Compliance Day in Mexico, focused on corruption and anti-money laundering; and three meetings with the People Compliance volunteer network on topics such as international sanctions and their impact on operations, how our values have changed over time, and conflicts of interest – real cases.
3 Training is available to any employee regardless of their contract type.
There are also management system guides for the different work areas which describe the general standards and requirements, controls and associated risks.

These in turn serve as a basis for carrying out specific risk assessments, and the implementation of measures that help prevent or mitigate compliance risks, as well as a guide to the compliance risk assessment methodology, which defines the necessary guidelines for the proper identification, analysis and assessment uniformly in all countries, business units, and corporate areas.

Due diligence Repsol has a third-party due diligence standard, which sets out the necessary measures that the Company has in place to promote adequate knowledge of the third parties with which it relates or will interact in relation to compliance risks.
All this with the purpose of allowing Repsol to have adequate information in the decision- making process and in the evaluation and mitigation of possible compliance risks related to the third party.

In addition, an in-house technological solution, known as RDD2, has been developed to manage due diligence with third parties, incorporating advanced data-driven and generative AI solutions, which allows Repsol to benchmark and match public and regulatory information, establish risk levels and propose mitigation measures.

Integrity The Company reiterates its focus on strict compliance with the regulations for the prevention and fight against corruption and fraud, in all its forms, and has developed an Integrity Policy, which sets out its express position against unlawful conduct of corruption, fraud and conflicts of interest in accordance with the Company’s ethical values.

In addition, it has implemented other specific regulations, including those relating to gifts and hospitality and conflicts of interest, and the guide to the keys to managing our relationship with public officials, which have undergone a planned review and update.

In addition, the N-1 Calculator has been designed, which identifies the Compliance Model to be included in the shareholders’ agreement with an anticipatory view and which must be required of investee companies where Repsol does not have control of the management, depending on the level of theoretical risk of the transaction, the degree of participation or the influence we have in the company, and the specific features or circumstances of the transaction.
Repsol annually updates its compliance risk map, which includes a theoretical and qualitative assessment of the inherent risks at the country, business, and area level.

This analysis allows Repsol to identify employees in the countries, businesses and areas most exposed to risk, thus providing an essential tool for their management and mitigation.

In particular, risk-exposed employees have been identified at the Upstream businesses in Algeria, Bolivia, Brazil, Libya, Peru, Mexico, and Venezuela; at the Trading business in the United States; at the Lubricants, Asphalts and Specialized Products businesses in Colombia and Peru; and at the Mobility, Aviation and Refining businesses in Peru.
In 2025, the coverage of training in the prevention of corruption and bribery for these risk-exposed employees stood at 94%.
Crime prevention The Ethics and Compliance Committee is Repsol’s crime prevention body in Spain, for the purposes of the provisions of Article 31 bis, section 2.2 of the Spanish Criminal Code.

Repsol has a standard that regulates the framework of action and responsibilities of the various areas involved in the Crime Prevention Model and the Crime Prevention Manual, which serve as guides to help employees understand criminal risks and the actions and conduct expected of them.
In addition, in terms of criminal prevention, Repsol has formal models in Peru, Chile, and Colombia.
Training, communication and awareness on matters of compliance Ethical culture, communication, and awareness are fundamental levers to strengthen the Integrated Compliance Model at Repsol.

To this end, the Organization makes great efforts in communication and training of internal regulations related to ethics and compliance, thus improving levels of knowledge and understanding among all employees.

It designs and deploys a global plan (countries and businesses) that annually takes into account: (i) the level of risk to which each group is exposed; (ii) the results of employee surveys (culture survey) and other sources in the People and Organization area; (iii) the requests of the areas, (iv) regulatory changes and legal updates, (v) compliance and audit recommendations, and (vi) the evaluation of the earnings of the actions carried out in previous years.

Specifically, Repsol has a training program for the Board of Directors of Repsol, S. A. on the Crime Prevention Model that is carried out every two years, and that is carried out annually for the Executive Committee on various compliance matters.
In 2025, face-to-face training has been given to both the Board of Directors and the Executive Committee focused on crime prevention.

The biannual training aimed at executive personnel and administrators of Spanish companies has continued, which has consisted of a face-to-face training program focused on Repsol’s Crime Prevention Model (CPM), with the aim of reaffirming executive management’s focus on the prevention of irregularities and deepening the understanding and importance of the model.
The interactive methodology has included practical exercises with simulated cases to identify compliance risks and apply CPM procedures, highlighting key legal responsibilities.

Company rules and regulations Internal regulations are a management tool, the main objective of which is to strengthen control systems and promote compliance with external and internal requirements; provide criteria for action that make it easier for employees to understand their responsibilities; to assist in the management and control of risks and to contribute to more agile and orderly execution of the Company’s activities.

The General Directorate of People and Organization is responsible for keeping the Company’s rules and regulations up to date, through a process that allows for the involvement of the affected areas in drawing up the different regulatory documents, their coherence and consistency with the hierarchy and structure of the regulatory body, and their approval at the appropriate level.
It also allows the internal dissemination of the regulations and their availability to all employees.

A I II Table of conversions and abbreviations Alternative performance measures III Risks IV V VI ≡ Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information Annual Corporate Governance Report Annual Report on Director Remuneration Nature of communications received 4.2 Responsible taxation The Repsol Group is aware that the taxes it contributes boost the economic development of the countries and communities in which it operates and contribute to the well-being of its citizens.

As a result of this conviction, Repsol undertakes to comply with best practices in responsible taxation and good governance, in line with the most widely recognized and prestigious international principles and recommendations (including the Spanish Code of Good Tax Practices, GRI 207, the OECD tax risk control model, and the B Team’s principles of responsible taxation, among others).

In 2025, the double materiality assessment carried out at the Group level once again revealed that taxation is a material ESG topic for the Company, with a positive impact that is particularly evident in: • Repsol’s contribution to the development of the countries in which it operates by complying with its tax obligations, applying criteria of transparency, and avoiding abusive practices or practices that are not aligned with industry standards (IRO-54).
4.2.1 Governance As indicated in Governance, Repsol’s Board of Directors is the governing body tasked with approving and supervising the Company’s Tax Policy, which includes the tax strategy.
This policy is mandatory for all employees and companies of the Group, and its execution and compliance is reviewed by the Board of Directors at least once a year.
The Board of Directors examines the most significant tax aspects in terms of management, risks and practical application of the Group’s Tax Policy and strategy.

Among other issues, it verifies the content of the Voluntary Tax Transparency Report, the taxes paid by Repsol, the impact of tax risks, the mechanisms for their management and control, the Group’s presence in tax havens and the initiatives carried out to promote tax transparency.

No member of the Company’s administrative, management and supervisory bodies, nor any employee in the scope of Repsol’s tax function, has their remuneration or receipt of incentives linked to specific tax savings objectives.

Risk management and internal controls The Tax area is present in the Group’s business decision- making, so that all decisions are in line with the principles enshrined in its Tax Policy and with the reality and economic motivation of its businesses.

To this end, internal rules and procedures are in place so that Repsol adopts its tax positions based on robust business fundamentals, does not use opaque or artificial corporate structures that could compromise the transparency of its activities, and applies the arm’s length principle in its intra-group operations.
Please see the Index of essential aspects of the due diligence process for further details.

The orderly management of Repsol’s tax affairs is carried out within a scope of activity, governance and Tax Control Framework (TCF) that rests on four basic pillars: (i) principles of action, (ii) expert team, (iii) processes and systems for tax compliance, and (iv) control and management of tax risks.
Repsol periodically evaluates and updates its TCF, benchmarking it with other internationally recognized standards.
Repsol’s tax department is made up of experts in a range of tax-related disciplines; these professionals are responsible for managing all the tax affairs of the businesses and areas of the Group.

Under a single dependency of the Tax Affairs Department (TAD), the management of tax matters is decentralized in the fiscal units of each country or business, in order to adequately address the particularities of each business and tax system.
The continuity of strategy implementation and tax management in the face of unforeseen events is underpinned by a contingency plan for suitable succession in key tax-management positions.
Repsol incorporates tax risk management into its Enterprise Risk Management System (ERMS) through processes, systems, and internal controls.
The TAD, as the body responsible for tax compliance, carries out tax risk control activities, among other initiatives, through the preparation and updating of a risk map.

It also reports to the Audit and Control Committee, as a support body to the Board of Directors, on the impact of tax risks on the Company’s activities and on the mechanisms for their prevention, management, and control within the ERMS framework.
Its powers include submitting transactions involving a particularly high tax risk to the Board for approval.

To mitigate tax risks, Repsol has documented processes that regulate tax compliance, assigning responsibilities and defining the tasks necessary to prepare and file tax returns and payments, supporting the reliability and traceability of the information.
In addition, it has information management systems that preserve integrity and minimize human error, and digitization initiatives are being carried out to better manage the risks of tax operations.

In terms of transparency, Repsol has an Ethics and Compliance Channel so that any employee or third party can report, confidentially and anonymously, possible breaches of the Code of Ethics and Business Conduct or the Crime Prevention Model, including potentially unethical or illegal conduct that affects the integrity of the Company in relation to taxation.
This channel is permanently managed by an independent third party.
See Business conduct for more information.

A I II Table of conversions and abbreviations Alternative performance measures III Risks IV V VI ≡ Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information Annual Corporate Governance Report Annual Report on Director Remuneration 4.2.2 Strategy Repsol’s Tax Policy is based on the responsible and efficient payment of its taxes, the application of good tax practices, the transparency of its actions and the promotion of cooperative relations with tax authorities.

In accordance with these principles, Repsol gives priority attention to the payment of taxes due in the different countries in which it operates, with the aim to comply with the law, respecting both its letter and its spirit.
Tax decisions are made responsibly, in accordance with a reasonable interpretation of prevailing tax law and regulations and are aligned with the economic activity of the various businesses.

Repsol reconciles strict compliance with the regulatory and competence framework in force in each jurisdiction with the defense of its legitimate interests, making use at all times of the remedies and actions provided for by law when it considers that certain actions may give rise to doubts as to their legal compliance.
Repsol is focused on being transparent and sharing significant information with its shareholders and stakeholders regarding the Business model and strategy.

To this end, it collaborates with tax authorities in the detection and search for solutions to fraudulent tax practices, facilitates access to relevant tax information, and prioritizes non-litigious ways of resolving disputes.

Repsol also promotes institutional relations in order to align the Company’s tax policies with social reality, contribute responsibly to the creation of a more balanced and fair international tax framework, and anticipate the management of tax matters in the face of possible regulatory changes, minimizing risks and impacts.
Taxes paid in 2025 It also maintains an open dialogue with various NGOs and social action platforms in the search for a more equitable and effective tax system.
Tax contribution Repsol’s tax contribution in 2025 was once again considerable: €13,265 million paid in taxes and similar public charges.
By country, the Company’s tax contribution in Spain of €9,899 million was particularly notable, representing 75% of the Group’s total tax payments.

This significant tax contribution, generated by our activities and paid by the Group companies, has been borne both by the Company (own taxes) and by its customers, employees and investors (taxes collected1).
The Company’s own taxes paid in 2025 amounted to €1,983 million (€746 million in Spain).
Corporate income tax (CIT) paid amounted to €361 million (-€513 million in Spain).
Lastly, the Group also paid a significant amount of environmental taxes in 2025, amounting to €7,308 million (€6,088 million in Spain).

1 To allow a homogeneous view of the taxation of fuels and combustibles, the taxation on Hydrocarbons is included here, when the Company is ultimately responsible for payment (even when it is paid through logistics operators), regardless of whether the Company itself bears it and not the consumers.
2 The Tax Contribution Report is available on Repsol’s corporate website, following this path: Home > Sustainability > Responsible taxation > Tax contribution.

Repsol not only discloses its Tax Policy, but also provides information to its stakeholders through regular reports available on the corporate website, which notably include: 1.- Tax Contribution Report, in which, among other issues, the taxes and public charges paid by item and country are detailed.

2.- Country-by-Country Report for corporate income tax (CIT), which is submitted to the tax authorities and which Repsol voluntarily makes public, accompanied by a set of additional explanations relating to the CIT paid in each jurisdiction in which it operates.

3.- Report on presence in tax havens, detailing and explaining the activities carried out and the taxes borne in non-cooperative jurisdictions and in other territories classified as controversial for tax purposes by civil organizations outside the official lists.

4.- Cooperative relations report, which sets out Repsol’s position on tax matters deemed significant (for example, in the face of the challenges of the energy transition), as well as our participation in different international forums and organizations.

5.- Report on payments made to public administrations in relation to Upstream activities, complying with Spanish and European regulations that require large companies in the extractive industries sector to prepare this report as a result of their extraction operations.

4.2.4 Action plans In 2025, Repsol submitted its Voluntary Group Tax Transparency Report to the State Tax Administration Agency (AEAT), a practice it has maintained since 2015 and plans to continue in the future.
It includes relevant information related to the Group’s economic activity, as well as actions and decisions on tax matters adopted by the Group during the year.

In addition, in order to deepen this relationship, in January 2025 and, for the second year running, Repsol voluntarily provided documentation on the related party transactions of the Repsol Group companies, in accordance with the proposals approved within the framework of the Code of Good Tax Practices.
Likewise, the Company maintains its track record of active participation in cooperative relationship programs and initiatives.

Following its participation in the first International Compliance Assurance Program (ICAP) and in the European Trust and Cooperation Approach (ETACA), this line of work was further strengthened in 2025.
Accordingly, Repsol participated in Brazil’s CONFIA pilot program and in the Large Companies Forum in Portugal.

Repsol was also invited to join the collaborative compliance program promoted by the Peruvian tax authorities, taking part in the first thematic working group dedicated to the drafting of a Good Governance Code.
Along the same lines, the Company has offered to participate in the Latin American tax risk assessment program promoted by the IDB (ICAP-like).

These actions, which will continue on an annual basis, are a further show Repsol’s firm focus on tax transparency and the improvement of the tax system within the framework of the promotion of cooperative relations.
Their purpose is to increase the confidence of its stakeholders by providing greater clarity and traceability in its tax conduct.

4.2.5 Metrics and targets Through its Tax Policy, in line with the Company'’ mission and values and with the tax objectives included in its Global Sustainability Plan, Repsol intends to be publicly recognized as a company with integrity and responsibility in tax matters.
Imbued with this spirit, Repsol has included measurable and traceable tax objectives in its Global Sustainability Plan, which is monitored annually.
Below is a summary of the progress made in 2025 toward the initiatives being pursued within the Group to achieve the aforementioned objectives: 1.
Zero presence in OECD/EU non-cooperative jurisdictions and streamlining of the corporate structure.
In 2025, more than 15 redundant entities were removed from Repsol’s corporate perimeter, located in 11 jurisdictions.
Repsol’s presence in Trinidad and Tobago (a non- cooperative jurisdiction according to the Spanish list) was reduced, as a Group subsidiary (dormant) located in that country was liquidated.
Maintaining leadership in fiscal governance best practices.

Once again, the GRI 207 standard was validated by the Group’s external auditors (in their exhaustive option) and Repsol earned public recognition for its good tax practices and transparency from various representative stakeholders.

Examples here include the award of the Three-Star “T Transparent” seal, the highest distinction granted by Fundación Haz; the renewal of the Fair Tax Mark, the gold standard awarded by the Fair Tax Foundation for responsible tax conduct; and the receipt of the award for the most tax-transparent company in the EU, granted by the Dutch association of responsible investors (VBDO).
Fostering cooperative relations with tax administrations.

Repsol submitted the Voluntary Tax Transparency Report to the Spanish tax authorities for the tenth year running, while also disclosing information on to its related-party transactions on a strictly voluntary basis, in accordance with the recommendations of the Code of Good Tax Practices in Spain.
For more information, see Action plans and Taxation (GRI 207).

This makes it more exposed to cyber risks, which may lead to operational disruptions, theft of intellectual property or sensitive information, fraud, and similar incidents.
As a result of the double materiality assessment carried out, a risk has been identified relating to: • The financial impact arising from events that affect the Company’s operations (IRO-53).

Cybersecurity risks are identified by assessing the impact that a compromise in the dimensions of availability, confidentiality, and integrity—both of information and of the technological assets that manage it—would have on the Company’s operations, its reputation, and the people affected.
4.3.1 Governance As indicated in Governance, Repsol’s governing bodies, in exercising their responsibilities in terms of sustainability, play an active role in the supervision of cybersecurity.

The Executive Committee is responsible for approving the Cybersecurity Charter and Policies, participating in cyber incident response preparedness exercises, and receiving direct reports from the Chief Information Security Officer (CISO).
The Executive Committee receives regular updates and information on developments in the field of cybersecurity, such as: • Code of Good Governance of Cybersecurity.
• Cybersecurity Charter and Policies.
• Threat situation and significant events related to cybersecurity at the Company.
• Cybersecurity risk assessment.
• Risk mitigation plans (cybersecurity strategy).
• Resources needed to implement and execute the plans.
Accordingly, matters of interest are submitted to the Executive Committee on an annual basis.
In addition, it requests information whenever it considers it necessary.
The Group also has the Audit and Control Committee attached to the Board of Directors, which also oversees cybersecurity matters.

This Committee receives direct information at least once a year from the CISO, who is responsible for defining and supervising the security of the networks and information systems within the Repsol Group, while also acting as a point of contact and technical coordinator with the competent authorities and the competent Computer Security Incident Response Team (CSIRT).
Aside from establishing the CISO’s roles, the Charter establishes the Cybersecurity Committee as an internal and advisory collegiate body to support the CISO in these tasks.

Risk management and internal controls The Company has a system of Internal Control over Sustainability Reporting (ICSR) to reasonably ensure the reliability of the reported sustainability information.
The details of the ICSR can be found in Risk management and internal controls over sustainability reporting.

Cybersecurity risks are evaluated together with businesses and mitigation objectives are established at the global level, using the Cybersecurity Operating Model aimed at enhancing the resilience of the systems and the operations they support, as well as periodic strategic plans.
This leads to state-of-the-art processes and technologies for protection, detection, response and recovery against cyberattacks, which are kept up to date through the Strategic Cybersecurity Plan.
In particular, during the last year, the deployment of the 24–26 Plan has continued, addressing more than 50 initiatives.

Cybersecurity risk management also takes account of the supply chain, since, depending on the purpose of the award, it is required to complete cybersecurity questionnaires for suppliers, requiring, depending on the risk, certifications and additional legal clauses in contracts.
Likewise, human risk is addressed via an annual mandatory cybersecurity course for employees and ongoing additional awareness activities, such as phishing simulations .
The Company also has audit procedures, both internal and external, for examining and supervising the control framework for cybersecurity risks.
In this context, the Company did not suffer any cybersecurity incidents with a significant impact in 2025.

4.3.2 Strategy As determined by the Cybersecurity Charter, the Company has appointed a CISO who leads a cybersecurity governance model with the following characteristics: • The CISO has the hierarchical level and independence necessary to perform the role and make decisions.
• The model is implemented through a policy, regulations and procedures for the protection of information and management of cybersecurity to support compliance.

• • It is based on the risks arising from the business, which are determined and monitored periodically, together with their controls and mitigation measures, in the corresponding areas and with top management.
Internal cybersecurity analyses and third-party audits are carried out on a regular basis.
International standards are taken as reference points and independent ratings and benchmarks are followed continuously to measure performance.
In addition, the CISO regularly takes part in national and international forums on the subject.
• Specific training and awareness campaigns are given to all workers.
• Business continuity and incident response plans are continuously trained and improved.

A I II Table of conversions and abbreviations Alternative performance measures III Risks IV V VI ≡ Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information Annual Corporate Governance Report Annual Report on Director Remuneration • There is a security operations center and a cyber intelligence service that continuously detects, analyzes, reports and corrects information on alerts and potential threats, identifies cyberattack patterns and manages security incidents.

In cybersecurity, we monitor that there is security with respect to information and infrastructure protection, so as to instill trust in all the Company’s stakeholders referred to in Business model and strategy.

4.3.3 Policies In response to the European NIS2 Directive and following the Code of Good Governance for Cybersecurity and Cybersecurity Risk Control, we have adopted the following measures: • Repsol has a Protection of Tangible and Intangible Assets Policy, in which it expresses its commitment to cybersecurity.

This commitment is formalized through a Cybersecurity Charter that provides structure and continuity to the role of the Chief Information Security Officer (CISO), establishing the criteria for appointment, the role’s functions and responsibilities, and its position within the Organization.
The Charter also establishes a Cybersecurity Committee as an advisory body to support the CISO in coordinating the management of cybersecurity risks.
• An asset protection and cybersecurity standard that establishes responsibilities for the protection of information and the devices, networks and systems that manage or store it.

4.3.4 Action plans In 2025, the rollout of the Cybersecurity Strategic Plan continued, with a focus on maintaining the high level of cyber resilience and information protection achieved under the previous plan, thereby offsetting the ongoing increase and growing sophistication of threats.
The plan also sets the objective of enhancing the capabilities of the Organization and its people, while at the same time making interaction with protection measures easier.
The plan consists of four workstreams: 1.
Strengthen the cybersecurity of critical infrastructures and maintain a high level of cyber resilience in order to avoid operational incidents.
Fortify the protection of digital information, identity control and the cloud for the protection of personal information.
Improve the internal cybersecurity capabilities, both of our Organization and our staff, through the optimization of processes, training and awareness.
Comply with new regulations and standards such as NIS2 and DORA.
With the Cybersecurity Strategic Plan, the aim is to improve cybersecurity maturity in all defined aspects.
The maturity review is carried out biannually in global terms and quarterly with the monitoring of the initiatives established in the Strategic Plan.
4.3.5 Metrics and targets It is important to note that the detailed information of these metrics and targets is sensitive information for the Company, since such information can have an impact on it.
Monitoring the effectiveness of the actions is carried out through oversight of the four lines of work of the Strategic Plan.

The plan is based on more than 60 capabilities, which are assessed in terms of their maturity, effectiveness, and coverage across the Organization, and are linked to the risks they help mitigate, with validation by an external firm.

To this end, each line of work is monitored on a quarterly operational basis to check its progress in line with the plan, and every 18 months the status of the capabilities is reassessed to confirm that the plan is having the expected effect on them.
The aim is the full rollout of all lines of work over the plan’s three-year duration.
